args: A 40-year-old man is evaluated in the pre-operative holding area this morning for a planned right shoulder arthroscopy with possible labral repair, a procedure the orthopedic team anticipates will last less than ninety minutes under general anesthesia. Three weeks ago he was involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision in which a burst fracture at the T11 vertebral level produced complete motor and sensory loss below the waist; neurosurgery stabilized the injury with a thoracolumbar brace but elected to defer open fixation because alignment remained acceptable and the patient was hemodynamically stable. Since that admission he has remained on the inpatient rehabilitation service, spending nearly all of his time supine or in a specialized wheelchair, and over the last four days the nursing flow sheet documents no more than assisted pivot transfers from bed to chair and back, with otherwise continuous bed rest, so he has effectively been immobilized for well over 72 hours. Because of poor venous access in his upper extremities after multiple blood draws and intravenous medications, a right internal jugular triple-lumen catheter was placed under ultrasound guidance twelve days ago; the insertion site is clean, dry, and secured with an occlusive dressing, and there have been no fever spikes or culture growths to suggest infection. The patient has not required intubation or vasopressors, has had no episodes of systemic inflammatory response, bloodstream infection, or lung consolidation, and his temperature curve has remained within normal limits. He denies orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, exertional chest pain, or prior episodes of decompensated heart failure. Review of respiratory symptoms is similarly benign, with clear breath sounds on daily examinations and no supplemental oxygen need. He has not undergone any other operative intervention or sedation since the original trauma evaluation, and there is no history of fractures to the pelvis, hip, or long bones, nor has he worn a fiberglass cast. The lower limbs, which he cannot voluntarily move, are warm and well perfused but have become puffy to mid-calf over the last week; circumferential measurements taken yesterday show a two-centimeter increase compared with baseline, and pitting edema is present to the anterior shins, though there is no discoloration, superficial venous engorgement, or calf tenderness on squeeze test, and he reports never having been told he has varicose veins. Duplex imaging has not yet been performed, and he denies prior clots, chest pain, hemoptysis, or unexplained shortness of breath at any time in the past. He recalls no relatives with blood disorders or thrombotic events, and a thrombophilia panel drawn years ago for unrelated research purposes was reportedly unremarkable; that evaluation specifically included genotyping for the Factor V Leiden mutation, which was negative. Past medical history is otherwise limited to well-controlled mild hypertension managed with lisinopril 10 mg daily; he has never had myocardial infarction, chronic lung disease, bowel inflammation, or malignancy. He does not smoke, drinks beer only on football weekends, and uses no recreational drugs. Medications include lisinopril, gabapentin for neuropathic pain, and a daily bowel regimen; no hormonal therapy, erythropoietin, or glucocorticoids are in use. Physical examination shows an alert man lying supine on the transport stretcher, speaking comfortably. Vital signs: blood pressure 122/76 mm Hg, heart rate 72 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, temperature 36.8 °C, oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. Jugular venous pressure is not elevated, the precordial impulse is normal, and S1 and S2 are crisp without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Breath sounds are vesicular throughout with no wheezes or crackles. The abdomen is soft and non-distended with active bowel sounds and no organomegaly. Both lower extremities are flaccid with 0/5 strength, absent deep tendon reflexes, and intact passive range of motion; skin is intact without pressure ulcers. Moderate bilateral ankle and pretibial edema is appreciated, and pedal pulses are palpable with Doppler. Upper extremity neurologic and motor function is full. The central line dressing is intact with no erythema or induration. Laboratory studies drawn yesterday reveal hemoglobin 14.0 g/dL, platelet count 255 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 6.8 × 10⁹/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 0.96 mg/dL, AST 24 U/L, ALT 19 U/L, fasting glucose 102 mg/dL, prothrombin time 12.2 s, INR 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 29 s. Chest radiograph taken four days ago for routine line placement confirmation shows clear lung fields and proper catheter tip position in the superior vena cava. A 12-lead electrocardiogram demonstrates normal sinus rhythm without ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities. Airway assessment reveals adequate mouth opening, full neck extension, Mallampati class II, and no dental issues that would complicate intubation. He has been nil per os since midnight, the operative consent has been verified, and sequential compression sleeves have been ordered but are not yet applied. No pharmacologic prophylaxis has been administered prior to incision as the anesthesia and surgical teams await completion of formal venous thromboembolism risk stratification.

CRITICAL HINT: For Caprini, Age 41-60=1 pt, 61-74=2 pts, >=75=3 pts. Elective lower extremity arthroplasty = 5 pts. Hip, pelvis or leg fracture = 5 pts. Stroke/paralysis = 5 pts. Multiple trauma = 5 pts. Acute spinal cord injury = 5 pts. Major surgery (>45 min) = 2 pts. Central venous access = 2 pts. Bed rest > 72 hours = 2 pts. Minor surgery = 1 pt. History of major surgery (<1 month) = 1 pt. COPD = 1 pt. BMI > 25 = 1 pt.

output.reasoning: Age 40: Not 41-60 (0 pts). Surgery: Planned right shoulder arthroscopy (not lower extremity, duration >45m = 2 pts). Paralysis/Spinal cord injury: T11 burst fracture, complete motor/sensory loss (5 pts). Multiple trauma/fracture: T11 fracture (5 pts, likely counts as 'Multiple trauma' or 'Hip, pelvis or leg fracture' categories are not met, but T11 fracture is spinal trauma). Bed rest > 72 hours: Immobility > 72h (2 pts). Central venous access: Internal jugular catheter present (2 pts). Hypertension: Present, but not a Caprini point. BMI > 25: Not stated/calculable from text (assume absent). History of major surgery (<1 month): Only trauma stabilization (not major surgery), no other prior surgery (0 pts).

```python
def extract_calculator_values(
    patient_note: str,
    calculator_name: str,
    field_descriptions: str,
    reasoning_context: str,
) -> dict:
    """Extract specific clinical values from a patient note for a medical calculator.

    You are given:
    - A patient note with clinical information
    - The name of the calculator to extract values for
    - A description of each field to extract (with units and expected ranges)
    - Optional reasoning context from a prior analysis stage

    Instructions:
    - Use the EXACT parameter names from the field descriptions as JSON keys
    - CRITICAL: Always extract the patient's baseline or admission values (prior to any treatment or surgery) unless the question specifically asks for post-treatment values. Pay close attention to the timeline in the note.
    - CRITICAL: If a field name ends in '_score' or '_points' (e.g., 'bun_score', 'age_score'), you MUST calculate and provide the integer point value based on the scoring system, NOT the raw clinical measurement (e.g., if bun_score is requested, give the points for BUN, not the raw BUN in mg/dL).
    - For all other fields, extract the RAW numeric value (e.g., if 'platelets' is requested, provide 115, not the point value).
    - Convert units ONLY for weight (lbs→kg: ×0.453592) and height (feet/inches→cm).
    - CRITICAL: DO NOT convert units for lab values. Extract the raw numeric value exactly as it appears in the text (e.g., if the text says 26 g/L, extract 26. If it says 120 mmol/L, extract 120).
    - Extract dates exactly as they appear in the text (e.g., "09/05/2001").
    - For boolean conditions: True if present, False if absent
    - If a condition is not mentioned, assume it is absent (False or 0)
    - For scoring systems with mutually exclusive severity levels (e.g., mild vs moderate/severe liver disease), mark ONLY the single most appropriate severity level as True.
    - Include ALL fields, even if the value is False/0/absent
    - If a required numeric value or field is entirely unmentioned and cannot be inferred, OMIT it from the 'extracted' dictionary completely instead of writing 'missing' or null.
    - CRITICAL: Do NOT guess or assume standard physiological values. If it is missing, omit it.

    Return a dict with:
    - "extracted": {"param_name": value, ...} for each field
    - "missing": ["list of values truly not determinable from the note"]

    Examples:
    >>> extract_calculator_values("A 70yo male, 80kg, 175cm, Cr 1.2", "Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault)", "age: years, sex: male/female, weight_kg: kg, creatinine_mg_dl: mg/dL", "")
    {'extracted': {'age': 70, 'sex': 'male', 'weight_kg': 80, 'creatinine_mg_dl': 1.2}, 'missing': []}
    """
    ...

```

args: An 83-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department late in the afternoon by a neighbor who noticed that the patient, normally able to walk her small terrier three blocks without assistance, became markedly winded after only half a block and had spent the past night propped upright in a recliner because lying flat worsened her cough and shortness of breath; the neighbor also reported hearing intermittent groans when the patient tried to clear thick sputum that had become blood-tinged earlier in the day. The patient lives alone in a one-story bungalow, pays her own bills, and usually prepares her own meals, though she keeps pillboxes filled by her daughter for a chronic dilated ventricular condition that was discovered eight years earlier following an episode of pedal edema and exertional dyspnea—her last cardiology visit six months ago documented an ejection fraction of 30 % and she has since remained on carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily, sacubitril-valsartan 24/26 mg twice daily, and a morning dose of furosemide 20 mg; she has never required device therapy or intravenous inotropes. She has no history of kidney stones, nephrotic syndrome, or dialysis, and her most recent outpatient chemistry panel three weeks ago showed a creatinine of 0.8 mg/dL and blood urea nitrogen in the mid-teens. She denies hepatitis, jaundice, heavy alcohol use, or prior abdominal imaging suggestive of chronic liver disease, and she has never undergone oncologic surgery, radiation, or chemotherapy. Influenza vaccination was obtained at a local pharmacy five weeks earlier; pneumococcal immunization was completed two years ago. She quit smoking thirty years ago after a 10-pack-year exposure, drinks half a glass of wine with dinner on Sundays, and has never used illicit drugs. The illness started four days prior with chills, diffuse myalgias, and a dry cough; by the second day the cough became productive of yellow sputum and she noted temperatures on her digital tympanic thermometer as high as 38.8 °C, managed only with a single 500 mg dose of acetaminophen. On the morning of presentation she experienced a shaking chill and felt “dizzy” when she tried to stand, prompting the neighbor’s call to emergency services. In the prehospital phase no oxygen or fluids were administered so that baseline measurements could be captured. Upon arrival in triage she was sitting forward, using accessory neck muscles to breathe but able to answer questions appropriately; initial vitals prior to any treatment revealed an oral temperature of 39.5 °C, regular tachycardia at 107 beats per minute, a narrow pulse pressure with cuff readings of 97/58 mm Hg confirmed manually, tachypnea at 38 breaths per minute with shallow excursions, and fingertip pulse oximetry of 84 % while breathing ambient air. Her skin was warm, flushed, and dry; jugular venous pulsation rose to the level of the clavicle when the head of the bed was at 45°, similar to prior heart-failure clinic notes, and trace bilateral ankle edema was present but unchanged from baseline according to the daughter’s description. Cardiac examination disclosed a rapid S1 and S2 with a low-frequency third sound best heard at the apex and no appreciable murmurs or pericardial rub. Pulmonary auscultation demonstrated coarse inspiratory crackles over the right lower third of the posterior thorax with near absence of breath sounds below the scapular tip and dullness to percussion along the same region, whereas the left fields transmitted vesicular sounds with only scattered late crackles at the base. Abdominal palpation was soft and nontender with normal bowel sounds and no hepatosplenomegaly. There was no calf tenderness or asymmetric swelling. Point-of-care capillary glucose obtained before intravenous access measured 145 mg/dL. An arterial blood sample while she continued on room air showed pH 7.51, PaCO₂ 28 mm Hg, PaO₂ 59 mm Hg, and calculated bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, consistent with an acute respiratory alkalosis driven by hyperventilation and significant hypoxemia. Venous chemistry drawn simultaneously revealed sodium 151 mEq/L, potassium 3.8 mEq/L, chloride 108 mEq/L, bicarbonate concordant with the blood gas, blood urea nitrogen 38 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, and serum glucose reconfirmed at 145 mg/dL. Complete blood count showed leukocytes 17.2 × 10⁹/L with 86 % segmented neutrophils, hematocrit 28 %, hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL, and platelets 190 × 10⁹/L. High-sensitivity troponin-T was below the assay threshold. A portable postero-anterior chest radiograph performed before any fluids, antibiotics, or diuretics depicted a dense right lower-lobe consolidation with air bronchograms and a layered meniscus measuring approximately 2 cm in depth along the right costophrenic angle, while cardiac silhouette and mediastinum matched prior outpatient films. Electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia at 106 beats per minute with left bundle branch block identical to baseline and no new ischemic changes. Two sets of blood cultures, sputum Gram stain, and urine antigens for Legionella and Streptococcus pneumoniae were obtained prior to therapy. Up to this point no antipyretics beyond the remote home dose, no vasoactive agents, no intravenous fluids, and no supplemental oxygen had been given, preserving her presenting hemodynamics and laboratory profile for risk stratification.

output.reasoning: Demographics: 83-year-old female (+83 points). Comorbidities: 1. Neoplastic disease: No history of cancer/oncology surgery mentioned (-0). 2. Liver disease: Denies history/imaging (-0). 3. Congestive heart failure: Known dilated ventricular condition, EF 30%, on carvedilol/sacubitril-valsartan/furosemide (+10). 4. Cerebrovascular disease: No mention of stroke/TIA (-0). 5. Renal disease: No history of renal failure/dialysis, creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL (-0). Physical exam: 1. Altered mental status: She answered questions appropriately, so absent (-0). 2. Respiratory rate >= 30: 38 breaths/min (+20). 3. Systolic BP < 90: 97 mm Hg, so absent (-0). 4. Temperature < 35 C or >= 40 C: 39.5 C (+15). 5. Pulse >= 125: 107 bpm, so absent (-0). Lab values: pH < 7.35: 7.51 (not present). BUN >= 30: 38 mg/dL (+20). Sodium < 130: 151 mEq/L (not present). Glucose >= 250: 145 mg/dL (not present). Hematocrit < 30%: 28% (+10). PaO2 < 60: 59 mm Hg (+10).

def analyze_scoring_conditions(patient_note: str, calculator_name: str, calculator_hints: str = "") -> dict:

args:
Approval and written informed consent for publication of this report were obtained from the patient.
A 76-year-old female (height, 155 cm; weight, 59.6 kg) had chronic heart failure with severe mitral regurgitation (MR), mild tricuspid regurgitation (TR), and chronical atrial fibrillation. Her comorbidities included hypertension and old cerebral infarction at the right medulla oblongata. A preoperative transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 62% and no left ventricular wall asynergy. Mitral valve repair, tricuspid annuloplasty, maze procedure, and left atrial appendage closure were scheduled. The surgical risk calculated by the Society of Thoracic Surgeons score (STS score) was 2.23% and that calculated by the European System for Cardiac Operative Risk Evaluation (EURO score) was 2.52%.
On arrival at the operating room, we inserted an arterial line into the right radial artery to monitor hemodynamics. And we also monitored bilateral regional cerebral oxygen saturation (rSO2) by using INVOSTM and the bispectal index (BIS) value to assess the patient’s depth of sedation. Anesthesia was induced using remimazolam at 6 mg/kg/h, remifentanil at 0.25 μg/kg/min, and rocuronium at 0.85 mg/kg. The patient did not complain of pain at the infusion site during administration of remimazolam. Remimazolam was adjusted by 1 mg/kg/h after loss of consciousness (LoC). The time from administration of remimazolam to LoC was 130 s. We inserted central venous and pulmonary artery catheters from the right internal jugular vein as well as a probe for monitoring transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) after intubation. During induction, the patient’s mean blood pressure (BP) decreased by more than 50%, from 137 to 54 mmHg, and 5 mg of ephedrine was administered three times to maintain mean BP over 65 mmHg. rSO2 was decreased within 10% from the baseline value regardless of hypotension during induction. The BIS value ranged from 31 to 42 in the period of induction (Fig. ). Remimazolam was adjusted by 0.6–1.0 mg/kg/h according to the BIS value until the end of the surgery, and the depth of sedation was stable when the BIS value ranged from 30 to 50, even in the period of undergoing CPB. We did not induce hypothermia, and body temperature was maintained at 35–36 °C during CPB.
Surgical procedures were completed successfully. While left ventricular function was preserved after CPB, oxygenation status deteriorated after CPB. PaO2/FiO2 ratio (P/F ratio) decreased to 104 mmHg from 250 mmHg before CPB. Although we repeated sputum suction and recruitment maneuvers, P/F ratio was not improved (136 mmHg) at discharge from the operating room. TEE showed trivial MR remaining after mitral valve repair and systolic function was preserved. At the end of the surgery, the intraoperative dose of fentanyl was 17 μg/kg. Intraoperative infusion volume was 1300 ml and we transfused 280 ml of red blood cells and 720 ml of fresh frozen plasma. Bleeding volume was 360 ml, and water balance was + 1570 ml in total. Total operation time was 365 min, total anesthesia time was 474 min, and CPB time was 252 min.
We transferred the patient from the operating room to the intensive care unit (ICU). Administration of remimazolam was stopped after arriving at the ICU. A chest X-ray and ultrasonography showed mild pleural effusion and atelectasis in the right lung field. We did not antagonize remimazolam with flumazenil because of the poor oxygenation. Dexmedetomidine was administered until her oxygenation improved sufficiently for extubation. The patient showed a nodding response about 90 min after arriving at the ICU. The tracheal tube was removed 220 min later when the P/F ratio was 248 mmHg. We confirmed that there was no intraoperative awareness or recall and that there were no serious complications associated with the use of remimazolam throughout the perioperative period. The patient was moved to the general ward on postoperative day (POD) 1. The patient had disorientation and delirium on POD 6, but magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed no new lesion of cerebral infarction. The patient showed a motor and sensory disorder in her left lower limb on POD 10 and she was diagnosed with right cerebral infarction. She was discharged on POD 41 and transferred to another hospital to continue rehabilitation.

weight_kg: Actual patient weight in kilograms (e.g., 100.0)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
height_cm: Patient height in centimeters (e.g., 170.0)

args:
A 58-year-old Caucasian female presented to her local emergency department with fever and diarrhea. The patient developed nonbloody, large-volume diarrhea after eating take-out 11 days ago, with four to five bowel movements per day. During this time, she also experienced fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, general malaise, and weakness. In addition, she observed leaking and drainage around her G-tube site with associated erythema and intense abdominal pain. The patient denied sick contacts or recent travel.
Her past medical history includes obesity, orthostatic hypotension, hyperlipidemia, chronic kidney disease, nephrolithiasis, gastrojejunal ulcer, Kawasaki’s disease, subdural hemorrhage, epilepsy, chronic pain syndrome, and anxiety. The patient’s past surgical history includes burr-hole craniotomy, lithotripsy, cholecystectomy, Roux-en-Y gastric bypass complicated by chronic refractory marginal ulceration and small bowel obstruction requiring two resections, ventral hernia repair, and gastrostomy tube insertion.
The patient was pregnant six times and had six miscarriages (G6P0060). She lives with her husband and adopted son with good social support. She is currently unemployed. The patient denies ever smoking and does not drink alcohol. Familial history consists of diabetes, heart disease, and nephrolithiasis in the patient’s mother. Her father had glaucoma, nephrolithiasis, and prostate cancer.
Medications before admission included pantoprazole 40 mg twice a day, sucralfate 1 g twice a day, ondansetron 4 mg twice a day as needed for nausea, midodrine 10 mg three times a day, fludrocortisone 0.1 mg twice a day, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, levetiracetam 1500 twice a day, methadone 10 mg every 8 hours, and lorazepam 0.5 mg daily as needed for anxiety. She was also on daily vitamin A, D3, and B-complex supplements and a monthly cyanocobalamin 1000 μg intramuscular injection, in addition to a nutritional supplement Isosource 1.5 Cal that is administered through the G-tube once every night.
Due to her complex past surgical history, the patient was sent from her local emergency department to our hospital. At presentation, the patient was febrile to 101 ℉. Blood pressure was 110/72 mmHg, and heart rate was 84 beats per minute. Further examination revealed diffuse abdominal tenderness with erythema and purulent drainage around the G-tube site on the left upper quadrant. The general examination showed a thin patient who is alert and oriented. Mucous membranes were moist. Lungs were clear to auscultation as she was breathing comfortably on room air. Heart sounds were regular without murmurs. Extremities were warm with intact pulses bilaterally and no edema. However, she had moderate muscle loss for her age. Skin was intact except for the previously mentioned erythema around the G-tube. Neurological examination revealed normal speech with comprehension, intact cranial nerves II–VII, XI, and XII, and 5/5 motor strength in all four extremities. Sensation was intact to light touch and pinprick throughout. Romberg test was negative. Gait was slowed owing to weakness but showed normal balance.
Serum chemistry showed sodium of 136 mmol/L, potassium of 2.9 mmol/L, chloride of 111 mmol/L, bicarbonate of 20 mmol/L, magnesium 1.2 mg/dL, calcium 6.1 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.0 mg/dL. Liver function tests showed low albumin of 2.5 g/dL, total bilirubin of 0.3 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase of 86 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) of 13 U/L and aspartate aminotransferase (AST) of 21 U/L. Complete blood count showed a white blood cell count of 3.97 × 109 /L, hemoglobin of 12.6 g/dL, hematocrit of 39.6%, and platelet count of 239 × 109 /L. Urinalysis showed normal appearance and color, specific gravity of 1.020, without detecting red blood cells (RBC), white blood cells (WBC), ketones, glucose, leukocytes esterase, or nitrites. A stool sample was sent for Clostridium difficile and a molecular enteric panel but was pending at that time. Two out of two blood cultures that were collected at her local emergency department were positive for Gram-negative rods. An abdominal radiograph confirmed G-tube location overlying the stomach and a computed tomography (CT) abdomen/pelvis with contrast showed nonspecific skin thickening and subcutaneous fat stranding along the G-tube insertion site (Fig. ). Due to a prior history of penicillin and sulfa anaphylactic reactions, the patient was empirically treated with intravenous ciprofloxacin 400 mg every 8 hours for bacteremia, and oral clindamycin 450 mg every 8 hours for the cellulitis surrounding her G-tube site, in addition to intravenous fluid resuscitation with normal saline at 75 mL/hour and electrolyte repletion with oral potassium chloride 20 mEq for four total doses, and intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g once.
A fluoroscopy G-tube study revealed no contrast leak or evidence of obstruction. On day 1 of hospitalization, the molecular enteric stool panel was positive for the Salmonella species. On day 4, two out of two blood cultures grew Salmonella enteritidis with susceptibilities to ampicillin, ceftriaxone, and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole. The patient was then transitioned from intravenous ciprofloxacin to a 14-day course of intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g every 24 hours. We resumed tube feeds through the G-tube on day 5 after a successful clamping trial. On day 6, the patient was somnolent and difficult to arouse, with poor skin turgor and dry mucous membranes. Vital signs remained within normal limits; T max of 98.6 ℉, blood pressure of 98/65 mmHg, and heart rate of 95 beats per minute. Laboratory results were significant for a serum creatinine level of 5.25 mg/dL and non-anion gap metabolic acidosis with sodium of 131 mmol/L, chloride of 105 mmol/L, and serum bicarbonate of 18 mmol/L. These clinical and laboratory findings of dehydration suggested the development of prerenal acute kidney injury (AKI) in the setting of prolonged GI volume loss. The patient was resuscitated with half normal saline containing 75 mEq sodium bicarbonate additive at 125 mL/hour. On day 8, the patient’s mental status improved but her G-tube began to leak, prompting clamping of the G-tube. The patient was encouraged to increase oral intake. Intravenous fluids were discontinued on day 9, as she was able to tolerate oral foods and liquids well. No further leakage was seen around her G-tube site. On day 13, the patient was discharged home with a peripherally inserted central line to finish a 14-day course of intravenous ceftriaxone (2 g every 24 hours).
Following completion of a 14-day course of intravenous ceftriaxone, the patient made a full recovery with resolution of her diarrhea and abdominal pain. Tube feeds were resumed without further issues. At a 4-month follow-up visit with her bariatric surgeon, the patient reports that she is doing well and happy with her progress. Electrolytes and serum creatinine were all within normal limits. The patient was readmitted 6 months following her initial hospitalization for abdominal pain and hematemesis due to a large marginal ulcer. She improved with conservative measures including bowel rest (nothing per os) and maintenance intravenous fluids, and was discharged 2 days later. At the time of discharge, her hemoglobin remained stable at 11.2 g/dL, and she was able to tolerate an oral diet. She continues to follow up with her bariatric surgeon in clinic with no recurrence of fever or diarrhea.

```python
from typing import Any
def simulate_medical_value(patient_note: str, question: str) -> Any:
    """Calculate a medical value from a patient note.

    Calculate a medical value from a patient note.
    
    Given a patient note and a calculation question, reason step by step:
    1. Carefully read the patient note to extract all relevant clinical values
    2. Identify what medical calculation/score is needed
    3. Apply the appropriate formula from the reference below
    4. Show your calculation step by step, checking your arithmetic
    
    FORMULAS (use these exact formulas):
    - APACHE II Score: Acute Physiology Score + Age Points + Chronic Health Points
    - Adjusted Body Weight: ABW = IBW + 0.4 * (actual_weight - IBW)
    - Albumin Corrected Anion Gap: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    - Anion Gap: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    - Body Mass Index (BMI): BMI = weight_kg / (height_m)^2
    - Body Surface Area (Mosteller): BSA = sqrt((height_cm * weight_kg) / 3600)
    - CHA2DS2-VASc Score: Sum of: CHF(+1), HTN(+1), Age>=75(+2)/65-74(+1), DM(+1), Stroke/TIA(+2), Vascular(+1), Female(+1)
    - CKD-EPI GFR (2021): 142 * min(Cr/kappa,1)^alpha * max(Cr/kappa,1)^-1.200 * 0.9938^age * sex_coef
    - CURB-65 Score: C + U + R + B + 65 (0-5 points)
    - Calcium Correction for Hypoalbuminemia: Corrected Ca = Ca + 0.8 * (4 - albumin)
    - Caprini VTE Score: Sum of weighted risk factors
    - Centor Score (McIsaac): Sum with age modifier
    - Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI): Sum of weighted comorbidity points + age points
    - Child-Pugh Score: Sum of points for bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
    - Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault): CrCl = ((140 - age) * adjusted_weight * gender_coef) / (Cr * 72)
    - Date of Conception: gestational_weeks - 2 (conception ~2 weeks after LMP)
    - Delta Gap: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (where 12 is normal AG)
    - Delta Ratio: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    - Estimated Due Date: 40 - current_gestational_age
    - FIB-4 Index: FIB-4 = (Age * AST) / (Platelets * sqrt(ALT))
    - FeverPAIN Score: Fever + Purulence + Attend rapidly + Inflamed tonsils + No cough/coryza (0-5)
    - Fractional Excretion of Sodium (FENa): FENa = (UNa * SCr) / (SNa * UCr) * 100
    - Framingham Risk Score: Complex multivariate equation based on age, sex, lipids, BP, smoking
    - Free Water Deficit: FWD = TBW% * weight * (Na/140 - 1)
    - Gestational Age: weeks + days/7
    - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): Eye + Verbal + Motor (3-15)
    - Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS): Sum of clinical and lab criteria (0-23)
    - HAS-BLED Score: H-A-S-B-L-E-D criteria (0-9 points)
    - HEART Score: Sum of History + ECG + Age + Risk factors + Troponin (each 0-2 points)
    - HOMA-IR: HOMA-IR = (Glucose * Insulin) / 405
    - Ideal Body Weight (Devine): Male: IBW = 50 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60), Female: IBW = 45.5 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60)
    - LDL Calculated (Friedewald): LDL = Total Cholesterol - HDL - (Triglycerides / 5)
    - MDRD GFR: 175 * (Cr)^-1.154 * (age)^-0.203 * sex_coef
    - MELD-Na Score: MELD = 10 * (0.957*ln(Cr) + 0.378*ln(Bili) + 1.120*ln(INR) + 0.643)
    - Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1 Rule): First 10kg: 4ml/kg/hr, Next 10kg: 2ml/kg/hr, Remaining: 1ml/kg/hr
    - Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): MAP = (2 * DBP + SBP) / 3
    - Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME): Sum of (opioid dose * conversion factor)
    - PERC Rule: 8 criteria; PERC negative if all 0
    - PSI/PORT Score: Demographics + Comorbidities + Physical Exam + Labs
    - QTc (Bazett): QTc = QT / sqrt(RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Framingham): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Fridericia): QTc = QT / RR^(1/3) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Hodges): QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)
    - QTc (Rautaharju): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI): Sum of 6 risk factors (0-6 points)
    - SIRS Criteria: Count of criteria met (>= 2 = SIRS positive)
    - SOFA Score: Sum of 6 organ system scores (0-24)
    - Serum Osmolality: Osm = 2*Na + BUN/2.8 + Glucose/18
    - Sodium Correction for Hyperglycemia: Corrected Na = Na + 0.024 * (glucose - 100)
    - Steroid Conversion: Prednisone equivalent = dose * conversion_factor
    - Target Weight: Target Weight = target_bmi * (height_m)^2
    - Wells' Criteria for DVT: Sum of clinical criteria points
    - Wells' Criteria for PE: Sum of weighted criteria
    
    Important:
    - Be precise with extracted values. Double-check your arithmetic.
    - For sex/gender: "man"/"male"/"he" → male, "woman"/"female"/"she" → female.
    - Convert units as needed (lbs→kg: ×0.453592, feet/inches→cm: (ft×12+in)×2.54).
    - For scoring systems: carefully check each criterion against the patient note.
      Look for conditions implied by medications or clinical findings, not just
      explicitly named conditions.
    
    CRITICAL FORMATTING INSTRUCTIONS FOR THE FINAL ANSWER:
    - If the answer is a numeric value, return ONLY the raw number (e.g., 6.25). Do NOT include units, commas, or symbols like "%" or "mmHg".
    - If the answer is a date, return ONLY the exact date string in MM/DD/YYYY format (e.g., 06/09/2002). Do NOT wrap it in extra quotes.

To calculate the albumin-corrected delta ratio, we use the patient's laboratory values from the time of initial admission:

1.  **Extract initial laboratory values:**
    *   Sodium (Na): 136 mmol/L
    *   Chloride (Cl): 111 mmol/L
    *   Bicarbonate (HCO3): 20 mmol/L
    *   Albumin: 2.5 g/dL

2.  **Calculate Anion Gap (AG):**
    *   Formula: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    *   Calculation: AG = 136 - (111 + 20) = 136 - 131 = 5

3.  **Calculate Albumin Corrected Anion Gap (Corrected AG):**
    *   Formula: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    *   Calculation: Corrected AG = 5 + 2.5 * (4 - 2.5)
    *   Calculation: 5 + 2.5 * (1.5) = 5 + 3.75 = 8.75

4.  **Calculate Delta Gap:**
    *   Formula: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (using the corrected AG as the effective anion gap)
    *   Calculation: Delta Gap = 8.75 - 12 = -3.25

5.  **Calculate Delta Ratio:**
    *   Formula: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    *   Calculation: Delta Ratio = -3.25 / (24 - 20) = -3.25 / 4 = -0.8125

<answer>
-0.8125
</answer>

args:
A 43-year-old woman presented to the outpatient clinic with the chief complaint of insidious-onset, persistent, moderately severe, and poorly localized abdominal pain for more than a month. She also mentioned the rapid onset of yellowish skin color, tea-colored urine, and swelling in bilateral legs 1 week prior to admission. She had a history of chronic hepatitis B infection and did not follow up regularly. The physical examination at admission revealed acute ill-looking appearance as well as icteric sclera, skin, and mucus membrane. Abdominal examination showed palpable liver and spleen contour with shifting dullness while no muscle guarding or rebounding pain was found upon palpation. The rest of the physical examination was unremarkable.
Laboratory examination revealed a total leukocyte count of 9,060 cells/μL, hemoglobin concentration of 12.1 g/dL, and platelet count of 243,000/μL. Her ALT was 127 U/L, AST 268 U/L, ALP 468 U/L, serum total bilirubin 2.9 mg/dL (direct fraction 1.1 mg/dL), albumin 3.2 g/dL, creatinine 76.9 µmol/L, lactate dehydrogenase 537 U/L, alpha-fetoprotein 317,333 IU/mL, INR 1.26, and activated partial thromboplastin time of 26.2 s on initial presentation. In addition, serum markers were positive for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B envelope antigen while hepatitis B virus DNA was 1.67 × 106 IU/mL. Abdominal sonography showed multiple hepatic masses over bilateral lobes, hepatosplenomegaly, and massive ascites indicating hepatic malignancy and liver cirrhosis. Triple-phase CT of the abdomen revealed infiltrative HCC bilaterally with lymphadenopathy over the retroperitoneum, main portal vein thrombosis, and bone metastasis at the third lumbar spine (Fig. ). According to the Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer (BCLC) classification the patient was diagnosed with advanced-stage HCC and received entecavir upon admission. Due to the rapid progression of tumor burden we administered nivolumab 200 mg (2.1 mg/day) intravenously on the 8th day of hospitalization and prescribed sorafenib 200 mg daily as combination therapy from the 9th day of hospitalization. On the 15th day sorafenib was discontinued due to deterioration of serum total bilirubin to 5.8 mg/dL, and on the 18th day her ALT was 1,303 U/L, AST 6,220 U/L, ALP 752 U/L, serum total bilirubin 16.9 mg/dL, INR 1.88, ammonia 54 µmol/L, creatinine 236 µmol/L, potassium 6.1 mmol/L, phosphate 7.4 mmol/L, calcium 2.1 mmol/L, and uric acid 1,118.2 µmol/L. Decompensated hepatic failure associated with TLS was diagnosed according to clinical and laboratory criteria defined by Cairo et al. []. After establishing TLS, we prescribed 1.5 mg of rasburicase twice daily from the 18th day, and the patient was admitted to the intensive care unit with vigorous hydration to maintain urine output. We also corrected electrolyte abnormalities and monitored vital signs closely. However, acute kidney injury with anuria developed along with hepatic encephalopathy despite intensive care, and the patient expired on the 21st day of hospitalization.

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 55)
ast: AST in U/L (e.g., 45)
alt: ALT in U/L (e.g., 50)
platelets: Platelet count in 10^9/L (e.g., 150)

args:
We report a case of 12 years old boy, black, student who was admitted to our department for a 1 year progressive slowly growing of not painful abdominal mass occupying the right flank and right-lower quadrant. The patient had history of chronic constipation of 2 years referring 1 bowel movements every third-fourth day with hard stools. He denies symptoms of vomiting, nausea, diarrhea, fever, hematocquezia, hematuria, anorexia, lost weight. Not learning or mental disabilities were reported from his parents.
The patient was hospitalized previously four times during his life by history of constipation treated with laxatives and enemas and discharged with food recommendations. Only family history of diabetes in his grandmother.
On initial evaluation, the patient had a temperature of 37°C, blood pressure of 100/70 mm Hg, a heart rate of 85 beats per minute, a respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, with weight 30 kg and height 133 cms (BMI = 16.5, 18th centile).
Physical examination revealed a good general condition, cardiac and lungs examination were unremarkable. Abdomen without scars, soft, not distended, tympanic in 4 quadrants, normoactive bowel sound, palpation revealing an abdominal mass smooth, mobile, nontender in the lower right flank and quadrant of ± 13 cm of diameter. Rectal examination revealed no palpable stool in the rectal vault and was hemoccult negative. No malformations were finding during the examination.
The laboratory test parameter during admission were: hemoglobin 13.2 g/dl, hematocrit of 39.5%, WBC 5.500, platelets 374.000 mm3; creatinine 0.8/BUN 70, Na:142, K:4.3, urinalysis and liver function tests were normal. Stool samples for parasites were negative.
Intravenous pyelogram revealed a large rounded mass of approximately 12 cm. in diameter at the colon which seemed to have an irregular mottled texture. The right ureter seemed to be somewhat displaced by the mass that produce a slightly enlargement of the renal pelvis (Fig. ). Roentgenologic examination of the colon after barium enema showed an intraluminal mass having a smooth contour and without any mucosal attachment (Fig. ).
Ultrasound of the abdomen showed a mass arising probably from the intestine with no evidence of hydronephrosis.
The flexible rectosigmoidoscopy was done and only passed through 25 cm of rectum which presented a normal rectum with a roomy sigmoid colon and a large, firm fecal ball. Sigmoidoscopic rectal biopsies were not performed and fragmentation was attempted but abandoned because of pain during the procedure. Repeated enemas and laxatives were unsuccessful in evacuating the fecal mass.
The patient underwent elective laparotomy. Preoperative bowel cleaning was performed with phosphosoda solution and lavage with warm saline until evacuations were liquid without fecal residue. A 20-cm midline incision (infraumbilical with supraumbilical extension) was made. During the procedure we founded an enlarged sigmoid colon lateralized to the right showing a large, rock-hard mass, smooth and ovoid in shape, measuring about 14 cm, which appeared to be a hardened mass of feces. The rest of the colon appeared normal and empty.
The sigmoid colon with the mass was resected (Fig. ) and an end-to-end anastomosis was done. The patient recovered for the surgical procedure and had an uneventful convalescence. After six months our patient refers 1 bowel movement per day performing soft stools with no recurrence of his symptoms leading a completely normal healthy life.
Examination of the gross specimen after incision of the intestinal wall showed a firm fecal mass covered with a white material (barium) which filled the lumen but was unattached to the wall of the colon (Fig. ). No lesions were observed at the sigmoid wall. Microscopic examination of the colon demonstrates normal intestinal mucosa without ulcers. Myenteric plexus shows nerve fibers with lack of ganglion cells (Fig. ).

args:
We report a case of 12 years old boy, black, student who was admitted to our department for a 1 year progressive slowly growing of not painful abdominal mass occupying the right flank and right-lower quadrant. The patient had history of chronic constipation of 2 years referring 1 bowel movements every third-fourth day with hard stools. He denies symptoms of vomiting, nausea, diarrhea, fever, hematocquezia, hematuria, anorexia, lost weight. Not learning or mental disabilities were reported from his parents.
The patient was hospitalized previously four times during his life by history of constipation treated with laxatives and enemas and discharged with food recommendations. Only family history of diabetes in his grandmother.
On initial evaluation, the patient had a temperature of 37°C, blood pressure of 100/70 mm Hg, a heart rate of 85 beats per minute, a respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, with weight 30 kg and height 133 cms (BMI = 16.5, 18th centile).
Physical examination revealed a good general condition, cardiac and lungs examination were unremarkable. Abdomen without scars, soft, not distended, tympanic in 4 quadrants, normoactive bowel sound, palpation revealing an abdominal mass smooth, mobile, nontender in the lower right flank and quadrant of ± 13 cm of diameter. Rectal examination revealed no palpable stool in the rectal vault and was hemoccult negative. No malformations were finding during the examination.
The laboratory test parameter during admission were: hemoglobin 13.2 g/dl, hematocrit of 39.5%, WBC 5.500, platelets 374.000 mm3; creatinine 0.8/BUN 70, Na:142, K:4.3, urinalysis and liver function tests were normal. Stool samples for parasites were negative.
Intravenous pyelogram revealed a large rounded mass of approximately 12 cm. in diameter at the colon which seemed to have an irregular mottled texture. The right ureter seemed to be somewhat displaced by the mass that produce a slightly enlargement of the renal pelvis (Fig. ). Roentgenologic examination of the colon after barium enema showed an intraluminal mass having a smooth contour and without any mucosal attachment (Fig. ).
Ultrasound of the abdomen showed a mass arising probably from the intestine with no evidence of hydronephrosis.
The flexible rectosigmoidoscopy was done and only passed through 25 cm of rectum which presented a normal rectum with a roomy sigmoid colon and a large, firm fecal ball. Sigmoidoscopic rectal biopsies were not performed and fragmentation was attempted but abandoned because of pain during the procedure. Repeated enemas and laxatives were unsuccessful in evacuating the fecal mass.
The patient underwent elective laparotomy. Preoperative bowel cleaning was performed with phosphosoda solution and lavage with warm saline until evacuations were liquid without fecal residue. A 20-cm midline incision (infraumbilical with supraumbilical extension) was made. During the procedure we founded an enlarged sigmoid colon lateralized to the right showing a large, rock-hard mass, smooth and ovoid in shape, measuring about 14 cm, which appeared to be a hardened mass of feces. The rest of the colon appeared normal and empty.
The sigmoid colon with the mass was resected (Fig. ) and an end-to-end anastomosis was done. The patient recovered for the surgical procedure and had an uneventful convalescence. After six months our patient refers 1 bowel movement per day performing soft stools with no recurrence of his symptoms leading a completely normal healthy life.
Examination of the gross specimen after incision of the intestinal wall showed a firm fecal mass covered with a white material (barium) which filled the lumen but was unattached to the wall of the colon (Fig. ). No lesions were observed at the sigmoid wall. Microscopic examination of the colon demonstrates normal intestinal mucosa without ulcers. Myenteric plexus shows nerve fibers with lack of ganglion cells (Fig. ).

temperature_abnormal: Temp > 38C or < 36C (+1)
heart_rate_elevated: HR > 90 bpm (+1)
respiratory_abnormal: RR > 20 or PaCO2 < 32 mmHg (+1)
wbc_abnormal: WBC > 12,000 or < 4,000 or > 10% bands (+1)

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: None
- Conditions ABSENT: temp_abnormal, tachycardia, tachypnea_hyperventilation, wbc_abnormal
- Demographics: Age=12, Sex=male

args: A 64-year-old man with a twelve-year history of compensated hepatitis C–related cirrhosis arrived to the hepatology day-hospital after his primary physician asked him to be evaluated for subtle abdominal fullness that had developed since the spring; he stated that by evening he occasionally loosened his belt one notch, yet he could still comfortably tie his work boots each morning and denied abrupt girth expansion, orthopnea, scrotal swelling, or early satiety. He had stopped drinking alcohol eight years prior and never resumed, denied intravenous drug use since achieving viral clearance with direct-acting antivirals three years ago, and had no prior episodes of gastrointestinal bleeding, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, or renal dysfunction. Other medical problems included well-controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily, primary hypertension treated with losartan 50 mg each morning, and mild hypertriglyceridemia for which he took omega-3 ethyl esters; he had never required diuretics, lactulose, rifaximin, or non-selective beta-blockers. Surgical history was limited to arthroscopic right knee meniscectomy, and he reported no medication allergies. He lives with his spouse, works as a school custodian, walks two miles on weekends, and quit a 20-pack-year tobacco habit fifteen years ago. Family history is notable for a brother who died of myocardial infarction at fifty-nine; no relatives have chronic liver disease. Review of systems revealed intermittent pruritus at night and occasional muscle cramps in the calves but no jaundice noticed by coworkers, dark urine, clay-colored stools, hematemesis, melena, bruising, confusion, sleep-wake reversal, weight loss, or fever. On arrival—before any intravenous access or medications—vital signs recorded temperature 36.9 °C, blood pressure 118/74 mm Hg, heart rate 82 beats/min, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. The patient was alert, conversed fluently, named the hospital and date correctly, and ambulated into the exam room without assistance. Sclerae were faintly yellow in bright light, conjunctivae were pink, and mild palmar erythema was present; there were fewer than a dozen spider angiomas scattered over the upper chest but no digital clubbing or Dupuytren contracture. Jugular venous pulsation measured approximately 6 cm H₂O at 30 °, carotid upstrokes were brisk, and the precordial examination revealed a regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lung fields were clear bilaterally. Inspection of the abdomen showed a gentle outward curve with a still-indented umbilicus; the abdominal wall moved symmetrically with respiration, and striae or caput medusae were absent. On percussion the mid-abdomen was tympanitic, transitioning to a narrow band of dullness in the dependent left flank while supine that slid medially when he was rolled onto that side, suggestive of only a small volume of free fluid. The fluid-wave maneuver required an assistant’s hand to appreciate a subtle impulse. The abdomen was non-tender, and a firm, nodular liver edge was felt 2 cm below the right costal margin on deep inspiration; the spleen tip was not palpable. There was no ankle edema, muscle wasting, or gynecomastia. Neurologic screening revealed normal speech, rapid alternating movements, 5/5 strength, symmetric reflexes, and no tremor on sustained wrist extension for thirty seconds. Capillary glucose measured 108 mg/dL. Point-of-care ultrasonography performed immediately prior to phlebotomy demonstrated a heterogeneously echogenic, shrunken liver with an irregular surface; portal venous flow was hepatopetal at 18 cm/s without thrombosis; the spleen measured 15.5 cm in length; and a thin anechoic stripe of fluid, maximal depth 1.8 cm in the right paracolic gutter and 1.5 cm in Morison’s pouch, displaced adjacent bowel but did not surround the liver dome—findings compatible with a modest amount of ascitic fluid. No focal hepatic mass or biliary duct dilation was seen, and both kidneys appeared normal in size and echogenicity. Immediately afterward a peripheral venous sample obtained before any vitamin K, albumin, diuretics, or antibiotics revealed: total bilirubin 1.55 mg/dL, serum albumin 3.26 g/dL, alanine aminotransferase 41 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 67 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 138 U/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase 176 U/L, sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 17 mg/dL, creatinine 0.94 mg/dL (eGFR > 90 mL/min/1.73 m²), hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, white blood cell count 5.6 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 118 × 10⁹/L, and a prothrombin time corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 1.28. Urinalysis obtained simultaneously showed 1+ bilirubin, moderate urobilinogen, no protein, and no evidence of infection. Upright chest radiograph, taken before any intervention, displayed mildly elevated hemidiaphragms without effusion or infiltrate, and a twelve-lead electrocardiogram documented normal sinus rhythm at 80 beats/min with a QTc of 408 ms and no ischemic changes. Up to this point the patient had not received paracentesis, diuretics, lactulose, albumin, antibiotics, beta-blockers, or sedation, so the clinical findings and laboratory values detailed above represent his untreated physiologic state at presentation.
CRITICAL HINT: For Child-Pugh, ascites_grade is 1=Absent, 2=Slight/Mild, 3=Moderate/Severe. encephalopathy_grade is 1=Absent, 2=Grade 1-2, 3=Grade 3-4.

args: A 52-year-old man with a history of alcohol-associated cirrhosis complicated by prior episodes of ascites and grade II encephalopathy was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after being found somnolent and jaundiced on the floor of his apartment by a neighbor who had last seen him well two days earlier. According to pre-hospital personnel, he was moaning incomprehensibly, opened his eyes only to painful sternal rub, and withdrew his upper limbs sluggishly, prompting them to perform a more formal neurologic check that yielded a Glasgow Coma Score of 7 in the field; no sedatives, opioids, or paralytics had been given during transport, and the airway remained uninstrumented throughout. On arrival he lay in the resuscitation bay breathing through a non-rebreather mask connected to wall oxygen; an arterial blood gas drawn within ten minutes demonstrated a PaO₂ of 65 mm Hg while receiving a measured 52 % inspired oxygen fraction, pH 7.31, and PaCO₂ 48 mm Hg. Respiratory rate was 28 breaths/min with see-saw pattern but no accessory muscle fatigue, and auscultation revealed coarse crackles at both lung bases without wheeze. His skin was cool and slightly clammy, capillary refill prolonged to four seconds, and automated cuff readings repeated three times showed a systolic pressure of 80 mm Hg and diastolic pressure of 45 mm Hg before any crystalloid infusion or vasoactive medications were started; heart rate was 118 beats/min in a regular rhythm, and peripheral pulses were thready. Scleral icterus and spider angiomas were evident, the abdomen was distended with a positive fluid wave yet soft to palpation, and shifting dullness suggested moderate ascites; there was right upper-quadrant tenderness without guarding. Lower extremities displayed pitting edema to the mid-shin. No stigmata of acute gastrointestinal bleeding were obvious, though dark dried emesis coated his beard, raising concern for recent hematemesis; nasogastric lavage performed later returned coffee-ground fluid but, at the time vital data were collected, no lavage had yet been attempted. Initial laboratory investigations obtained prior to any antibiotics, fluids, albumin, or lactulose included a complete blood count showing a white cell count of 14.1 × 10³/µL, hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL, and a platelet count of 115 000 /µL. Basic metabolic panel revealed sodium 131 mmol/L, potassium 4.6 mmol/L, chloride 97 mmol/L, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, glucose 102 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 28 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, comparable to his documented outpatient baseline of 1.0. Total bilirubin was markedly elevated at 3.5 mg/dL with a direct fraction predominance, aspartate aminotransferase 88 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 60 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase 190 U/L, in keeping with cholestatic decompensation. International normalized ratio was 2.1 and activated partial thromboplastin time 46 seconds, reflecting synthetic dysfunction. Venous lactate returned at 3.8 mmol/L. Point-of-care ultrasound demonstrated a small, hyperdynamic left ventricle, a flattened inferior vena cava with >50 % collapse on inspiration, and moderate ascitic fluid without sonographic evidence of portal vein thrombosis. Bedside chest radiograph obtained while he remained on the mask showed bilateral hazy opacities worse at the dependent zones, suggestive of aspiration pneumonitis or evolving pulmonary edema; cardiac silhouette size was normal. Foley catheterization performed on arrival drained scant amber urine, but review of the emergency medical services flow sheet combined with collateral history from a visiting friend suggested he had voided roughly 1.1 liters in the preceding twenty-four hours, consistent with preserved renal output. An electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes, and bedside glucose remained stable during observation. args: A 68-year-old man arrived this afternoon to the comprehensive peri-operative clinic at the request of the vascular surgery service, six days before a planned open repair of a 5.8-cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm discovered incidentally on surveillance ultrasound for renal insufficiency; the operative team anticipates a transperitoneal midline approach lasting four to five hours with need for aortic cross-clamping below the renal arteries, possible cell-saver autotransfusion, and immediate postoperative admission to the surgical intensive care unit for invasive hemodynamic and urine-output monitoring. He strolled slowly from the parking lot using the handrail on the front steps and requested a brief rest in the waiting area, explaining that over the past year he has become short of breath after walking two New-York-City blocks or carrying a basket of laundry up one flight. Three years ago he was hospitalized for rapid weight gain, bilateral ankle swelling, and nocturnal breathlessness; echocardiography obtained during that admission demonstrated a globally dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 30 %, mild functional mitral regurgitation, and normal right-sided chamber size. He required two additional admissions for volume overload in the subsequent 18 months, most recently three months ago after a long road trip that limited his access to prescribed medications; intravenous loop diuretic therapy achieved a 4-kg net weight reduction. His current regimen includes carvedilol 25 mg twice daily, sacubitril-valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily, furosemide 40 mg with breakfast, spironolactone 25 mg at bedtime, and a 2-gram-sodium diet; he records daily morning weights in a notebook reviewed today and calls his primary clinician if he gains more than 2 lb in 24 hours. He sleeps on three pillows, awakens once or twice weekly gasping for air until he sits at the edge of the bed, and notes that his socks leave imprints by evening although the swelling subsides overnight. There have been no episodes of syncope, palpitations, or unexplained presyncope. Blood pressure has been elevated since his early fifties and is treated with amlodipine 10 mg each morning; type 2 diabetes diagnosed five years earlier is managed solely with metformin extended-release 1000 mg with supper and a carbohydrate-restricted diet taught by a nutritionist, with a glycated hemoglobin of 7.0 % at last month’s primary-care visit. He monitors capillary glucose twice weekly, reporting readings between 110 and 160 mg/dL and no hypoglycemic events. He has never experienced abrupt unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, monocular vision loss, or episodes suggestive of transient neurologic ischemia. Chronic kidney disease stage 3a, attributed to combined hypertensive and diabetic microvascular change, is co-managed with nephrology; serum creatinine has hovered in the 1.2–1.4 mg/dL range for the past two years. Surgical history is limited to a remote laparoscopic cholecystectomy without complications. args: A 55-year-old man with long-standing hepatitis C cirrhosis complicated by portal hypertension, large esophageal varices previously treated with rubber-band ligation, and a recent flare of renal dysfunction attributed to type 1 hepatorenal syndrome was rushed to the emergency department after vomiting approximately 400 mL of bright-red blood at home earlier this morning; emergency medical services suctioned the oropharynx, placed two 18-gauge peripheral lines, and gave nothing by mouth or vein en route so that initial laboratories would reflect the untreated state. His partner reported that over the last seven days he had become progressively more icteric and confused and, because serum creatinine had crept from a long-standing baseline near 1.0 mg/dL to more than double that value at a neighborhood hospital, nephrology there arranged two standard intermittent hemodialysis runs—one three days ago and another yesterday—through a fresh right internal jugular temporary catheter; between treatments he remained alert but lethargic, and no continuous blood-purification device was ever started. The patient has never undergone a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt, abdominal surgery, or transplantation evaluation, takes no anticoagulants, and stopped drinking alcohol five years ago; medications at home include nadolol 40 mg nightly, lactulose 30 mL twice daily, and low-dose furosemide–spironolactone, all held since the bleeding episode began. There is no history of diabetes, chronic dialysis dependence, cardiac disease, or inherited coagulopathies, and family history is unremarkable. He smokes ten cigarettes per day, denies illicit drugs, and works sporadically as a mechanic. On arrival—before any intravenous crystalloids, blood products, proton-pump inhibitors, octreotide, vasopressors, or antibiotics were started—vital signs were blood pressure 102/60 mm Hg, heart rate 112 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, temperature 36.8 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. He appeared pale and deeply jaundiced, with dried sanguineous crust on the lips and a fetid, fishy odor to the breath. Neck inspection showed a recently placed triple-lumen catheter without erythema or exudate; no arteriovenous fistulae, graft scars, or tunneled devices were seen elsewhere. Jugular venous pressure was flat at the angle of the mandible. Cardiac examination revealed a hyperdynamic precordium and grade I/VI midsystolic murmur; lung fields were clear. The abdomen was distended but soft, with a fluid wave suggesting moderate ascites; the liver edge was difficult to palpate, the spleen tip descended to two fingerbreadths below the costal margin, and there was no rebound or guarding. Caput medusae were absent, yet numerous spider angiomas dotted the upper chest; bilateral pitting edema reached the mid-calf. Neurologically he was oriented to person and place but not date, answered questions slowly, and displayed asterixis on dorsiflexion of the wrists. Rectal examination produced maroon stool positive for occult blood. Shortly after placement of a large-bore nasogastric tube, which returned a column of dark clots, a 10-mL peripheral venous sample was obtained for laboratory assessment. Initial chemistry panel, resulted within thirty minutes and still prior to any therapeutic intervention, demonstrated serum sodium 121 mEq/L, potassium 4.4 mEq/L, chloride 95 mEq/L, bicarbonate 20 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 32 mg/dL, and creatinine 68 µmol/L. Total bilirubin measured 9.7 mg/dL with direct fraction pending; albumin was 2.6 g/dL. Aspartate aminotransferase was 88 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 56 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase 162 U/L. Coagulation studies revealed a prothrombin time of 21.9 seconds corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 1.95; fibrinogen was 178 mg/dL, and platelet count 66 × 10³/µL. Arterial blood gas on room air showed pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 33 mm Hg, PaO₂ 92 mm Hg, and lactate 3.1 mmol/L. Complete blood count recorded hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL, hematocrit 25 %, mean corpuscular volume 100 fL, and white blood cell count 12.8 × 10³/µL with 84 % neutrophils. A point-of-care ultrasound performed at the bedside before ordering any fluids revealed a coarse, nodular liver with hepatofugal portal venous flow, moderate free fluid, and kidneys of normal size but increased cortical echogenicity; inferior vena cava diameter was collapsible with inspiration, consistent with intravascular depletion. Portable chest radiograph showed clear lungs and correct catheter tip positioning in the superior vena cava; electrocardiography displayed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes. Bladder scan measured 40 mL, and no urine had been passed since arrival. The cumulative presentation—fresh hematemesis, significant jaundice, moderate coagulopathy, dilutional hyponatremia, elevated bilirubin, and recent need for two standard hemodialysis sessions—was reviewed with gastroenterology, hepatology, critical-care, and nephrology teams while transfusion products, endoscopic equipment, and pharmacologic resuscitation were being mobilized, but the patient had received none of these interventions at the moment the initial laboratory values were documented.

output.reasoning: The MELD-Na score requires serum bilirubin, creatinine, sodium, and INR. From the note: Bilirubin is 9.7 mg/dL. Creatinine is 68 µmol/L (0.77 mg/dL). Sodium is 121 mEq/L. INR is 1.95. All values are explicitly documented in the initial laboratory results prior to intervention.

def extract_calculator_values(
    patient_note: str,
    calculator_name: str,
    field_descriptions: str,
    reasoning_context: str,
) -> dict:
    """Extract specific clinical values from a patient note for a medical calculator.

    You are given:
    - A patient note with clinical information
    - The name of the calculator to extract values for
    - A description of each field to extract (with units and expected ranges)
    - Optional reasoning context from a prior analysis stage

    Instructions:
    - Use the EXACT parameter names from the field descriptions as JSON keys
    - CRITICAL: Always extract the patient's baseline or admission values (prior to any treatment or surgery) unless the question specifically asks for post-treatment values. Pay close attention to the timeline in the note.
    - CRITICAL: If a field name ends in '_score' or '_points' (e.g., 'bun_score', 'age_score'), you MUST calculate and provide the integer point value based on the scoring system, NOT the raw clinical measurement (e.g., if bun_score is requested, give the points for BUN, not the raw BUN in mg/dL).
    - For all other fields, extract the RAW numeric value (e.g., if 'platelets' is requested, provide 115, not the point value).
    - Convert units ONLY for weight (lbs→kg: ×0.453592) and height (feet/inches→cm).
    - CRITICAL: DO NOT convert units for lab values. Extract the raw numeric value exactly as it appears in the text (e.g., if the text says 26 g/L, extract 26. If it says 120 mmol/L, extract 120).
    - Extract dates exactly as they appear in the text (e.g., "09/05/2001").
    - For boolean conditions: True if present, False if absent
    - If a condition is not mentioned, assume it is absent (False or 0)
    - For scoring systems with mutually exclusive severity levels (e.g., mild vs moderate/severe liver disease), mark ONLY the single most appropriate severity level as True.
    - Include ALL fields, even if the value is False/0/absent
    - If a required numeric value or field is entirely unmentioned and cannot be inferred, OMIT it from the 'extracted' dictionary completely instead of writing 'missing' or null.
    - CRITICAL: Do NOT guess or assume standard physiological values. If it is missing, omit it.

    Return a dict with:
    - "extracted": {"param_name": value, ...} for each field
    - "missing": ["list of values truly not determinable from the note"]

    Examples:
    >>> extract_calculator_values("A 70yo male, 80kg, 175cm, Cr 1.2", "Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault)", "age: years, sex: male/female, weight_kg: kg, creatinine_mg_dl: mg/dL", "")
    {'extracted': {'age': 70, 'sex': 'male', 'weight_kg': 80, 'creatinine_mg_dl': 1.2}, 'missing': []}
    """
    ...

```

args: A 55-year-old man with long-standing hepatitis C cirrhosis complicated by portal hypertension, large esophageal varices previously treated with rubber-band ligation, and a recent flare of renal dysfunction attributed to type 1 hepatorenal syndrome was rushed to the emergency department after vomiting approximately 400 mL of bright-red blood at home earlier this morning; emergency medical services suctioned the oropharynx, placed two 18-gauge peripheral lines, and gave nothing by mouth or vein en route so that initial laboratories would reflect the untreated state. His partner reported that over the last seven days he had become progressively more icteric and confused and, because serum creatinine had crept from a long-standing baseline near 1.0 mg/dL to more than double that value at a neighborhood hospital, nephrology there arranged two standard intermittent hemodialysis runs—one three days ago and another yesterday—through a fresh right internal jugular temporary catheter; between treatments he remained alert but lethargic, and no continuous blood-purification device was ever started. The patient has never undergone a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt, abdominal surgery, or transplantation evaluation, takes no anticoagulants, and stopped drinking alcohol five years ago; medications at home include nadolol 40 mg nightly, lactulose 30 mL twice daily, and low-dose furosemide–spironolactone, all held since the bleeding episode began. There is no history of diabetes, chronic dialysis dependence, cardiac disease, or inherited coagulopathies, and family history is unremarkable. He smokes ten cigarettes per day, denies illicit drugs, and works sporadically as a mechanic. On arrival—before any intravenous crystalloids, blood products, proton-pump inhibitors, octreotide, vasopressors, or antibiotics were started—vital signs were blood pressure 102/60 mm Hg, heart rate 112 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, temperature 36.8 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. He appeared pale and deeply jaundiced, with dried sanguineous crust on the lips and a fetid, fishy odor to the breath. Neck inspection showed a recently placed triple-lumen catheter without erythema or exudate; no arteriovenous fistulae, graft scars, or tunneled devices were seen elsewhere. Jugular venous pressure was flat at the angle of the mandible. Cardiac examination revealed a hyperdynamic precordium and grade I/VI midsystolic murmur; lung fields were clear. The abdomen was distended but soft, with a fluid wave suggesting moderate ascites; the liver edge was difficult to palpate, the spleen tip descended to two fingerbreadths below the costal margin, and there was no rebound or guarding. Caput medusae were absent, yet numerous spider angiomas dotted the upper chest; bilateral pitting edema reached the mid-calf. Neurologically he was oriented to person and place but not date, answered questions slowly, and displayed asterixis on dorsiflexion of the wrists. Rectal examination produced maroon stool positive for occult blood. Shortly after placement of a large-bore nasogastric tube, which returned a column of dark clots, a 10-mL peripheral venous sample was obtained for laboratory assessment. Initial chemistry panel, resulted within thirty minutes and still prior to any therapeutic intervention, demonstrated serum sodium 121 mEq/L, potassium 4.4 mEq/L, chloride 95 mEq/L, bicarbonate 20 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 32 mg/dL, and creatinine 68 µmol/L. Total bilirubin measured 9.7 mg/dL with direct fraction pending; albumin was 2.6 g/dL. Aspartate aminotransferase was 88 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 56 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase 162 U/L. Coagulation studies revealed a prothrombin time of 21.9 seconds corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 1.95; fibrinogen was 178 mg/dL, and platelet count 66 × 10³/µL. Arterial blood gas on room air showed pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 33 mm Hg, PaO₂ 92 mm Hg, and lactate 3.1 mmol/L. Complete blood count recorded hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL, hematocrit 25 %, mean corpuscular volume 100 fL, and white blood cell count 12.8 × 10³/µL with 84 % neutrophils. A point-of-care ultrasound performed at the bedside before ordering any fluids revealed a coarse, nodular liver with hepatofugal portal venous flow, moderate free fluid, and kidneys of normal size but increased cortical echogenicity; inferior vena cava diameter was collapsible with inspiration, consistent with intravascular depletion. Portable chest radiograph showed clear lungs and correct catheter tip positioning in the superior vena cava; electrocardiography displayed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes. Bladder scan measured 40 mL, and no urine had been passed since arrival. The cumulative presentation—fresh hematemesis, significant jaundice, moderate coagulopathy, dilutional hyponatremia, elevated bilirubin, and recent need for two standard hemodialysis sessions—was reviewed with gastroenterology, hepatology, critical-care, and nephrology teams while transfusion products, endoscopic equipment, and pharmacologic resuscitation were being mobilized, but the patient had received none of these interventions at the moment the initial laboratory values were documented.

args:
A 60-year-old female with 2 years history of hypothyroidism presented to our emergency department with the complaints of dry cough and fever for 3 days and severe weakness of all limbs for 3 h before presenting to the hospital. The history of presenting complaints revealed that for last 7-8 months she had experienced occasional brief episodes of limb weakness, which tends to occur at rest following exertion. For the last 3 days, the patient experienced increasing episodes of weakness and along with her meals she had also consumed approximately 200 ml coconut water per day. There was no history of recent animal bite, illicit drug or alcohol abuse. She had no past medical history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, tuberculosis, and chronic kidney disease. Her current medications included tablet thyroxine 100 mcg before breakfast.
On presentation, she was afebrile. Her heart rate was 80/min, respiratory rate was 20/min, systolic blood pressure was 86 mmHg, and oxygen saturation of 100% on room air. Physical examination revealed hyperpigmentation of the palmar creases and the knuckles and patchy hyperpigmentation of the oral mucosa [Figures and ]. Neurological examination revealed fully intact mental status, 1/5 power in both upper and lower extremities on the medical Research Council scale and diminished deep tendon reflexes in all extremities. Superficial, deep, and cortical sensations were intact and cranial nerves; fundoscopy was normal. Examination of abdomen, cardiovascular, and respiratory systems were unremarkable.
The initial laboratory tests showed serum sodium of 123 mmol/L, serum potassium 10.4 mmol/L, serum chloride 103 mmol/L, serum creatinine 0.89 mg/dL, and random blood sugar 99 mg/dL. Arterial blood gas analysis showed metabolic acidosis with high anion gap: pH 7.29, PCO224, PO282, HCO312, anion gap 20. Electrocardiogram (ECG) showed tall and peaked T-waves. Transtubular potassium gradient value was 5.1. Other hematological and biochemical tests, including complete blood counts, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, urine analysis, serum calcium, serum magnesium, creatine phosphokinase, thyroid and liver function tests were all within the normal limits. Hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis C virus, and human immunodeficiency virus serology was negative. Chest X-ray and abdominal ultrasound examination did not reveal any abnormality.
Hypotension, hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia suggested adrenal crisis due to acute stress in long standing primary adrenal insufficiency. She was treated with normal saline, antibiotics, 10 ml of 10% calcium gluconate intravenously, 10 mg of nebulized salbutamol, slow intravenous injection of 10 units of regular insulin added to 50 ml glucose 50% and 100 mg intravenous bolus of hydrocortisone every 6 hourly. Her limb power improved to 4/5 in few hours. After 6 h serum sodium was 126 mmol/L, potassium 7.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 17 mmol/L, and ECG abnormalities resolved. Intravenous hydrocortisone was tapered over 3 days and replaced with long acting glucocorticoid prednisolone in replacement dosage. Her serial serum potassium and sodium levels after steroids replacement returned to normal level. Adrenal function test results showed: Baseline cortisol 2.87 mcg/dL (normal level 6-26 mcg/dL), increasing to 3.01 mcg/dL (normal > 20 mcg/dL) 60 min after short synacthen test. Autoantibody screening showed positive thyroid autoantibodies. Adrenal autoantibodies against adrenal cytoplasm, 21-hydroxylase, 17-alpha hydroxylase and side chain cleavage enzyme could not be done.

sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
chloride: Serum chloride in mEq/L (e.g., 100)
bicarbonate: Serum bicarbonate in mEq/L (e.g., 24)
