args: A 4-month-old female infant was referred to the Department of Pediatric Neurology of Daejeon St. Mary's Hospital with a diagnosis of microcephaly and developmental delay. There was no family history of neurodevelopmental or genetic disorders, and she was the only child of Korean nonconsanguineous parents. The pregnancy had been uneventful. At age 4 months, the weight was 6.6 kg (25th percentile), length was 63 cm (50th percentile), and head circumstance was 35.5 cm (less than the third percentile). Her early development was characterized by delays in fine motor, achieving gross motor, language, and social–cognitive milestones. She could not control her head or hold objects until 4 months of age. Estimation using the Bayley Scale of Infant and Toddler Development, Third Edition, was performed at age 2 years and demonstrated marked GDDs (language cognitive, developmental, and motor ages: 8, 13, and 9–10 months, respectively). At age of 2 years, she was in the 20th percentile for weight (9.6 kg), the 50th percentile for height (81 cm), and first percentile for head circumference (43 cm). Physical examination demonstrated a well-nourished girl with microcephaly at age of 2 years and without CFN dysplasia (). Estimated orbital parameters at age of 2 years were all within the 10th percentile to 25th percentile (interpupillary distance 43 mm, inner canthal distance 24 mm, and outer canthal distance 71 mm). The palpebral fissures slightly sloped upward. Skull X-ray revealed no gross abnormality in cranial vault and no craniosynostosis (). Her ears, feet, and hands were normal in shape and size (). Skeletal surveillance including spine, both hands, and foot was done, and results were normal (). Deep tendon reflexes, muscle bulk, and muscle tone were normal. Brain magnetic resonance imaging revealed schizencephaly and dysgenesis of corpus callosum (). Her audiometry test result (auditory evoked potential and auditory brain stem response) was normal, and her response to sound seems to appropriate. The results of visual evoked potential and ophthalmologic tests were normal, but her visual perception was slow and did not seem accurate. Ophthalmological examinations of fundus and optic nerve were normal at ages 6 months and 2 years. Electroencephalography (EEG) observed continuous high-amplitude spikes and slow waves on both temporoparietal areas (). Despite abnormalities in EEG, she did not have a history of seizures. Pelvic sonogram screen reported no malformations. Echocardiogram showed no structural abnormalities. The laboratory tests such as growth hormone, thyroid function test, and metabolic workup were all within normal ranges.

weight_kg: Patient weight in kilograms (e.g., 70.0)
height_cm: Patient height in centimeters (e.g., 170.0)

args:
The patient was a 25-year-old obese man (height, 159.8 cm; body weight, 92.5 kg). At birth, he was diagnosed with double-outlet right ventricle, pulmonary atresia, and atrioventricular septal defect after cyanosis and heart murmur were evident. He underwent a right-modified Blalock-Taussig shunt at 34 days of age to increase the pulmonary blood flow. Cardiac catheterization indicated that the left ventricle was hypoplastic (left ventricular end-diastolic volume, 58% of normal), and pediatric cardiologists clarified the impossibility of biventricular repair. Therefore, he underwent the Glenn procedure at the age of 1 year and a fenestrated total cavopulmonary connection at the age of 3 years.
In this patient, the systemic ventricle was a morphologic right ventricle with a low ejection fraction, and the central venous pressure (CVP) was high at 16 mmHg; therefore, he was at a greater risk of hemodynamic instability.
At the age of 20 years, abdominal ultrasonography detected a large (>6 cm) tumor in segment 6 of the liver. Surgical treatment in Fontan-palliated patients is deemed unpredictable; therefore, he was treated non-surgically. However, at 25 years, protein induced by vitamin K antagonist-II level increased to 336 mAU/ml (cutoff value, 40 mAU/ml). Hepatocellular carcinoma was suspected; therefore, he was admitted to our hospital for surgical liver resection. On admission, the Child-Pugh class was determined as A. Blood hemoglobin was 17.3 mg/dl, platelet count was 21.3 × 104/μl, and prothrombin time was 82.3%. The liver function was well preserved. Preoperative echocardiography revealed that the right-ventricular ejection fraction was 34% with a limited atrioventricular valvular regurgitation; CVP was 16 mmHg, saturation of peripheral oxygen (SpO2) was 94% on room air at rest, and PVR was low (1.0 WUm2).
It is difficult to determine whether laparoscopic surgery or open laparotomy procedure is more advantageous for a patient with Fontan physiology. Considerable preoperative discussions were conducted among the medical team members. In patients with Fontan physiology, adequate intravascular volume (preload) and low PVR should be maintained during the surgical procedure. During laparoscopic surgery, hypercarbia due to CO2 insufflation and high airway pressure caused by obesity could increase PVR. However, postoperative pain after open surgery could also increase PVR; therefore, we selected the less-invasive laparoscopic surgery for this Fontan-palliated patient. During pneumoperitoneum, this patient was scheduled to inhale nitric oxide, which dilates the pulmonary blood vessels selectively. Moreover, CO was measured continuously by transpulmonary thermodilution, such as a pulse contour cardiac output catheter (PiCCO; PULSION Medical Systems, Munich, Germany), in addition to transesophageal echography.
We planned to maintain low abdominal air pressure during pneumoperitoneum and to shift promptly to open abdominal surgery if hemodynamic instability became apparent because of the accumulated partial pressure of carbon dioxide (PaCO2) or postural change.
In the operating room, the patient’s electrocardiogram, SpO2, and noninvasive blood pressure (NIBP) were monitored. At baseline, NIBP was 132/88 mmHg, heart rate was 91/min, and SpO2 was 93% under room air conditions. After insertion of epidural catheter, general anesthesia was induced by propofol (90 mg), remifentanil (0.2 μg/kg/min), and rocuronium (100 mg). After orotracheal intubation, anesthesia was maintained by desflurane inhalation and fentanyl and remifentanil infusion. He received volume-controlled ventilation with fraction of inspired oxygen (FIO2) of 0.7, positive end-expiratory pressure of 6 cmH2O, peak inspiratory pressure of less than or equal to 22 cmH2O, tidal volume of 450 ml, and respiratory rate of 15–18 breaths/min. Minute ventilation was adjusted to maintain end-tidal CO2 (EtCO2) at 30–35 mmHg.
With ultrasound guidance, CO and stroke volume variant were monitored via the PiCCO catheter. Then, a central venous catheter (PreSep CatheterTM; Edwards Lifesciences, Irvine, CA, USA) was placed via the right internal jugular vein to monitor CVP and central venous saturation (ScvO2) continuously. The tip of the central venous catheter was located just cephalad to the connection between the superior vena cava and the right pulmonary artery. Figure shows the trend of the vital signs, including CO, cardiac index (CI), CVP, ScvO2, and EtCO2 during anesthesia. Time 0 was defined as the start of general anesthesia. At baseline, FIO2 was 0.7, CI was 1.75 l/min/m2, CVP was 20 mmHg, and ScvO2 was 74%.
After initiating pneumoperitoneum, arterial blood pressure (ABP) increased slightly, but CO and ScvO2 were invariant. After postural change to the reverse Trendelenburg position, CVP decreased from 17 to 13 mmHg, but CO and ScvO2 remained unchanged. The insufflation pressure was maintained at 8–10 cmH2O. We administered an appropriate muscle relaxant for optimum surgical conditions. During pneumoperitoneum, the patient inhaled nitric oxide to decrease PVR. The Pringle maneuver during the liver resection did not affect CVP adversely. After withdrawal of the reverse Trendelenburg position, the CVP increased rapidly from 14 to 24 mmHg, but decreased subsequently to 20 mmHg. Nevertheless, CO and ScvO2 remained stable. CI and CO were stable at low levels, and laparoscopic partial hepatectomy was concluded successfully with hemodynamic stability. Transesophageal echography revealed no remarkable changes. Blood loss and urine output were 295 ml and 158 ml, respectively, which were replaced with 2200 ml of crystalloid.
After reversal of neuromuscular blockade, the patient was extubated and admitted to the intensive care unit. Postoperative analgesia using levobupivacaine and fentanyl was successfully administered via an epidural catheter. He remained hemodynamically stable and was shifted to the general ward on the 1st postoperative day. He was discharged on the 8th postoperative day with no postoperative complications. The resected liver mass was pathologically diagnosed as focal nodular hyperplasia with no malignancy.

weight_kg: Actual patient weight in kilograms (e.g., 100.0)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
height_cm: Patient height in centimeters (e.g., 170.0)

args:
Clinical summary
A 43-year-old Danish man who had suffered from persistent occipito-nuchal pain for 10 years flew to Japan for sightseeing in June, 1979. He vomited in the airplane and complained of sever malaise. Next day, progressive muscle weakness and dyspnea on exertion appeared. On day 3, myalgia of both thighs started, and marked sweating happened on day 4. He was admitted to a local hospital in Tokyo on day 5. Urine excretion was kept, but the urine color was black. He smoked cigarettes and drank alcohol, but did not use any special medication. No family history of muscle disease was recorded. On admission, his mental status was clear. Hypothermia (35°C), tachycardia (120/minutes), and hypertension (150-170/100-120 mmHg) were pointed out. He complained of cold sensation on the lower extremities. Because of systemic myalgia with tenderness, he could not sit up on the bed. Decrease of urine volume (415 mL/day) with black-colored appearance persisted. The laboratory data on admission included neutrophilia (20,000/µL), blood urea nitrogen 42 mg/dL, creatinine 2.0 mg/dL, aspartate transaminase 700 IU/L, alanine aminotransferase 130 IU/L, lactate dehydrogenase 1,860 IU/L, sodium 137 mEq/L, potassium 5.4 mEq/L, chloride 95 mEq/L, and calcium 3.7 mEq/L. The urine revealed macroscopic hematuria but without red cells in the sediment. Myoglobin was immunologically detected in his urine. On day 7, he expired suddenly and unexpectedly. No dialysis therapy was given throughout his illness.
Autopsy findings
Autopsy was performed 6 hours after death in the Department of Pathology, Keio University School of Medicine, Tokyo, Japan. A well-muscled body weighed 72 kg, with height of 175 cm. No gross abnormality was discerned in skeletal and cardiac muscles (Figure ).
Microscopically, myolytic fibers were dispersed in both skeletal and cardiac muscles. Cellular reactions were sparse: infiltration of macrophages and neutrophils was minimal. The kidneys weighed 160 g (left) and 140 g (right). Eosinophilic granular casts were observed in the lower nephrons, and the casts were positive for Ralph’s benzidine reaction [] (Figure , ).
The casts in the FFPE sections showed myoglobin immunoreactivity with a peroxidase-antiperoxidase (PAP) method [] (Figure ). The direct cause of death was pulmonary edema due to acute heart failure. The cause for his persistent nuchal pain was not clarified.
Liquid chromatography tests for arsenics and heavy metals were negative in the liver. Viral isolation trials using the -20°C frozen striated muscles, kidney, and serum were unsuccessful: in vitro experiment with human erythroleukemia cells and in vivo experiment with suckling mice failed to identify any virus. Immunofluorescence studies for Coxsackie viruses (A12, B3, B4, and B5) and herpes simplex virus were negative.
The final anatomical diagnosis was idiopathic rhabdomyolysis with acute renal failure due to myoglobin cast formation.

weight_kg: Patient weight in kg (e.g., 70)
sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 150)
age: Patient age in years (e.g., 65)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")

args:
A 31-year-old woman was diagnosed with hypertension by a private clinic in August 2013, based upon a systolic blood pressure of 160 mm Hg and she was prescribed antihypertensive drugs to treat the condition. Initial therapy consisted of hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg and losartan 50 mg daily. After 1 week, her blood pressure was still high, so the doctor changed her medication to amlodipine 5 mg, losartan 100 mg, and indapamide 2.5 mg per day. Five days after initiating this second regimen of drugs, she developed pretibial pitting edema, and thorough workup was done for the symptom. There were bilateral adrenal masses on abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT). Routine blood tests revealed the following: potassium 2.2 mmol/L, plasma renin activity (PRA) 0.12 ng/mL/hr, plasma aldosterone concentration (PAC) 16.76 ng/dL, and an aldosterone/renin ratio (PAC/PRA) >30, indicative of primary hyperaldosteronism.
Following her possible diagnosis of primary hyperaldosteronism, the patient was referred to our hospital for additional testing. At the time of admission, her blood pressure was 155/111 mm Hg, height was 164.5 cm, and weight was 63.5 kg (body mass index, 23.47 kg/m2). Pretibial pitting edemas were detected, but no physical signs of Cushing syndrome were observed.
A routine blood test at the time of admission showed the following: white blood cell 6,900/µL, hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL, platelet 351,000/µL, total protein 6.9 g/dL, albumin 4.1 g/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 76 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 126 U/L, blood urea nitrogen 7.3 mg/dL, creatinine 0.71 mg/dL, sodium 141 mmol/L, potassium 2.9 mmol/L, chloride 101 mmol/L, total cholesterol 210 mg/dL, triglyceride 104 mg/dL, high density lipoprotein 50 mg/dL, low density lipoprotein 141 mg/dL, and hemoglobin A1c 5.9%. Biochemical evaluation of pheochromocytoma showed no excessive catecholamine production. The transtubular potassium concentration gradient was 12.64%, suggesting the possibility of hyperaldosteronism or hypercortisolism. PAC and PRA were 24.4 ng/dL and 0.76 ng/mL/hr, respectively, and the PAC/PRA ratio was 32.11 (). After correcting for hypokalemia, a saline loading test was performed, which revealed no suppression of the aldosterone level after saline administration, consistent with a diagnosis of hyperaldosteronism (the basal PAC 24.4 ng/dL increased to 27.3 ng/dL after a 2 L saline infusion). Dexamethasone suppression tests (DSTs) resulted in no suppression of cortisol levels (). Adrenal CT demonstrated two bilateral adrenal masses in each of the adrenal glands, including a 1.6-cm mass on the right and a 2-cm mass on the left adrenal gland (). An adrenal cortical scan using iodine-131-6β-iodomethyl-19-norcholesterol (NP-59) showed focally increased radioactive uptakes in the bilateral adrenal gland areas, with a substantially higher uptake on the left side ().
Bilateral adrenal venous sampling (AVS) with adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) stimulation () revealed inadequate right adrenal venous blood sampling (right adrenal vein cortisol level/peripheral blood cortisol level [CRt/CP] <10), whereas the cortisol concentration in the left adrenal venous blood showed successful sampling on the left side. One explanation for this difference may be that the low level of CRt was the result of suppressed cortisol secretion due to excessive cortisol secretion from the left adrenal mass. The left adrenal vein aldosterone/cortisol (ALt/CLt) ratio was lower than that of peripheral blood, implying the possibility of suppressed aldosterone secretion from this side caused by a contralateral adrenal mass which might secrete excessive aldosterone []. Four days after the first AVS, a second AVS was performed using low-dose DST but without ACTH infusion (). This time, we also measured the epinephrine levels in the both adrenal veins and peripheral blood; a large peripheral-to-adrenal gradient of epinephrine assured successful catheterization on both sides []. CLt/CP testing confirmed that the left adrenal mass was a cortisol-secreting adenoma [].
Even though those two AVS results appeared to provide clear evidence of the lateralization of functioning adrenal masses, such an independent functioning of bilateral masses was highly suspicious. We therefore concluded that sequential adrenalectomies, rather than concurrent operations, would be beneficial for making a definitive diagnosis. Moreover, the patient agreed to do sequential operations. The first procedure was a laparoscopic partial adrenalectomy of the right adrenal gland to remove the putative aldosterone-producing mass, with the goal of controlling hypertension and hypokalemia. A firm, 1.5-cm ovoid, well-circumscribed, bright yellow tumor mass lacking necrosis or hemorrhage was arising from the cortex of the adrenal gland in the pathologic specimen. Within 24 hours after surgery, the patient's blood pressure had fallen to 110/80 mm Hg without antihypertensive medication, serum potassium normalized at 4.5 mmol/L, and PAC decreased to 3 ng/dL. These large postoperative changes strongly suggested that the right adrenal adenoma was producing excessive aldosterone. By day 3, an overnight DST continued to show no cortisol suppression, indicating persistent secretion of corticosteroid from the left adrenal mass. To limit the likelihood of subclinical Cushing syndrome progressing to overt Cushing syndrome, we opted to completely remove the left adrenal gland as a means of controlling hypercortisolism. The excised portion of the adrenal mass contained an encapsulated brown solid mass.
The patient began taking prednisolone 10 mg once a day for steroid replacement 1 day after the second surgery. By 2 months she had lost 5 kg of body weight. At that time, a rapid ACTH test revealed adrenal insufficiency (). At 16 months after surgery, the patient has gradually reduced her prednisolone intake to 5 mg every other day. Her blood pressure and serum potassium levels have remained within normal ranges, without the need for additional antihypertensive medications or potassium supplements.

args:
A 4-year-old boy, who had no systemic or inherited disease, presented with a 3-week history of intermittent vomiting without diarrhea or abdominal pain. In the past year, he experienced polydipsia and polyuria. Physical examination revealed body weight 17.5 kg (50th percentile), body height 100 cm (15~50th percentile), blood pressure 230/120 mmHg, heart rate 138 /min, and decreased skin turgor. There was no focal neurological deficit, blood pressure discrepancy between upper and lower extremities, palpable mass, nor any appreciation of an abdominal thrill. Laboratory studies revealed serum Na+ 124 mmol/L, K+ 2.4 mmol/L, Cl− 87 mmol/L, Ca2+ 8.5 mg/dL, HCO3− 34.5 mEq/L, creatinine 0.41 mg/dL, albumin 3.4 g/dL, IgG 247 mg/dL, and osmolality 290 mOsm/KgH2O. Urine analysis was significant for creatinine 11.2 mg/dL, Na+ 24 mEq/L, K+ 18 mEq/L, Cl− 24 mEq/L, osmolality 232 mOsm/KgH2O, RBC 168/μL, FENa 6%, and nephrotic-range proteinuria (55 mg/m2/hour). Survey for possible glomerulonephritis demonstrated the absence of anti-streptolysin O, p-ANCA, c-ANCA, ANA, and normal immunoglobulin A, C3, and C4 levels. In addition, work-up for secondary hypertension included: free T4 1.51 (normal range 0.8–2.0 ng/dL), TSH 5.7 (normal range 0.25–5.00 μIU/mL), cortisol 40.18 (normal range 4.3–25 μg/dL), ACTH 9.32 (normal range < 46 pg/mL), renin 1745 (normal range 2–15 ng/L), aldosterone 92.6 (normal range 4–25 ng/dL), and urine vanillylmandelic acid 3.8 (normal range 1.9–9.9 g/day). Renal ultrasonography revealed hyperechoic right kidney (7.6 cm in length) and small left kidney (5.3 cm in length). Due to the presence of hyperreninemic hypertension, natriuretic-hyponatremia, hypokalemia, and nephrotic range proteinuria, HHS was highly suspected. Computed tomography angiography confirmed high-grade renal artery stenosis with hypoplasia of the left kidney (Fig. ).
In terms of management for this case, we began with volume repletion by normal saline administration. Subsequently, his blood pressure declined from 210/120 mmHg to 180/90 mmHg. Intravenous calcium channel blocker was used to treat his hypertensive emergency, while oral captopril was prescribed for RAA axis blockage after diagnosis of unilateral renal artery stenosis. The systolic blood pressure gradually declined to 150~160 mmHg on the 3rd day. Potassium supplement was infused for his profound hypokalemia and generalized muscle weakness. Due to the severity of left renal artery stenosis, he was not a candidate for angiographic intervention, and decision was made to proceed with left nephrectomy. Overall, electrolyte abnormalities such as hyponatremia and hypokalemia were corrected within 1 week after admission, and resolution of polyuria, polydipsia, proteinuria, and hypertension were achieved 2 weeks after nephrectomy (Additional file : Table S1).
What is the patient's albumin corrected delta ratio? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

To calculate the patient's "albumin corrected delta ratio," we must determine what this term implies, as it is not a standard medical formula provided in the list. The list includes "Anion Gap," "Delta Gap," and "Delta Ratio."

1.  **Identify relevant values from the patient note (at admission):**
    *   Na+ = 124 mmol/L
    *   Cl- = 87 mmol/L
    *   HCO3- = 34.5 mEq/L
    *   Albumin = 3.4 g/dL

2.  **Calculate Anion Gap (AG):**
    *   Formula: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    *   Calculation: 124 - (87 + 34.5) = 124 - 121.5 = 2.5

3.  **Calculate "Albumin Corrected Anion Gap" (Correction formula provided):**
    *   Formula: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    *   Calculation: 2.5 + 2.5 * (4 - 3.4)
    *   2.5 + 2.5 * (0.6)
    *   2.5 + 1.5 = 4.0

4.  **Calculate "Delta Gap":**
    *   Formula: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (where 12 is normal AG)
    *   Using the *Corrected* AG as the base for the delta calculation (as requested by "albumin corrected delta ratio"):
    *   Delta Gap = 4.0 - 12 = -8.0

5.  **Calculate "Delta Ratio":**
    *   Formula: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    *   Calculation: -8.0 / (24 - 34.5)
    *   -8.0 / -10.5 ≈ 0.7619

args:
A 45-year-old woman with a substantial past medical history of squamous cell cancer (SCC) was treated with laryngectomy and offered tracheostomy. She presented in the emergency department with complaints of shortness of breath (SOB). Her shortness of breath was getting progressively worse starting two days prior to admission. She denied any fevers, chills, sick contacts, nausea, abdominal pain, or diarrhea. She specified that she had actually been drinking a lot more water than regular prior to admission.
On admission, her vital signs revealed a blood pressure of 101/73 mmHg, a heart rate of 91 beats/min, a temperature of 37.4 degree Celsius, and a respiratory rate of 26 breaths/min. Her body mass index (BMI) was 18.6 kg/m2. Physical examination revealed a sick-appearing woman in severe respiratory distress using accessory muscles. She had a dry mucous membrane with poor skin turgor. The rest of the physical examination was unremarkable.
On laboratory assessment, the hemoglobin was 11.9 mg/dl, leukocyte count 3.6/mm3, serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dl, potassium 3.3 mmol/L, chloride 110 mmol/L, sodium 148 mmol/L, and bicarbonate 6 mmol/L. She had high anion gap metabolic acidosis (AGMA), (anion gap (AG) = 22). Her serum albumin on admission was 4.2 g/L, urine analysis revealed 80 mg/dl ketones, and serum lactate was 1.9 mmol/L. Furthermore, her liver enzymes revealed aspartate aminotransferase (AST) = 48 units/l, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) = 82 units/l, and alkaline phosphatase 199 units/l. Additionally, her blood glucose level was 133 mg/dl, salicylates = 6.8 mg/dl, and acetaminophen level was <2.0 ug/ml. Her blood alcohol level was normal and chest X-ray (CXR) on admission did not show any sign of acute cardiopulmonary problems.
Based on the initial evaluation, she received stoma suctioning and was placed on high O2 via a tracheostomy mask. Her history of laryngeal cancer, mild tachycardia and hypoxia raised the suspicion of pulmonary embolism, so a chest computed tomography angiography (CTA) was ordered. The chest CTA was negative for pulmonary embolism but showed mild emphysema.
In addition to that, an arterial blood gas (ABG) test was done, which revealed a high anion gap metabolic acidosis (HAGMA) as presented in Table . The patient continued to hyperventilate to compensate for the acidosis and was subsequently intubated.
Differential diagnoses including carbon monoxide poisoning, aminoglycoside toxicity, methanol, uremia, diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), alcoholic ketosis, acetaminophen toxicity, iron ingestion, lactic acidosis, ethanol toxicity, salicylate toxicity, and aspirin ingestion were investigated. However, given the patient's normal acetaminophen, salicylate, lactic acid, and ethanol levels, these etiologies were ruled out. Furthermore, serum osmolality was normal and the osmolal gap was less than 10. Our differential diagnosis was further narrowed due to the presence of elevated ketones. Given the patient's malnourished state, the most likely cause of HAGMA in this patient was starvation ketosis. The patient was started on 5% dextrose water and sodium bicarbonate drip, and tube feeds. The ABG level swiftly improved within three days and she was extubated.
After four days of intensive care unit (ICU) course, she was downgraded and later on discharged after detailed counseling from a nutritionist regarding a balanced diet and avoiding fasting. She was followed up after three weeks of discharge with significant improvement on repeat basic metabolic panel with bicarbonate of 25 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 3.8 mmol/L, and chloride 102 mmol/L.

sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
chloride: Serum chloride in mEq/L (e.g., 100)
bicarbonate: Serum bicarbonate in mEq/L (e.g., 24)

args: A 73-year-old man was evaluated this afternoon on the orthopedic surgery ward in preparation for a scheduled left total knee replacement that is set to take place tomorrow morning under general anesthesia and a femoral nerve block; the operative team anticipates a three-hour procedure with at least several days of inpatient recovery. The forthcoming joint reconstruction is the latest step in management of end-stage tricompartmental osteoarthritis that has limited his ability to stand for more than a few minutes over the past year. Three weeks ago he underwent a laparoscopic sigmoid resection for complicated diverticulitis at this same hospital, an operation that lasted just over four hours and required two postoperative nights on the surgical floor before discharge; he recovered uneventfully from that abdominal procedure. Unfortunately, one week after going home he slipped while descending his porch steps, landing awkwardly on his left leg and sustaining a minimally displaced lateral tibial plateau fracture confirmed by plain radiographs and CT scan. Because the articular surface remained congruent, the fracture was treated non-operatively with protected weight bearing in a hinged neoprene brace rather than a hard cast. Since that fall he has been largely confined to his bedroom recliner or bed, getting up only with assistance to use the bedside commode, and nursing documentation shows fewer than 50 feet of ambulation in the last four days; he notes that any attempt to move around causes immediate knee pain that he fears will worsen the fracture. He denies fevers, shakes, night sweats, purulent sputum, dysuria, or abdominal discomfort since the colectomy, and recalls no recent episodes of systemic infection. Review of systems is negative for orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, new ankle swelling, exertional chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or transient neurologic deficit. He has a long history of tobacco-related chronic bronchitis; although he quit cigarettes 10 years ago after a 45-pack-year exposure, he still uses a twice-daily long-acting muscarinic inhaler and keeps a rescue albuterol inhaler nearby for intermittent wheeze but has not required oral steroids or emergency visits this year. He has never experienced clot formation in the legs or lungs, and none of his close relatives has been treated for a thrombotic event. He recalls receiving unfractionated heparin only during prior surgeries without any unexplained drop in platelet counts, and no physician has ever advised him of an underlying blood-clotting disorder. Other chronic conditions include diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and well-managed hypertension; there is no personal history of cancer, inflammatory bowel illness, myocardial infarction, or peripheral vascular disease. Current medications are tiotropium, albuterol as needed, lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen-hydrocodone for pain, and a multivitamin; he takes no hormone therapy, steroids, anticoagulants, or anti-platelet agents other than baby aspirin, which was held yesterday per orthopedic protocol. Socially, he lives with his spouse in a single-story home, used to garden regularly, drinks a glass of wine on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. On examination he is resting supine in an adjustable hospital bed, conversant and oriented. Vital signs: temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 128/76 mm Hg, pulse 84/min and regular, respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 95 % on room air. He is slender with body mass index 23 kg/m² and breathes comfortably without accessory muscle use. Cardiovascular assessment reveals a normal S1 and S2 with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs are clear to auscultation with slightly prolonged expiratory phase but no crackles or rhonchi. Abdomen is soft and non-tender; a well-healed 5-cm infra-umbilical trocar scar is noted without hernia or incisional pain. The left lower extremity is encased in a black hinged brace locked at 30 degrees; gentle palpation along the lateral tibial plateau produces mild discomfort but there is no warmth, erythema, or swelling, and calf circumference is symmetric to the right side. Skin of both legs is warm and well perfused, without discoloration, varicosities, or pitting edema. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ bilaterally. Sensory and motor testing show intact light touch and full strength of ankle dorsiflexion, though knee extension is limited by pain. There is no peripherally inserted central catheter or other indwelling vascular device; two 20-gauge peripheral IVs placed yesterday remain patent in the forearm. Neurologic examination is nonfocal. Laboratory results obtained two hours ago reveal hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, hematocrit 39 %, platelet count 247 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.3 × 10⁹/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, glucose 108 mg/dL, AST 22 U/L, ALT 24 U/L, and a baseline coagulation profile of PT 12.3 s (INR 1.0) and aPTT 30 s. Electrocardiogram records normal sinus rhythm at 82 bpm with nonspecific ST-T wave changes unchanged from previous tracings. Upright chest radiograph shows hyperinflated lungs consistent with his chronic bronchitis but no acute infiltrate or effusion. Pulmonary function tests from last year demonstrated an FEV₁ of 62 % predicted without significant bronchodilator response. The anesthesia service has deemed his airway Mallampati class II, and he tolerates lying flat without desaturation. He has had nothing by mouth since midnight, and no mechanical or pharmacologic thromboprophylaxis has yet been initiated as the peri-operative plan is still being finalized by the surgical team in conjunction with medicine and anesthesia.

CRITICAL HINT: For Caprini, Age 41-60=1 pt, 61-74=2 pts, >=75=3 pts. Elective lower extremity arthroplasty = 5 pts. Hip, pelvis or leg fracture = 5 pts. Stroke/paralysis = 5 pts. Multiple trauma = 5 pts. Acute spinal cord injury = 5 pts. Major surgery (>45 min) = 2 pts. Central venous access = 2 pts. Bed rest > 72 hours = 2 pts. Minor surgery = 1 pt. History of major surgery (<1 month) = 1 pt. COPD = 1 pt. BMI > 25 = 1 pt.

output.reasoning: Patient is 73 years old (Caprini: 61-74 = 2 pts). Male. Elective lower extremity arthroplasty (knee replacement) is planned (5 pts). He has a 'minimally displaced lateral tibial plateau fracture' (Hip/pelvis/leg fracture = 5 pts). He had 'laparoscopic sigmoid resection' 3 weeks ago (Major surgery < 1 month = 1 pt). He has 'chronic bronchitis' (COPD = 1 pt). Bed rest/limited mobility: He has been 'largely confined to his bedroom recliner or bed' since the fall (more than 72 hours) (2 pts). BMI: 23 kg/m² (not >25). No central venous access (no PICC/CVC, just peripheral IVs). No history of stroke/paralysis. No multiple trauma. No acute spinal cord injury. No current hormone therapy. No history of VTE. No malignancy.

args: A 73-year-old man was evaluated this afternoon on the orthopedic surgery ward in preparation for a scheduled left total knee replacement that is set to take place tomorrow morning under general anesthesia and a femoral nerve block; the operative team anticipates a three-hour procedure with at least several days of inpatient recovery. The forthcoming joint reconstruction is the latest step in management of end-stage tricompartmental osteoarthritis that has limited his ability to stand for more than a few minutes over the past year. Three weeks ago he underwent a laparoscopic sigmoid resection for complicated diverticulitis at this same hospital, an operation that lasted just over four hours and required two postoperative nights on the surgical floor before discharge; he recovered uneventfully from that abdominal procedure. Unfortunately, one week after going home he slipped while descending his porch steps, landing awkwardly on his left leg and sustaining a minimally displaced lateral tibial plateau fracture confirmed by plain radiographs and CT scan. Because the articular surface remained congruent, the fracture was treated non-operatively with protected weight bearing in a hinged neoprene brace rather than a hard cast. Since that fall he has been largely confined to his bedroom recliner or bed, getting up only with assistance to use the bedside commode, and nursing documentation shows fewer than 50 feet of ambulation in the last four days; he notes that any attempt to move around causes immediate knee pain that he fears will worsen the fracture. He denies fevers, shakes, night sweats, purulent sputum, dysuria, or abdominal discomfort since the colectomy, and recalls no recent episodes of systemic infection. Review of systems is negative for orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, new ankle swelling, exertional chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or transient neurologic deficit. He has a long history of tobacco-related chronic bronchitis; although he quit cigarettes 10 years ago after a 45-pack-year exposure, he still uses a twice-daily long-acting muscarinic inhaler and keeps a rescue albuterol inhaler nearby for intermittent wheeze but has not required oral steroids or emergency visits this year. He has never experienced clot formation in the legs or lungs, and none of his close relatives has been treated for a thrombotic event. He recalls receiving unfractionated heparin only during prior surgeries without any unexplained drop in platelet counts, and no physician has ever advised him of an underlying blood-clotting disorder. Other chronic conditions include diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and well-managed hypertension; there is no personal history of cancer, inflammatory bowel illness, myocardial infarction, or peripheral vascular disease. Current medications are tiotropium, albuterol as needed, lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen-hydrocodone for pain, and a multivitamin; he takes no hormone therapy, steroids, anticoagulants, or anti-platelet agents other than baby aspirin, which was held yesterday per orthopedic protocol. Socially, he lives with his spouse in a single-story home, used to garden regularly, drinks a glass of wine on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. On examination he is resting supine in an adjustable hospital bed, conversant and oriented. Vital signs: temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 128/76 mm Hg, pulse 84/min and regular, respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 95 % on room air. He is slender with body mass index 23 kg/m² and breathes comfortably without accessory muscle use. Cardiovascular assessment reveals a normal S1 and S2 with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs are clear to auscultation with slightly prolonged expiratory phase but no crackles or rhonchi. Abdomen is soft and non-tender; a well-healed 5-cm infra-umbilical trocar scar is noted without hernia or incisional pain. The left lower extremity is encased in a black hinged brace locked at 30 degrees; gentle palpation along the lateral tibial plateau produces mild discomfort but there is no warmth, erythema, or swelling, and calf circumference is symmetric to the right side. Skin of both legs is warm and well perfused, without discoloration, varicosities, or pitting edema. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ bilaterally. Sensory and motor testing show intact light touch and full strength of ankle dorsiflexion, though knee extension is limited by pain. There is no peripherally inserted central catheter or other indwelling vascular device; two 20-gauge peripheral IVs placed yesterday remain patent in the forearm. Neurologic examination is nonfocal. Laboratory results obtained two hours ago reveal hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, hematocrit 39 %, platelet count 247 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.3 × 10⁹/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, glucose 108 mg/dL, AST 22 U/L, ALT 24 U/L, and a baseline coagulation profile of PT 12.3 s (INR 1.0) and aPTT 30 s. Electrocardiogram records normal sinus rhythm at 82 bpm with nonspecific ST-T wave changes unchanged from previous tracings. Upright chest radiograph shows hyperinflated lungs consistent with his chronic bronchitis but no acute infiltrate or effusion. Pulmonary function tests from last year demonstrated an FEV₁ of 62 % predicted without significant bronchodilator response. The anesthesia service has deemed his airway Mallampati class II, and he tolerates lying flat without desaturation. He has had nothing by mouth since midnight, and no mechanical or pharmacologic thromboprophylaxis has yet been initiated as the peri-operative plan is still being finalized by the surgical team in conjunction with medicine and anesthesia.

age_score: Age points
surgery_score: Surgery type points
mobility_score: Mobility restriction points
condition_scores: Sum of condition points (varies by condition)

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: age_61_74, elective_lower_extremity_arthroplasty, hip_pelvis_or_leg_fracture, major_surgery_less_than_1_month, copd, bed_rest_greater_than_72_hours
- Conditions ABSENT: bmi_greater_than_25, central_venous_access, stroke_or_paralysis, multiple_trauma, acute_spinal_cord_injury
- Demographics: Age=73, Sex=male

CALCULATOR SPECIFIC HINTS:
CRITICAL HINT: For Caprini, Age 41-60=1 pt, 61-74=2 pts, >=75=3 pts. Elective lower extremity arthroplasty = 5 pts. Hip, pelvis or leg fracture = 5 pts. Stroke/paralysis = 5 pts. Multiple trauma = 5 pts. Acute spinal cord injury = 5 pts. Major surgery (>45 min) = 2 pts. Central venous access = 2 pts. Bed rest > 72 hours = 2 pts. Minor surgery = 1 pt. History of major surgery (<1 month) = 1 pt. COPD = 1 pt. BMI > 25 = 1 pt.

args: A 48-year-old Vietnamese-American woman presented to the academic internal-medicine clinic at 06:35 for a “true baseline fasting draw” after an employer-sponsored biometric fair two weeks earlier produced a capillary glucose reading in the low 130s that the screener advised her to confirm formally before she embarked on any diet overhaul, herbal detox kits, or renewed gym membership. She works full-time as a home-based technical translator for a multinational software firm; deadlines across three time zones mean that she sits at her computer from 04:00 until mid-afternoon, frequently breaking focus only long enough to microwave sticky rice and pork sausage left over from the previous evening, refill a 24-ounce sweetened milk-coffee tumbler, or raid a desk drawer stocked with sesame biscuits shipped by relatives. Over the past six years her weight has crept from 60 kg to 79 kg at an unchanged height of 160 cm; she now purchases relaxed-fit blouses two sizes larger and concedes that climbing a single flight of townhouse stairs leaves her mildly winded. She voids once or twice nightly but attributes that to pre-bed green-tea habit; she denies relentless thirst, has had no abrupt weight loss, and reports no blurred vision, cutaneous boils, or distal numbness. When conference calls run past 11:00 she notices tremulousness, irritability, and a hollow epigastric sensation that disappear within minutes of sipping a canned sugary soda kept in her mini-fridge. Past medical history lists only seasonal allergic conjunctivitis managed with otc ketotifen drops; she has never been prescribed glucocorticoids, atypical antipsychotics, antiretrovirals, metformin, or any other glucose-lowering agent, and she has not trialed low-carbohydrate apps, time-restricted eating, or nutraceutical powders despite colleagues’ recommendations. Surgical history is limited to a laparoscopic appendectomy at age 29. Both parents developed adult-onset diabetes in their early 60s—her mother managed on tablets, her father later requiring basal insulin—and a younger brother recently received “pre-diabetes” counseling from his primary physician. The patient has never smoked tobacco, drinks a single glass of rosé on Friday movie nights, and denies illicit drug use. Aside from occasional weekend tai-chi in the park she engages in no structured exercise, citing ankle discomfort after a childhood fracture and pressures of caregiving for an elderly aunt who lives with her. Review of systems is otherwise negative for exertional chest pain, paroxysmal dyspnea, orthostatic light-headedness, depressive mood, heat or cold intolerance, or change in glove or shoe size. On arrival she sat quietly scrolling through overnight software tickets, appearing well groomed and in no acute distress. After ten minutes of seated rest nursing staff recorded blood pressure 124/80 mm Hg in the right arm, regular pulse 78 beats/min, respiratory rate 14/min, oral temperature 36.6 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Weight on the calibrated scale was 79.4 kg, yielding a body-mass index of 31.0 kg/m²; waist circumference measured 100 cm at the midpoint between the lowest rib and iliac crest. Physical examination revealed a faint velvety hyperpigmented band in the posterior cervical folds and several 2-mm papillomas in the axillae, but no thyromegaly or carotid bruits. Cardiac auscultation produced crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs; distal pulses were 2+ bilaterally, and no peripheral edema was present. Lungs were clear. The abdomen was soft with moderate central adiposity and no palpable organomegaly. Neurologic screen showed intact cranial nerves, symmetric 2+ deep-tendon reflexes, preserved 10-g monofilament sensation on plantar surfaces, and vibration perception equal at great toes and medial malleoli. Funduscopic inspection demonstrated sharp disc margins with no microaneurysms or cotton-wool spots. She confirmed that after a final evening meal of pho at 18:15 yesterday she consumed only plain water, deliberately skipping dawn coffee. Venipuncture was completed at 06:43; tubes were placed on wet ice and reached the hospital’s CAP-accredited core laboratory within 15 minutes. The hexokinase analyzer reported a fasting plasma glucose of 128 mg/dL, and a simultaneously obtained serum insulin quantified by electrochemiluminescent immunoassay measured 110 pmol/L. From that same specimen additional assays showed hemoglobin A1c 5.9 %, serum creatinine 0.84 mg/dL with estimated glomerular filtration rate >90 mL/min/1.73 m², sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 13 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 32 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 28 U/L, total cholesterol 212 mg/dL, low-density lipoprotein 136 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein 45 mg/dL, and triglycerides 184 mg/dL. Thyroid-stimulating hormone read 1.8 µIU/mL, morning cortisol 12 µg/dL, and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein 2.7 mg/L. Urinalysis revealed specific gravity 1.013, trace protein, negative ketones, and no glucosuria; spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio was 15 mg/g. A baseline 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained prior to phlebotomy demonstrated normal sinus rhythm at 74 beats/min without conduction delay or ischemic changes. The patient reiterated that she has not modified diet, exercise, or medications since the workplace screen and seeks evidence-based nutritional strategies compatible with Vietnamese cuisine, joint-sparing activity options feasible during breaks between translation projects, and clarification regarding when pharmacologic intervention would be considered if lifestyle measures alone do not suffice.

args:
A 52-year-old female presented to our outpatient department with increasing exertional dyspnea and chest pain for the past two months. Her medical history was positive for ischemic heart disease, diabetes mellitus, and hypertension. Five years ago, she underwent cardiac catheterization followed by a percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) with an implantation of five stents. Her ongoing medication included subcutaneous insulin, antianginals (diltiazem, nitroglycerine, ranolazine, TriCardin®), clopidogrel, and an antacid.
On presentation, the patient was afebrile with a blood pressure 162/82 mmHg, heart rate 92/min, respiratory rate 24/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Due to the worsening of her angina symptoms, she was admitted for elective coronary angiography to assess the status of previously placed stents. On admission, her labs revealed poor diabetic control (hemoglobin A1C 9.3%) and dyslipidemia (total cholesterol 214 mg/dl, triglycerides 203 mg/dl, low-density lipoprotein 165 mg/dl, very low density lipoprotein 41 mg/dl, and high-density lipoprotein 45 mg/dl). Cardiac enzymes were not raised (creatine kinase-myocardial band 19.4 U/l and troponin-I 0.44 ng/ml). Blood counts, serum electrolytes, renal and liver function tests, viral markers, and coagulation profile were within normal limits. Her previous transthoracic echocardiography was significant for hypokinesis of the inferoseptal segment of the left ventricle and an ejection fraction of 50%.
An elective coronary angiography revealed critical ISR in the distal right coronary artery (RCA) stent (Figure ). Furthermore, critical stenosis (80%) at the mid-distal junction of the left anterior descending (LAD) artery and 40%-50% stenosis of the proximal ostium were also seen (Figure ). Patent stents were present in the proximal RCA and mid-LAD artery segments. The patient refused to undergo coronary artery bypass grafting. Therefore, a two-staged elective PCI involving the RCA and LAD artery was planned to treat the ISR. Stage I PCI to RCA was performed after pre-dilating the vessel. RCA ostium was intubated with guiding catheter JL3.5-6Fr. The lesion was crossed using the Hi-Torque Balance Middleweight Universal II Guidewire (Abbott Cardiovascular, Santa Clara, CA). The area was then dilated with a Mini-Trek balloon (2 x 20 mm; Abbott Cardiovascular). Finally, a Xience Xpedition® everolimus-eluting cobalt-chromium coronary stent (2.5 x 48 mm; Abbott Cardiovascular) was deployed at 17 atmospheres (ATMs) while overlapping the previous stent. The entire length of the stent was post-dilated with an NC-Trek balloon (2.75 x 15 mm; Abbott Cardiovascular). An excellent final angiographic result with well-deployed stents and thrombolysis in myocardial infarction (TIMI) III distal blood flow was achieved.
Stage II PCI to the LAD artery was performed two days later. Left mainstem ostium was intubated with guiding catheter JL3.5-6Fr. The stenosed area was crossed using a Hi-Torque Balance Middleweight Universal II Guidewire. The mid-LAD artery lesion was directly stented with a Xience PrimeTM everolimus-eluting cobalt-chromium coronary stent (2.5 x 18 mm; Abbott Cardiovascular) and deployed at 13 ATMs overlapping the previously placed mid-LAD artery stent. Next, the overlapped segment and the previously placed stent were post-dilated with the same stent balloon at 15 ATMs. Ostial proximal LAD artery lesion was directly stented with another Xience PrimeTM coronary stent (2.5 x 18 mm) and deployed at 13 ATMs overlapping the first stents. Then the entire length of the stents was post-dilated with an NC-TREK balloon (2.5 x 15 mm) at 17 ATMs. An excellent final angiographic result with well-deployed stents and TIMI III distal blood flow was achieved in this case as well (Figure ). The patient was counseled regarding risk factors and diet modification. Finally, the patient was discharged on antianginal and anti-hypertensive medication.

total_cholesterol: Total cholesterol in mg/dL (e.g., 200)
hdl: HDL cholesterol in mg/dL (e.g., 50)
triglycerides: Triglycerides in mg/dL (e.g., 150)

    - For scoring systems with mutually exclusive severity levels (e.g., mild vs moderate/severe liver disease), mark ONLY the single most appropriate severity level as True.

    - If a required numeric value or field is entirely unmentioned and cannot be inferred, OMIT it from the 'extracted' dictionary completely instead of writing 'missing' or null.

    - "missing": ["list of values truly not determinable from the note"]

args: A 70-year-old woman who resides in her own bungalow and still gardens most mornings was brought to the emergency department by her husband shortly after lunchtime because of forty-eight hours of feverish chills, a productive cough yielding yellow-green sputum, and new right-sided chest discomfort that worsened whenever she tried to lie flat; she deliberately skipped her usual morning prescriptions—carvedilol, low-dose bumetanide, and nadolol prescribed for portal-related variceal prophylaxis—so that, in her words, “the numbers won’t be watered down.” She had eaten only dry toast at dawn and drank no coffee, alcohol, or acetaminophen before arrival, and she used neither her home incentive spirometer nor the salbutamol metered-dose inhaler she keeps for occasional winter wheeze. Past medical background includes longstanding congestive pump dysfunction following an anthracycline-based chemotherapy course in early adulthood for Hodgkin disease (she has had no malignant recurrence and has routine oncology surveillance that remains clear) and biopsy-confirmed non-alcoholic steatohepatitis with compensated portal hypertension; her last hepatology clinic visit three months earlier documented normal synthetic markers, occasional esophageal varices managed endoscopically, and no ascites. She has never required renal replacement therapy and her primary-care records show an estimated glomerular filtration rate consistently above 60 mL/min; she denies any history of stroke, transient neurologic events, carotid surgery, or focal deficits. Immunizations are current, including both pneumococcal formulations and this season’s influenza injection, and she quit a fifteen-pack-year cigarette habit nearly two decades ago. On ambulance roll-in she sat upright, conversed in full sentences, correctly recited the date and the names of her three grandchildren, and followed multi-step commands without hesitation. Skin was warm and lightly diaphoretic, sclerae were anicteric, and nail beds were pink with brisk capillary refill. First-pass, untreated vital signs obtained while she breathed ambient air at this sea-level facility were: oral temperature 38.05 °C, regular radial pulse 75 beats per minute confirmed on telemetry, non-invasive right-arm blood pressure 103/61 mm Hg verified manually, respiratory rate counted over a full minute at 20 breaths per minute with mild intercostal effort but no accessory muscle use, and fingertip pulse oximetry varying between 93 % and 94 %. Jugular venous contour was mildly elevated at the angle of Louis, similar to her cardiology baseline, and trace bilateral ankle edema was noted. Cardiac auscultation revealed an S3 gallop already documented in previous notes as well as crisp S1 and S2 without new murmurs or rubs. Pulmonary examination demonstrated dullness to percussion and absent breath sounds over the dependent half of the right posterior hemithorax with coarse inspiratory crackles and egophony just above that zone; the left lung transmitted vesicular airflow throughout. Abdomen was soft, non-tender, and mildly distended; a smooth liver edge was palpable 2 cm below the costal margin and no splenomegaly was appreciated. A focused neurologic screen showed equal, reactive pupils, intact extra-ocular movements, fluent speech, and 5/5 strength in all extremities. Capillary glucose checked immediately prior to phlebotomy read 93 mg/dL. Within ten minutes of bay entry—before any fluids, antimicrobials, vasodilators, or supplemental oxygen—peripheral venous blood and a right radial arterial sample were drawn. The basic metabolic panel returned sodium 140 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 104 mEq/L, bicarbonate 23 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 30 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. A complete blood count demonstrated leukocytes 15.1 × 10⁹/L with 88 % segmented neutrophils, hematocrit 33 %, hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, and platelets 189 × 10⁹/L. Arterial blood gas on room air revealed pH 7.36, PaCO₂ 37 mm Hg, PaO₂ 86 mm Hg, and calculated bicarbonate 21 mEq/L, consistent with mild hypoxemia and near-physiologic acid–base balance. Serum lactate was 1.7 mmol/L, and high-sensitivity troponin I fell below the analytical threshold. A portable postero-anterior chest radiograph obtained before any therapy demonstrated a dense consolidation within the posterior segment of the right lower lobe accompanied by a crescentic fluid collection layering laterally that blunted the ipsilateral costophrenic angle; cardiac silhouette matched prior films and there was cephalization of pulmonary vasculature similar to previous images. Electrocardiography displayed sinus rhythm at 74 beats per minute with low-voltage QRS complexes and nonspecific ST-T changes unchanged from tracings in her cardiology file. Two sets of peripheral blood cultures, a nurse-assisted expectorated sputum sample for Gram stain and culture, urinary antigens for Streptococcus pneumoniae and Legionella pneumophila, and a multiplex respiratory viral PCR panel were dispatched to the laboratory prior to any antibiotic, diuretic, vasodilator, bronchodilator, corticosteroid, or oxygen order. Up to the moment this narrative is completed she has received only the minimal heparinized saline needed to maintain patency of one 18-gauge peripheral catheter, ensuring that every physiologic observation, laboratory value, and imaging finding reported above represents her untreated state at presentation.

output.reasoning: Age: 70 years (+70 pts). Sex: Female (0 pts). Nursing home resident: No (0 pts). Neoplastic disease: Past history of Hodgkin disease (no active malignancy) (0 pts). Liver disease: Biopsy-confirmed non-alcoholic steatohepatitis with compensated portal hypertension (+20 pts). Heart failure: Congestive pump dysfunction/S3 gallop/elevated JVP (+10 pts). Cerebrovascular disease: Denies history of stroke/TIA/carotid surgery (0 pts). Renal disease: Normal creatinine (0.9), GFR > 60 (0 pts). Altered mental status: Alert, oriented, follows commands (0 pts). Respiratory rate: 20 bpm (0 pts). Systolic blood pressure: 103 mmHg (0 pts). Temperature: 38.05 °C (0 pts). Pulse: 75 bpm (0 pts). pH: 7.36 (0 pts). BUN: 30 mg/dL (+20 pts). Sodium: 140 mEq/L (0 pts). Glucose: 93 mg/dL (0 pts). Hematocrit: 33% (+10 pts). PaO2: 86 mmHg (0 pts). Oxygen saturation: 93-94% (assumed >90%) (0 pts). Pleural effusion: Present on CXR (+10 pts).

args:
An 11-year-old Indian boy from Bijapur district of Karnataka State was referred to the paediatric unit of the Al Ameen Medical College Hospital with history of short stature, dysmorphic facies and failure to thrive for evaluation. Patient was good weight normal full term delivery at home. Maternal and Neonatal History was uneventful. Immunization was adequate with normal developmental milestones and intelligence. Parents denied any history of trauma or fractures. There was no history of frequent respiratory tract infections, snoring or tuberculosis contact. He was the youngest of three siblings born of second degree consanguineous marriage. Other siblings and parents are normal. On admission child’s weight was 28 kg. His standing height was 126 cm and upper segment was 65.5 cm and lower segment was 60.5 cm. Head circumference was 51 cm. He had mid facial hypoplasia with proptosed eyes.
He had frontal and bilateral parietal bossing .The sagittal, coronal and lambdoid sutures were separated and anterior and posterior fontanels were widely open. Examination of the mouth revealed a narrow high arched grooved palate. The teeth were hypoplastic with lateral open bite and multiple missing teeth. Bilaterally temporomandibular joints were retracted with slight deviated nasal septum to left. Scoliosis was seen. His digits were short, spoon shaped, stubby with joint laxity, metaphyseal widening and dystrophic nails. A sandal gap deformity was seen. There was no significant pallor, hepatomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Sexual maturity rating showed pre-adolescent stage. Laboratory investigations such as complete blood count, serum calcium, serum inorganic phosphate and alkaline phosphatase were normal.
The radiological findings were significant showing diffuse skeletal hyperostosis with sparing of medullary cavity. Skull X-rays showed widely separated cranial sutures and widely open anterior and posterior fontanel. The paranasal sinuses were non-pneumatised with angle of the mandible being obtuse. Acro-osteolysis was seen. The bone age was equal to his chronological age. Audiometry showed mild to moderate bilateral conductive hearing loss.
Fundus examination showed bilateral vitreous hyperplasia with lots of fibrous bands.
He was diagnosed as Pycnodysostosis based on characteristic clinical and radiological findings.
The parents of the child were offered an option for CTSK gene mutation testing for confirmation of diagnosis of pycnodysostosis, however they refused for same in view of financial constraints as the same testing is not available currently in our country (India).
We suggest for active screening of all children diagnosed with pycnodysostosis for hearing defects and ophthalmological evaluation as similar evaluation was not seen previously in published literature.

weight_kg: Patient weight in kilograms (e.g., 80.0)
height_cm: Patient height in centimeters (e.g., 175.0)

args: An 81-year-old Asian man presented to our department complaining of fever since the preceding day. The patient had been under treatment for the previous 3 years for chronic heart failure and chronic renal failure. He did not have a history of malignancy, diabetes mellitus, cytotoxic therapy, or corticosteroid use, and no foreign bodies had been implanted. The patient’s family history was unremarkable. Physical examination revealed a heart rate of 101 beats/min, blood pressure of 87/48 mmHg, respiratory rate of 20 breaths/min, temperature of 37.0 °C, and oxygen saturation of 87% on room air. He had no caries or periodontitis. Results of respiratory, cardiac, and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Limb examination demonstrated mild edema of both legs. Abdominal computed tomography (CT) showed a low-density mass in the right iliopsoas muscle indicative of an iliopsoas abscess (Fig. ). The patient’s white blood cell count, C-reactive protein (CRP), and procalcitonin levels were 19,400/μl, 13.35 mg/dl, and 3.950 ng/ml, respectively. Serum blood urea nitrogen and creatinine were elevated at 77.2 mg/dl and 3.69 mg/dl, respectively. A CT-guided percutaneous drainage of the psoas abscess was performed, and an indwelling catheter was placed. Gram staining of the drained fluid revealed many neutrophils and Gram-positive streptococci. On the basis of these findings, a presumptive diagnosis of iliopsoas abscess caused by Streptococcus species was made, and treatment with ampicillin/sulbactam (ABPC/SBT) 1.5 g, administered intravenously every 8 h, was initiated. Results of organism cultures of the abscess and blood were positive, and P. micra was identified by using the API ZYM system (Sysmex-bioMérieux Co. Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), with the organism exhibiting susceptibility to penicillin G, ampicillin, clindamycin, and meropenem. By day 7, the patient’s white blood cell count normalized. By day 20, his CRP level was decreased to 0.35 mg/dl. Therefore, the pigtail catheter was removed. The patient died of peritonitis due to colon diverticulum perforation after 5 weeks of treatment. An autopsy revealed no right iliopsoas abscess at the time of death.

CRITICAL HINT: 'absence_of_cough' or 'no_cough' MUST be marked TRUE if cough is explicitly denied OR if cough is not mentioned at all. Only mark FALSE if the patient actually has a cough. For FeverPAIN, 'attend_rapidly' is TRUE if seen within 3 days.

output.reasoning: Fever: Patient explicitly complained of fever (Present). Purulence: No mention of tonsillar exudate or purulence (Absent). Attend rapidly: Patient presented one day after symptom onset, which is within the 3-day threshold (Present). Inflamed tonsils: No physical examination of the tonsils mentioned (Absent). No cough: Cough is not mentioned in the patient note; per calculator instructions, this is marked as present.

args: An 81-year-old Asian man presented to our department complaining of fever since the preceding day. The patient had been under treatment for the previous 3 years for chronic heart failure and chronic renal failure. He did not have a history of malignancy, diabetes mellitus, cytotoxic therapy, or corticosteroid use, and no foreign bodies had been implanted. The patient’s family history was unremarkable. Physical examination revealed a heart rate of 101 beats/min, blood pressure of 87/48 mmHg, respiratory rate of 20 breaths/min, temperature of 37.0 °C, and oxygen saturation of 87% on room air. He had no caries or periodontitis. Results of respiratory, cardiac, and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Limb examination demonstrated mild edema of both legs. Abdominal computed tomography (CT) showed a low-density mass in the right iliopsoas muscle indicative of an iliopsoas abscess (Fig. ). The patient’s white blood cell count, C-reactive protein (CRP), and procalcitonin levels were 19,400/μl, 13.35 mg/dl, and 3.950 ng/ml, respectively. Serum blood urea nitrogen and creatinine were elevated at 77.2 mg/dl and 3.69 mg/dl, respectively. A CT-guided percutaneous drainage of the psoas abscess was performed, and an indwelling catheter was placed. Gram staining of the drained fluid revealed many neutrophils and Gram-positive streptococci. On the basis of these findings, a presumptive diagnosis of iliopsoas abscess caused by Streptococcus species was made, and treatment with ampicillin/sulbactam (ABPC/SBT) 1.5 g, administered intravenously every 8 h, was initiated. Results of organism cultures of the abscess and blood were positive, and P. micra was identified by using the API ZYM system (Sysmex-bioMérieux Co. Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), with the organism exhibiting susceptibility to penicillin G, ampicillin, clindamycin, and meropenem. By day 7, the patient’s white blood cell count normalized. By day 20, his CRP level was decreased to 0.35 mg/dl. Therefore, the pigtail catheter was removed. The patient died of peritonitis due to colon diverticulum perforation after 5 weeks of treatment. An autopsy revealed no right iliopsoas abscess at the time of death.

fever: Fever in last 24 hours (+1)
purulence: Pharyngeal/tonsillar exudate (+1)
attend_rapidly: Attend within 3 days of symptom onset (+1)
inflamed_tonsils: Severely inflamed tonsils (+1)
no_cough_coryza: No cough or coryza (+1)

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: fever, attend_rapidly, no_cough
- Conditions ABSENT: purulence, inflamed_tonsils
- Demographics: Age=81, Sex=male

CRITICAL HINT: 'absence_of_cough' or 'no_cough' MUST be marked TRUE if cough is explicitly denied OR if cough is not mentioned at all. Only mark FALSE if the patient actually has a cough. For FeverPAIN, 'attend_rapidly' is TRUE if seen within 3 days.

args: A 55-year-old African American male with past medical history of hypertension, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia was admitted for fever and difficulty breathing for several days. His associated symptoms were chest tightness without any pain. A diagnosis of possible community-acquired coronavirus was made. His vital signs were a temperature of 101.8 °F, heart rate of 100 beats/min, blood pressure of 163/101 mmHg, respiratory rate of 16 breaths/min, and pulse oximetry of 96% on room air. Physical examination was remarkable for decreased breath sounds without any rales or rhonchi. The patient was placed on nasal cannula. Tests for influenza and respiratory syncytial virus by PCR were negative. Initial chest X-ray (CXR) revealed right upper lobe and bilateral lower lobe infiltrates (Figure ), without hilar fullness, congestion, or Kerley lines. Complete blood count and complete metabolic panel were remarkable for sodium of 133 mmol/L, blood glucose of 230 mg/dL, and a creatinine of 1.51 mg/dL. Troponin was elevated at 0.307 ng/ml and it slowly trended down afterwards. He was transferred to our facility on hospital day 2. The initial workup at our facility was unremarkable with a creatinine of 1.2 mg/dL and negative troponin level. Hemoglobin A1c was 8.7%. His inflammatory markers were markedly elevated, with a C-reactive protein (CRP) of 34.86 mg/dL and a D-dimer of 462. A CXR showed bilateral infiltrate (Figure ). He was placed on airborne and contact precautions. He was started on oral hydroxychloroquine and intravenous ceftriaxone with the eventual addition of doxycycline for a suspected superimposed bacterial pneumonia. His home medication of Losartan was held during hospitalization. Repeated nasal COVID PCR swabs on hospital day 2 and 3 were negative. Blood and urine cultures were negative as well. Electrocardiogram revealed left bundle branch block. Echocardiogram suggested by cardiologist revealed a reduced ejection fraction of 35%. The patient was placed on 4-5 liters of oxygen via nasal cannula throughout the hospital course and his symptoms were well controlled. Subsequently, he was able to wean off the supplemental oxygen. His CRP and D-dimer trended down. He was discharged with instructions on self-isolation.

output.reasoning: The PERC rule assesses clinical probability of Pulmonary Embolism (PE). Criteria are: Age < 50 (Patient is 55, condition absent); Pulse < 100 (Patient is 100, condition absent); SaO2 >= 95% (Patient is 96%, condition present); No unilateral leg swelling (Not mentioned, assumed absent); No hemoptysis (Not mentioned, assumed absent); No recent surgery/trauma (Not mentioned, assumed absent); No prior PE/DVT (Not mentioned, assumed absent); No hormone use (Not mentioned, assumed absent). Patient is male.

args: A 22-year-old woman with no past medical history, no chronic medication use, and up-to-date routine vaccinations was transported to the critical care receiving area twenty minutes after being pulled from a freshwater quarry where she had been swimming with classmates during a midsummer outing; witnesses reported that she inhaled water during a brief struggle to stay afloat and was unconscious for “less than a minute” before lifeguards extricated her. Paramedics arrived eight minutes after the call and documented that on ambient air her pulse oximetry registered in the low seventies, prompting placement of a tightly-fitting Venturi mask delivering 50 % inspired oxygen; during the ten-minute ride she received no intravenous fluids, no bronchodilators, no antimicrobials, no vasopressors, and no opioids, and her airway remained patent without adjuncts or positive-pressure support. Upon stretcher entry to the resuscitation bay—and before any hospital-initiated therapy—vital signs were captured: tympanic temperature 38.2 °C, regular but rapid pulse 118 beats per minute confirmed on three-lead telemetry, observed respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute with moderate accessory muscle recruitment, and a non-invasive blood pressure that cycled consistently at 122⁄70 mm Hg on both arms. She opened her eyes to spoken name, answered most orientation questions but was unsure of the exact date, and followed two-step commands, establishing a Glasgow Coma Score of 14 without sedatives, ethanol, or head trauma. Skin examination revealed flushed cheeks, warm extremities, brisk capillary refill, and no peripheral cyanosis or edema. Jugular venous pulsations were not distended at 30 degrees, carotid upstrokes were sharp, and distal pulses were bounding. Cardiac auscultation disclosed a loud S1, physiologic split S2, and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Breath sounds were coarse bilaterally with diffuse crackles more pronounced over the mid-zones, and percussion was mildly dull at the bases, consistent with fluid-laden alveoli rather than pleural effusion. The abdomen was flat, nondistended, and nontender with active bowel sounds; no hepatosplenomegaly was appreciated. There was no costovertebral angle tenderness. Extremities displayed full range of motion without crepitus or deformity, and neurologic screen showed equal pupils, symmetric facial movement, and 5⁄5 strength in all limbs. Seven minutes after arrival, while she remained on the unchanged 50 % oxygen source, a right radial arterial sample produced a pH of 7.43, PaCO₂ 37 mm Hg, and an analyzer-calculated alveolar-to-arterial oxygen gradient of 480 mm Hg, indicative of severe impairment of oxygen transfer despite near-normal ventilation. Capnography showed an end-tidal CO₂ of 36 mm Hg, correlating with the arterial value. Simultaneous point-of-care basic metabolic panel revealed sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, glucose 102 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 12 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.8 mg/dL—values matching those from a collegiate sports physical filed six months earlier, suggesting intact renal function without acute injury. A rapid complete blood count returned a white-cell count of 17 050 cells/mm³ with neutrophil predominance, hematocrit 42 %, hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, and platelets 244 000/µL. Venous lactate measured 2.4 mmol/L. Portable anteroposterior chest radiography obtained before any diuretics, antibiotics, or positive-pressure ventilation demonstrated bilateral patchy infiltrates with ill-defined air bronchograms, no pleural fluid, and a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. Point-of-care lung ultrasound corroborated diffuse B-lines throughout both fields without discrete consolidation or pneumothorax. Focused transthoracic echocardiography revealed a small, vigorously contracting left ventricle with an estimated ejection fraction over 65 % and an inferior vena cava measuring 1.4 cm that collapsed completely with inspiration, findings consonant with euvolemia rather than fluid overload. Twelve-lead electrocardiography printed during the arterial draw showed sinus tachycardia at 116 beats per minute, PR interval 150 ms, QRS duration 88 ms, QTc 420 ms, and no acute ischemic changes. A bladder scan indicated 90 mL of urine, and a straight catheter placed for accurate output monitoring drained 25 mL of clear yellow urine over the subsequent ten minutes. Importantly, from the moment of extraction from the water through handoff to the intensive-care team—a span of roughly forty minutes—the patient had not received intravenous crystalloid, antimicrobial agents, bronchodilators, corticosteroids, analgesics, vasopressors, or diuretics; the only intervention in place remained the Venturi mask delivering 50 % oxygen. Thus, the contemporaneous physiologic and laboratory measurements—temperature 38.2 °C, systolic pressure 122 mm Hg, diastolic pressure 70 mm Hg, heart rate 118 beats per minute, respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute, arterial pH 7.43, serum sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, hematocrit 42 %, white blood cell count 17 050 /mm³, inspired oxygen fraction 50 %, and an alveolar-to-arterial gradient of 480 mm Hg—represent her untreated presentation for suspected aspiration-related acute lung injury manifesting as moderate fever, leukocytosis, and significant hypoxemia.

CRITICAL HINT: For APACHE II oxygenation, use PaO2 to score if FiO2 < 0.50. Only use A-a gradient if FiO2 >= 0.50. Do not hallucinate A-a gradient if PaCO2 is missing. GCS score points = 15 minus actual GCS.

Chronic health status: No past medical history, no organ insufficiency (0 pts).

args: A 29-year-old woman who delivered her first child sixteen hours earlier at a rural birthing center was transferred by ground ambulance to the emergency department for evaluation of worsening confusion, intractable nausea, and markedly diminished urine output noted since the early hours of the morning; her midwife reported that the second stage of labor had lasted more than three hours, during which she received multiple one-liter bags of a dextrose-containing hypotonic solution mixed with a high-dose oxytocin infusion because the center had temporarily run out of isotonic saline, and that postpartum blood loss was estimated at 450 mL without need for uterotonics or transfusion. The patient had no prior renal disease, hypertension, or diabetes, but a week-old prenatal panel documented a serum creatinine of 0.7 mg/dL and a plasma sodium of 138 mEq/L. Her only medications were a single prenatal chewable vitamin and two 200-mg ibuprofen tablets taken four hours after delivery for perineal pain; she denied herbal diuretics, contrast studies, aminoglycosides, or recreational drugs. According to the midwife, over the past eight hours the patient had insisted on frequent sips from a one-liter water bottle to “quench terrible thirst,” yet the indwelling Foley catheter—placed to monitor postpartum diuresis—had yielded less than 40 mL of very dark amber urine during that entire span. En route to the hospital no intravenous line was started and no medications were administered. On stretcher arrival, before any emergent interventions, vital signs were: oral temperature 37.2 °C, blood pressure 96/52 mm Hg (prenatal records averaged 118/72), heart rate 112 beats/min regular, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 97 % on room air. She appeared somnolent but arousable to voice, answering simple questions sluggishly and repeatedly asking where her infant was. Height was 165 cm, weight 68 kg—approximately 2 kg above her booking weight after delivery. Examination revealed puffy eyelids, tense non-pitting edema over the hands, and moist axillae; oral mucosa were nonetheless tacky, and her tongue was furrowed. Jugular venous pulsations were scarcely visible at 45°, capillary refill was four seconds, and the skin of the upper sternum tented briefly on release. Cardiac auscultation disclosed a rapid, regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lung fields were clear to percussion and auscultation. The uterus was firm at the level of the umbilicus with scant lochia rubra; the abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender except for mild suprapubic discomfort. Bilateral costovertebral angles were non-tender, and there was no peripheral cyanosis. Neurologic screening found equal 3-mm pupils that reacted sluggishly to light, intact extraocular movements, absent nystagmus, but generalized 4/5 strength attributed to fatigue; no focal deficits or asterixis were elicited, and deep tendon reflexes were 1+. A handheld bladder scanner estimated 35 mL residual volume, suggesting reduced kidney output rather than outlet obstruction. Two 18-gauge antecubital lines were placed within ten minutes, after which venous blood and a freshly aspirated Foley urine sample—28 mL of concentrated dark tea-colored fluid—were delivered to the laboratory; at that moment no crystalloid, diuretic, vasopressor, hypertonic saline, or nephrotoxic agent had yet been given. Stat chemistries returned a plasma sodium of 100 mEq/L, potassium 3.9 mEq/L, chloride 70 mEq/L, bicarbonate 20 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 38 mg/dL, glucose 94 mg/dL, calcium 8.4 mg/dL, phosphorus 3.3 mg/dL, and an abrupt increase in serum creatinine to 2.33 mg/dL. Measured serum osmolality was 215 mOsm/kg. A venous blood gas on ambient air showed pH 7.31 with PaCO₂ 32 mm Hg and base deficit −7 mEq/L. Complete blood count revealed hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, hematocrit 34 %, leukocytes 13.2 ×10⁹/L with 82 % neutrophils, and platelets 265 ×10⁹/L. Urinalysis on the contemporaneous specimen demonstrated specific gravity 1.022, trace protein, moderate heme with few red cells on microscopy, several muddy brown granular casts, and occasional renal tubular epithelial cells; nitrite and leukocyte esterase were negative. Simultaneous spot urine chemistry measured a sodium concentration of 66 mEq/L and a creatinine concentration of 44 mg/dL; urine osmolality, still pending at dictation, was anticipated to exceed the markedly depressed plasma tonicity. Point-of-care renal ultrasonography revealed kidneys of normal length with preserved corticomedullary differentiation and no pelvicalyceal dilatation; Doppler interrogation yielded arterial resistive indices of 0.76 bilaterally, and the inferior vena cava measured 0.8 cm in quiet expiration with near-total inspiratory collapse, suggesting depleted effective circulating volume rather than true fluid overload. Focused cardiac ultrasound displayed a small hyperdynamic left ventricle without pericardial effusion. A portable postpartum anteroposterior chest radiograph obtained within fifteen minutes showed clear lung fields and a normal cardiac silhouette, and a twelve-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia at 114 beats/min with subtly flattened anterior T waves but no ST-segment deviations or conduction delays. During the subsequent forty-minute observation window—still prior to any intravenous fluid bolus, hypertonic therapy, oxytocin discontinuation, vasopressor support, antibiotic, diuretic, or additional analgesic—the Foley catheter yielded only an extra 6 mL of highly concentrated urine, confirming persistent oliguria in the context of profound hypo-osmolar hyponatremia and a sudden rise in serum creatinine of uncertain precise mechanism; obstetric, nephrology, and critical-care services were paged concurrently for immediate bedside consultation while strict intake–output charting and continuous cardiac monitoring were instituted.

urine_sodium: Urine sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 20)
serum_sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
urine_creatinine: Urine creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 100)
serum_creatinine: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.5)
