args: A 43-year-old woman was admitted for the evaluation of a 25 kg weight loss resulting in severe protein malnutrition due to intentional calorie restriction (300 Kcal/day) for six months. She had no previous medical or surgical history and was not receiving medications. The patient believed that she developed an “autoimmune disease” that improved with self-induced calorie restriction. On presentation, the patient weighed 30.1 kg (BMI 11), temperature 98.6°F, heart rate 75 bpm, and blood pressure 105/68 mmHg. She was markedly cachectic with temporal wasting, scaphoid abdomen, and normal liver span without stigmata of cirrhosis. She was normally oriented and asterixis was absent. Her admission laboratory testing revealed AST (2895 U/L), ALT (1868 U/L), alkaline phosphatase (460 U/L), bilirubin (2.9 mg/dL), albumin (3.7 g/dL), INR (1.9), amylase 188, lipase 322, CPK 535, normal TSH, negative viral hepatitis and autoimmune serologies, ceruloplasmin (17 mg/dL), and elevated 24-hour urine copper (117 mcg). Abdominal ultrasound revealed a heterogeneous liver with patent vessels. A slit lamp exam failed to reveal Kayser-Fleischer rings. Psychiatry consultation diagnosed a somatic delusional disorder. She did not fit the DSM-IV TR diagnostic criteria for anorexia nervosa. Five days after the initiation of oral refeeding and intravenous vitamin K, laboratory testing revealed AST (221 U/L), ALT (539 U/L), alkaline phosphatase (373 U/L), bilirubin (1.3 mg/dL), and INR (1.43). During her initial refeeding period, she had occasional episodes of bradycardia, hypothermia, hypotension, and EKG changes with inverted T waves and prolonged QTc. On hospital day 9, her transaminases were improving but still 5–7 times the upper limit of normal and a liver biopsy was performed. The liver biopsy revealed mild non-specific acute hepatitis with intrasinusoidal and mild centrilobular perivenular fibrosis, focal necroinflammatory foci, acidophil bodies (long arrow), and increased mitotic figures in the hepatic lobules (short arrow) (). Dry copper weight was 53.8 ug/g. ATP7B PCR gene testing was negative for Wilson's disease.

args: A 43-year-old woman was admitted for the evaluation of a 25 kg weight loss resulting in severe protein malnutrition due to intentional calorie restriction (300 Kcal/day) for six months. She had no previous medical or surgical history and was not receiving medications. The patient believed that she developed an “autoimmune disease” that improved with self-induced calorie restriction. On presentation, the patient weighed 30.1 kg (BMI 11), temperature 98.6°F, heart rate 75 bpm, and blood pressure 105/68 mmHg. She was markedly cachectic with temporal wasting, scaphoid abdomen, and normal liver span without stigmata of cirrhosis. She was normally oriented and asterixis was absent. Her admission laboratory testing revealed AST (2895 U/L), ALT (1868 U/L), alkaline phosphatase (460 U/L), bilirubin (2.9 mg/dL), albumin (3.7 g/dL), INR (1.9), amylase 188, lipase 322, CPK 535, normal TSH, negative viral hepatitis and autoimmune serologies, ceruloplasmin (17 mg/dL), and elevated 24-hour urine copper (117 mcg). Abdominal ultrasound revealed a heterogeneous liver with patent vessels. A slit lamp exam failed to reveal Kayser-Fleischer rings. Psychiatry consultation diagnosed a somatic delusional disorder. She did not fit the DSM-IV TR diagnostic criteria for anorexia nervosa. Five days after the initiation of oral refeeding and intravenous vitamin K, laboratory testing revealed AST (221 U/L), ALT (539 U/L), alkaline phosphatase (373 U/L), bilirubin (1.3 mg/dL), and INR (1.43). During her initial refeeding period, she had occasional episodes of bradycardia, hypothermia, hypotension, and EKG changes with inverted T waves and prolonged QTc. On hospital day 9, her transaminases were improving but still 5–7 times the upper limit of normal and a liver biopsy was performed. The liver biopsy revealed mild non-specific acute hepatitis with intrasinusoidal and mild centrilobular perivenular fibrosis, focal necroinflammatory foci, acidophil bodies (long arrow), and increased mitotic figures in the hepatic lobules (short arrow) (). Dry copper weight was 53.8 ug/g. ATP7B PCR gene testing was negative for Wilson's disease.

prior_pe_dvt: Previous PE or DVT (+1.5 points)
hemoptysis: Hemoptysis (+1 point)
malignancy: Active cancer (treatment within 6 months or palliative) (+1 point)

- Demographics: Age=43, Sex=female

args: A 64-year-old man with insulin-treated type 2 diabetes, stage 1 chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.0 mg/dL four months earlier), remote open cholecystectomy for gallstone pancreatitis, and a twenty-pack-year smoking history was rushed to the emergency department from a rural urgent-care office where he had collapsed in the waiting room after two days of crampy right-upper-quadrant pain, intermittent chills, and progressive lethargy. The transferring clinician reported that, moments before the syncopal event, the patient was febrile to 39.2 °C, diaphoretic, and had vomited green-tinged fluid; no intravenous fluids, antiemetics, antibiotics, sedatives, or vasopressors were given at that facility because transport was immediately arranged. During the thirty-minute ambulance ride he remained un-intubated, breathing spontaneously through a snug non-rebreather mask flushed with 15 L/min oxygen—documented in the run sheet as approximating an inspired fraction of 0.61—and pulse oximetry hovered between 87 % and 89 % without manual ventilation. No continuous positive-pressure device or advanced airway was employed, and the crew started only a single 18-gauge peripheral line for lactated Ringer’s to keep vein open at 30 mL/hr. On entry to the resuscitation bay—before any hospital-initiated therapy—a right radial arterial catheter was placed on the first attempt, yielding an immediate blood gas that revealed a pH of 7.30, PaCO₂ 35 mm Hg, bicarbonate 17 mEq/L, and a PaO₂ of 73 mm Hg while the mask remained in place delivering the unchanged 61 % oxygen. Simultaneous non-invasive vitals confirmed a blood pressure of 97/58 mm Hg, heart rate 122 beats per minute in sinus rhythm, respiratory rate 30 with shallow excursions, and a rectal temperature of 38.8 °C. The patient lay supine but restless, pupils 3 mm and sluggishly reactive; he opened his eyes only to noxious trapezius squeeze, uttered incomprehensible groans, and withdrew from nail-bed pain without localizing, generating a Glasgow Coma Score of 7. Skin examination demonstrated warm flushed face contrasted by cool, mildly mottled lower extremities; sclerae were faintly icteric, and dry mucous membranes were noted. Neck veins were flat at 30°, and capillary refill at the fingertips exceeded four seconds. The chest rose symmetrically around the mask, breath sounds were coarse bilaterally with scattered inspiratory crackles but no wheezes, and there was no accessory-muscle retraction. Cardiac auscultation disclosed a rapid regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. The abdomen was distended with marked tenderness and guarding in the right upper quadrant, positive Murphy sign, and a palpable, mildly enlarged liver edge; bowel sounds were hypoactive. There were no stigmata of chronic liver failure such as ascites or spider angiomata. Extremities showed no edema, petechiae, or track marks; peripheral pulses were thready but palpable. A 16-French Foley catheter inserted during the primary survey immediately drained 50 mL of dark amber urine, and paperwork from the patient’s home health aide, who records outputs for diabetic management, documented a cumulative 1 080 mL voided over the previous twenty-four hours. Laboratory studies drawn concurrently with the arterial sample—before any crystalloid bolus, antimicrobials, analgesics, or vasopressor agents—returned: white blood cell count 22.9 × 10³/µL with 18 % bands, hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL, hematocrit 36 %, and platelet count 114 000 /µL. Comprehensive metabolic panel showed sodium 130 mmol/L, potassium 4.7 mmol/L, chloride 98 mmol/L, an anion gap of 16, glucose 212 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 46 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.58 mg/dL, elevated from baseline. Hepatic indices revealed total bilirubin 1.55 mg/dL (direct fraction predominating), aspartate aminotransferase 92 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 88 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 284 U/L, and an international normalized ratio of 1.3. Serum lactate measured 5.0 mmol/L, high-sensitivity troponin was 0.02 ng/mL, and C-reactive protein exceeded 200 mg/L. Bedside sonography performed within the first five minutes displayed a dilated common bile duct measuring 11 mm with echogenic intraluminal shadowing near the pancreatic head, intrahepatic ductal prominence, and a thick-walled gallbladder remnant without stones; inferior vena cava diameter was 1.1 cm collapsing more than 50 % on inspiration, suggesting low intravascular filling. A focused lung ultrasound revealed diffuse B-lines in dependent zones and small pleural effusions bilaterally. Portable anteroposterior chest radiograph obtained while he remained on the non-rebreather demonstrated mild bilateral patchy opacities without focal consolidation or pneumoperitoneum, and abdominal upright film showed sentinel loops in the epigastrium but no free air under the diaphragm. Twelve-lead electrocardiogram recorded sinus tachycardia at 125 beats per minute with nonspecific ST-T changes and a corrected QT interval of 452 ms. Up to the exact moment those physiologic and biochemical data were captured, the patient had received neither intravenous antibiotics nor additional fluids beyond the minimal keep-open rate, had not been intubated or placed on any form of positive-pressure ventilation, remained borderline hypotensive at 97/58 mm Hg without vasoactive infusion, displayed mild thrombocytopenia, modest hyperbilirubinemia, early renal impairment with only slightly reduced urine production, and suffered significant hypoxemia with an arterial oxygen tension of 73 mm Hg while breathing spontaneously through a high-concentration facemask.

Simultaneous non-invasive vitals confirmed a blood pressure of 97/58 mm Hg, heart rate 122 beats per minute in sinus rhythm, respiratory rate 30 with shallow excursions, and a rectal temperature of 38.8 °C. The patient lay supine but restless, pupils 3 mm and sluggishly reactive; he opened his eyes only to noxious trapezius squeeze, uttered incomprehensible groans, and withdrew from nail-bed pain without localizing, generating a Glasgow Coma Score of 7. Skin examination demonstrated warm flushed face contrasted by cool, mildly mottled lower extremities; sclerae were faintly icteric, and dry mucous membranes were noted. Cardiac auscultation disclosed a rapid regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. There were no stigmata of chronic liver failure such as ascites or spider angiomata. Extremities showed no edema, petechiae, or track marks; peripheral pulses were thready but palpable. A 16-French Foley catheter inserted during the primary survey immediately drained 50 mL of dark amber urine, and paperwork from the patient’s home health aide, who records outputs for diabetic management, documented a cumulative 1 080 mL voided over the previous twenty-four hours. Laboratory studies drawn concurrently with the arterial sample—before any crystalloid bolus, antimicrobials, analgesics, or vasopressor agents—returned: white blood cell count 22.9 × 10³/µL with 18 % bands, hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL, hematocrit 36 %, and platelet count 114 000 /µL. Comprehensive metabolic panel showed sodium 130 mmol/L, potassium 4.7 mmol/L, chloride 98 mmol/L, an anion gap of 16, glucose 212 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 46 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.58 mg/dL, elevated from baseline. Hepatic indices revealed total bilirubin 1.55 mg/dL (direct fraction predominating), aspartate aminotransferase 92 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 88 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 284 U/L, and an international normalized ratio of 1.3. args: A 52-year-old woman presented to the infusion center sick bay late this morning after oncology nursing staff noted that her left lower leg looked subtly fuller than the right while they were preparing to start her third cycle of carboplatin–paclitaxel. She completed an interval cytoreductive laparotomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and omentectomy five weeks ago for stage III high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, has a tunneled chest port placed for chemotherapy, and continues to work half-days as an accountant, driving herself to and from the office and walking several blocks between the parking garage and her building without limitation. She denies spending prolonged time in bed other than routine nighttime sleep and states she performed light housework, grocery shopping, and a 20-minute stationary bicycle session within the past two days. Yesterday evening she first perceived a faint sense of tightness behind the left knee while climbing stairs but attributed it to deconditioning. This morning, while donning compression socks recommended by her oncologist for long chair sessions during infusions, she realized the elastic cuff felt more snug on the left. She has not noticed redness, warmth, skin streaking, itching, insect bite, trauma, muscle cramp, or joint pain, and reports no fever, chills, night sweats, unintentional weight change, rash, or urinary complaints. She denies dyspnea, pleuritic chest discomfort, palpitations, hemoptysis, light-headedness, or syncope. Her medical history is otherwise notable only for diet-controlled hyperlipidemia and remote appendectomy at age 17; she has never experienced venous thrombosis or pulmonary embolism, nor has any first-degree relative. Current medications include atorvastatin 20 mg nightly, ondansetron as needed for nausea, and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system placed three years ago; she takes no estrogen therapy, corticosteroids, or hormonal supplements. She quit smoking 12 years ago after a 10-pack-year exposure, consumes wine socially on weekends, and denies illicit drug use. On examination prior to any anticoagulant administration she appeared comfortable seated upright, conversing in complete sentences. Vital signs were temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 126/74 mm Hg, heart rate 82 beats/min regular, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 99 % on room air. Cardiac auscultation revealed a regular S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lung fields were clear bilaterally. Abdominal inspection showed a well-healed midline incision with no tenderness or organomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated mild fullness limited to the mid-calf on the left: a flexible tape placed 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity measured 37.8 cm on the symptomatic side and 35.9 cm on the right, a disparity of 1.9 cm. Circumference at the mid-thigh (51 cm bilaterally) and around the malleoli (25 cm bilaterally) was symmetrical. Skin color was pink and comparable to the contralateral limb, with no hyperpigmentation, brawny changes, or serpiginous superficial venous channels. The surface felt neither warmer nor cooler than the right when assessed with the dorsum of the examiner’s hand. Gentle palpation along the femoral triangle, adductor canal, popliteal fossa, posterior calf groove, and distal posterior tibial track elicited no focal tenderness and the patient described only a diffuse awareness of pressure when the gastrocnemius was compressed. There was no palpable cord, and ankle dorsiflexion with the knee extended did not reproduce pain. No cellulitis, ruptured Baker’s cyst, musculotendinous tear, or lymphedema was apparent to account for the unilateral calf contour change, and the patient’s recent abdominal-pelvic oncologic surgery together with ongoing cytotoxic therapy heighten concern for an intraluminal obstruction within the deep venous pathway of the left lower limb despite the modest girth difference and absence of classic tenderness. Duplex ultrasonography of the iliac, femoral, popliteal, and calf veins has therefore been requested urgently while plans for chemotherapy administration are temporarily deferred pending imaging results.

args:
A 66-year-old man (height 172 cm, weight 92 kg) presented with diabetic nephropathy for planned living donor kidney transplantation. He suffered from CAD, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, insulin dependent diabetes mellitus II and a chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. He had undergone coronary artery bypass surgery in a different hospital 7 months before (left internal mammary artery (LIMA) to left anterior descending (LAD) and saphenous vein graft to the ramus diagonalis). The ramus circumflexus (RCX) was not accessible to revascularization during bypass operation according to the documents of the different hospital. The LAD was supplied with a drug-eluting stent three years before. The RCX was supplied with a bare-metal stent one year before and, because of an in-stent restenosis, 5 month before with a Genous stent in a different hospital. Additionally another Genous stent was placed distally to the previous stent area.
The patients’ wife was intended to serve as the organ donor. She had a history of severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with intercurrent infectious exacerbation, why the transplantation had been called off twice, yet. Prior to the planned date of transplantation she was in good condition.On account of the medical history of the recipient and according to the pre-transplantation guidelines of our institution with regard to high-risk patients a cardiac catheterization was carried out 5 days before transplantation. Here an in-stent restenosis of the proximal ramus circumflexus, which had already been supplied with drug-eluting and bare metal stents two times before, was observed (see Figure ). After intensive discussion between the cardiologist, the surgeon and the anaesthesiologist the following therapeutic plan was made: the stenosis was dilated with a 3.0/15 mm drug-eluting balloon (Inc. Braun, Germany) followed by anticoagulation with tirofiban and aspirin until surgery. The post-interventional angiography showed a very good result after PTCA (see Figure ). Definitive stenting was planned after full recovery from the kidney transplantation.
Five days later the planned kidney transplantation was carried out. Medication including aspirin and tirofiban was continued up to the day of operation. In the operating room (OR) induction with sufentanil 0.33 μg kg-1, propofol 1.6 mg kg-1 and rocuronium 0.33 mg kg-1 was performed. Anaesthesia was maintained with sevofluoran 1.6 Vol%. During whole operation mean arterial pressure was above 70 mmHg with a heart rate between 50 and 60 bpm. The patient received norepinephrine with a maximum dose of 0.11 μg kg-1 min. Intraoperative blood loss was 800 ml, and the lowest level of haemoglobin was 12.2 g/l. The patient received 2500 ml of crystalloid solution. After successful kidney transplantation he was extubated in the OR and transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU).One hour after admission to the intensive care unit (ICU) the patient developed pectanginous symptoms. The ECG at 10 minutes after clinical presentation of angina revealed no significant abnormalities. The cardiac troponin I was initial negative and 13.9 pg/ml after 4 hours with a peak level of >50.0 pg/ml 7 hours after admission. Cardiac catheterization, carried out 20 minutes after the first complaint of angina by the patient presented a sub-acute in-stent thrombosis of the RCX stent which had been dilated preoperatively with a drug-eluting balloon (see Figure ). After PTCA with a 2.0 mm balloon the implantation of a 3.0/24 mm drug-eluting stent (Medtronic Endeavor Resolute, Medtronic, Germany) followed. The angiography again showed a good result (see Figure ). Postinterventionally, after consultation of the surgeons, anticoagulation with tirofiban over 3 days was started followed by prasugrel for 12 months and aspirin. During anticoagulation only minor skin bleeding appeared. 12 days after kidney transplantation the patient could be dismissed home in a good state and is still well after three years.

args:
The patient was an 18-year-old man who had been complaining of exertional cough and dyspnea for 4 months. He had no medical and familial history. He graduated from junior high school but did not go to high school or work. He presented to our hospital after visiting a general practitioner for epigastric discomfort and nausea. At that time, urinalysis revealed massive proteinuria. On the next day, he was referred and admitted to our hospital for a thorough examination. He had no history of hypertension, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection, drug abuse, ureteral reflux, premature birth, or any other diseases.
On admission, physical examination revealed a body temperature of 36.7°C, a body weight of 62 kg, and a height of 168 cm. Blood pressure was 157/85 mm Hg. There were evident leg edema, bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy, and no skin lesions. The laboratory findings revealed decreased serum total protein (5.9 g/dL) and albumin (2.6 g/dL); normal serum creatinine (SCr, 0.9 mg/dL) and eGFR (95 mL/min/1.73 m2); elevated LDL cholesterol (224 mg/dL); and slightly elevated serum calcium (10.6 mg/dL). BNP level was remarkably elevated (1,850 pg/mL). Urinalysis revealed a subnephrotic level of urinary protein (3.0 g/day), microhematuria (5–9/HPF), no leukocyturia, and no bacteria. The soluble interleukin 2 receptor level was elevated (2,905 U/mL); serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) level was normal (14.2 U/L); renin activity was remarkably elevated (39 ng/mL/h); plasma aldosterone level was within normal limits (184 pg/mL). The urinary N-acetyl-glucosaminidase level was remarkably elevated (85.6 IU/L). Hepatitis B virus surface antigen, hepatitis C virus antibody, and HIV antigen and antibody were all negative. Immunoglobulins, complements, rheumatoid factor, antinuclear antibody, and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies were all negative. Tuberculin test was negative.
The chest X-ray showed enhanced pulmonary congestion and no detectable hilar lymphadenopathy. In contrast, chest computed tomography (CT) revealed hilar lymphadenopathy without fibrosis in any lung field. Furthermore, abdominal CT revealed bilateral normal-sized kidneys and bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy. Gallium-67 scintigraphy revealed uptakes in the pulmonary hilum, mediastinum, bilateral submaxillary glands, bilateral parotid glands, and bilateral inguinal regions. Echocardiography detected severe cardiac dysfunction (ejection fraction 25%), diffuse akinesis of the ventricular wall, and atrial septal defect, but it did not detect thinning of the interventricular septum base. However, electrocardiogram-gated cardiac CT revealed thinning of the interventricular septum base, and this finding was supported by a cine magnetic resonance imaging. Cardiac dysfunction and thinning of the ventricular septum base were supportive findings of cardiac sarcoidosis.
On the first admission, the patient received treatment for heart failure and hypertension, including temocapril hydrochloride, calcium channel antagonist, carvedilol, spironolactone, and carperitide. Thereafter, the urinary protein level decreased to <1 g/day, and the heart failure and hypertension improved. On the 30th hospital day, the patient was discharged. The inguinal biopsy that was performed as outpatient procedure detected granulomas; therefore, the diagnosis was sarcoidosis. On the 69th day, the SCr level gradually increased to 2.00 mg/dL (eGFR 39.4 mL/min/1.73 m2) and the urinary protein-to-creatinine ratio increased to 3.2 g/gCr. At this time, the serum albumin and LDL cholesterol levels were 3.7 g/dL and 134 mg/dL, respectively. Therefore, he was readmitted for renal investigation.
Subsequent renal biopsy revealed the presence of 32 glomeruli, 8 of which showed global sclerosis. Mesangial proliferation, crescent formation, or epithelial change was not observed. In 4 glomeruli, segmental sclerotic lesions were found near the vascular pole and were classified as the perihilar variant of FSGS [] (Fig. ). The diameter of the Bowman's space of the FSGS lesions was maintained at approximately 200–230 μm. Conversely, several ischemic glomerular changes were found, including atrophic Bowman's capsule, glomerular tuft shrinking, and global sclerosis (Fig. ). Tubulointerstitial fibrosis and inflammatory cell infiltration accounted for 70% of the renal parenchyma. Mononuclear cell infiltration, giant cells, and noncaseous granulomas in the tubulointerstitium were noted. The majority of the granulomas involved small arteries and/or arterioles, many of which had intimal wall thickness, reduplicated internal elastic lamina, and narrow lumen (Fig. ). Many of the epithelioid cells of these granulomas were arranged concentrically around the artery/arteriole and formed a multilayer structure.
Immunostaining by an enzyme-labeled antibody method revealed focal and segmental deposits of IgM (1+) in the glomerular lesions. In the tubulointerstitium, no significant deposits of IgG, IgA, IgM, C1q, and C3 were found. Immunohistochemistry revealed CD68-positive macrophage accumulation in the tubulointerstitial granulomas but not in the glomeruli. Electron microscopy did not detect any glomeruli. Based on these findings, the pathological diagnosis was tubulointerstitial granulomatous angiocentric vasculitis with FSGS.
He received oral prednisolone 30 mg/day after the renal biopsy revealed renal sarcoidosis. At 27 days after the initiation of steroid therapy, the SCr level decreased to 1.60 mg/dL (eGFR 50.2 mL/min/1.73 m2), with a simultaneous decrease in the urinary protein-to-creatinine ratio to <0.5 g/gCr. Thereafter, oral prednisolone was tapered to 25 mg/day, and the patient was discharged on the 38th day of the second hospital stay (the 108th day).
After discharge, his blood pressure stabilized. Later in the clinical course, nifedipine could be reduced, and temocapril was discontinued. Echocardiography showed recovery of cardiac function (ejection fraction 55.4%). Oral prednisolone was gradually tapered to 2 mg/day after 8 years of steroid initiation, and the SCr level decreased to 1.22 mg/dL (eGFR 60.6 mL/min/1.73 m2) after 8.8 years of the initial presentation. The urinary protein-to-creatinine ratio was maintained at <0.3 g/gCr. No other immunosuppressants were used. No relapse in the clinical course has occurred at the time of writing this manuscript.

args: A 15-year-old girl, who was suffered from ESRD since she was thirteen, was admitted to our hospital with dyspnea, chest pain, cellulitis, and osteomyelitis. Two months after admission, the patient had an operation with the diagnosis of septic knee arthritis. In the sonography performed 1 month later, renal stones were observed. The patient underwent HD and PD. Two days before transplantation, the BUN and Cr levels were 73 and 5.9 mg/dl, respectively. Before the operation, the patient had received methylprednisolone and vitamin B6. She underwent kidney transplantation from an independent living donor. Two days after transplantation, BUN and Cr levels were 24 and 1.4 mg/dl, respectively. The patient developed dyspnea and severe ascites and the urine volume decreased. Five days after transplantation, BUN and Cr levels increased (66 and 4 mg/dl, respectively). The leukocyte count also increased. Furthermore, renal transplant scintigraphy showed mild increased perfusion with reduced function of transplanted kidney with the evidence of dilated ureter (ureterovesical junction stenosis). Therefore, core needle biopsy of the transplanted kidney was done. Histopathologic view of the biopsy showed nonsignificant changes in glomeruli, moderate infiltration of lymphocytes accompanying edema in the interstitium, and marked Ox crystal deposition in some tubules and endarteritis consistent with type II acute T-cell-mediated rejection. Two months after transplantation, she developed avascular necrosis of the head of the femur. Finally, 9 months after transplantation, the nephrectomy was conducted due to graft failure. Renal transplant nephrectomy revealed excessive CaOx deposition. Subsequently, the peritoneal catheter was implanted which was functional for 1 month. At the end, she was again admitted with hyperkalemia and the patient died [Figures and ].

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 65)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
creatinine_mg_dl: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.2)

from typing import Any
def simulate_medical_value(patient_note: str, question: str) -> Any:
    """Calculate a medical value from a patient note.

    Calculate a medical value from a patient note.
    
    Given a patient note and a calculation question, reason step by step:
    1. Carefully read the patient note to extract all relevant clinical values
    2. Identify what medical calculation/score is needed
    3. Apply the appropriate formula from the reference below
    4. Show your calculation step by step, checking your arithmetic
    
    FORMULAS (use these exact formulas):
    - APACHE II Score: Acute Physiology Score + Age Points + Chronic Health Points
    - Adjusted Body Weight: ABW = IBW + 0.4 * (actual_weight - IBW)
    - Albumin Corrected Anion Gap: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    - Anion Gap: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    - Body Mass Index (BMI): BMI = weight_kg / (height_m)^2
    - Body Surface Area (Mosteller): BSA = sqrt((height_cm * weight_kg) / 3600)
    - CHA2DS2-VASc Score: Sum of: CHF(+1), HTN(+1), Age>=75(+2)/65-74(+1), DM(+1), Stroke/TIA(+2), Vascular(+1), Female(+1)
    - CKD-EPI GFR (2021): 142 * min(Cr/kappa,1)^alpha * max(Cr/kappa,1)^-1.200 * 0.9938^age * sex_coef
    - CURB-65 Score: C + U + R + B + 65 (0-5 points)
    - Calcium Correction for Hypoalbuminemia: Corrected Ca = Ca + 0.8 * (4 - albumin)
    - Caprini VTE Score: Sum of weighted risk factors
    - Centor Score (McIsaac): Sum with age modifier
    - Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI): Sum of weighted comorbidity points + age points
    - Child-Pugh Score: Sum of points for bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
    - Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault): CrCl = ((140 - age) * adjusted_weight * gender_coef) / (Cr * 72)
    - Date of Conception: gestational_weeks - 2 (conception ~2 weeks after LMP)
    - Delta Gap: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (where 12 is normal AG)
    - Delta Ratio: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    - Estimated Due Date: 40 - current_gestational_age
    - FIB-4 Index: FIB-4 = (Age * AST) / (Platelets * sqrt(ALT))
    - FeverPAIN Score: Fever + Purulence + Attend rapidly + Inflamed tonsils + No cough/coryza (0-5)
    - Fractional Excretion of Sodium (FENa): FENa = (UNa * SCr) / (SNa * UCr) * 100
    - Framingham Risk Score: Complex multivariate equation based on age, sex, lipids, BP, smoking
    - Free Water Deficit: FWD = TBW% * weight * (Na/140 - 1)
    - Gestational Age: weeks + days/7
    - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): Eye + Verbal + Motor (3-15)
    - Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS): Sum of clinical and lab criteria (0-23)
    - HAS-BLED Score: H-A-S-B-L-E-D criteria (0-9 points)
    - HEART Score: Sum of History + ECG + Age + Risk factors + Troponin (each 0-2 points)
    - HOMA-IR: HOMA-IR = (Glucose * Insulin) / 405
    - Ideal Body Weight (Devine): Male: IBW = 50 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60), Female: IBW = 45.5 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60)
    - LDL Calculated (Friedewald): LDL = Total Cholesterol - HDL - (Triglycerides / 5)
    - MDRD GFR: 175 * (Cr)^-1.154 * (age)^-0.203 * sex_coef
    - MELD-Na Score: MELD = 10 * (0.957*ln(Cr) + 0.378*ln(Bili) + 1.120*ln(INR) + 0.643)
    - Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1 Rule): First 10kg: 4ml/kg/hr, Next 10kg: 2ml/kg/hr, Remaining: 1ml/kg/hr
    - Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): MAP = (2 * DBP + SBP) / 3
    - Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME): Sum of (opioid dose * conversion factor)
    - PERC Rule: 8 criteria; PERC negative if all 0
    - PSI/PORT Score: Demographics + Comorbidities + Physical Exam + Labs
    - QTc (Bazett): QTc = QT / sqrt(RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Framingham): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Fridericia): QTc = QT / RR^(1/3) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Hodges): QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)
    - QTc (Rautaharju): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI): Sum of 6 risk factors (0-6 points)
    - SIRS Criteria: Count of criteria met (>= 2 = SIRS positive)
    - SOFA Score: Sum of 6 organ system scores (0-24)
    - Serum Osmolality: Osm = 2*Na + BUN/2.8 + Glucose/18
    - Sodium Correction for Hyperglycemia: Corrected Na = Na + 0.024 * (glucose - 100)
    - Steroid Conversion: Prednisone equivalent = dose * conversion_factor
    - Target Weight: Target Weight = target_bmi * (height_m)^2
    - Wells' Criteria for DVT: Sum of clinical criteria points
    - Wells' Criteria for PE: Sum of weighted criteria
    
    Important:
    - Be precise with extracted values. Double-check your arithmetic.
    - For sex/gender: "man"/"male"/"he" → male, "woman"/"female"/"she" → female.
    - Convert units as needed (lbs→kg: ×0.453592, feet/inches→cm: (ft×12+in)×2.54).
    - For scoring systems: carefully check each criterion against the patient note.
      Look for conditions implied by medications or clinical findings, not just
      explicitly named conditions.
    
    CRITICAL FORMATTING INSTRUCTIONS FOR THE FINAL ANSWER:
    - If the answer is a numeric value, return ONLY the raw number (e.g., 6.25). Do NOT include units, commas, or symbols like "%" or "mmHg".
    - If the answer is a date, return ONLY the exact date string in MM/DD/YYYY format (e.g., 06/09/2002). Do NOT wrap it in extra quotes.
    - If the answer is a tuple of weeks and days, return the exact Python tuple of strings (e.g., ('33 weeks', '5 days')).

args:
Question 1: What is the diagnosis when a patient develops a rash as shown in Figure A and B?
Answer 1: A 67-year-old male with a history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, atrial fibrillation, and right total knee arthroplasty presented with a progressive rash in the lower extremities. Two weeks earlier, he had right knee debridement for septic arthritis caused by methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus for which cefazolin was initiated for 6 weeks. Upon presentation, his vital signs were within normal limits. Examination demonstrated clusters of palpable, purplish purpurae with associated intact and ruptured blisters scattered over anterior, medial, and lateral aspects of the legs bilaterally below the knees. Laboratory investigations revealed urea of 17 mg/dL, creatinine of 0.89 mg/dL, ESR of 117 mm/h, CRP of 88.14 mg/L, WBC count of 10.6 k/μL without peripheral eosinophilia, hemoglobin of 8.1 g/dL, and platelet count of 312 k/μL. Urinalysis was significant for 2+ proteinuria and microhematuria. Investigations for hepatitis B, hepatitis C, ANA, ANCA, and cryoglobulins were negative. Biopsy of the rash showed perivascular papillary dermal infiltration of polymorphonuclear leukocytes and eosinophils with prominent foci of leukocytoclasis and extravasated RBCs along with a spongiotic intraepidermal and subepidermal bullous change. Based on the clinical presentation, characteristic rash and biopsy results, he was diagnosed with leukocytoclastic vasculitis (LCV) secondary to cefazolin with cutaneous and renal involvement.
Corticosteroids were deferred in light of an ongoing infection and normal renal functions in this patient. Cefazolin was switched to vancomycin and colchicine was commenced with resultant improvement in the rash. When encountered with drug-induced leukocytoclastic vasculitis, a thorough review of medications should be made to identify the implicated drug and should be discontinued promptly. In the absence of overt organ damage, discontinuation of the offending drug is usually sufficient and immunosuppressive therapy may not be necessary as demonstrated by this case.
Most cases of leukocytoclastic vasculitis are idiopathic; however, drugs, bacterial, or viral infections, sepsis, connective tissue diseases, and underlying malignancies are associated in the rest . Histopathologically, the hallmark of diagnosis is perivascular polymorphonuclear infiltration, leukocytoclasia (degranulation and nuclear fragmentation, also known as karyorrhexis) of the neutrophils, and fibrinoid necrosis .

output.reasoning: The patient is a 67-year-old male (Age 65-74 = 1 point). History of hypertension (HTN = 1 point). History of diabetes mellitus (DM = 1 point). History of atrial fibrillation (AF = 2 points). No history of stroke, TIA, or thromboembolism. No history of vascular disease (right total knee arthroplasty is orthopedic, not vascular). No history of congestive heart failure.

Question 1: What is the diagnosis when a patient develops a rash as shown in Figure A and B?
Answer 1: A 67-year-old male with a history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, atrial fibrillation, and right total knee arthroplasty presented with a progressive rash in the lower extremities. Two weeks earlier, he had right knee debridement for septic arthritis caused by methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus for which cefazolin was initiated for 6 weeks. Upon presentation, his vital signs were within normal limits. Examination demonstrated clusters of palpable, purplish purpurae with associated intact and ruptured blisters scattered over anterior, medial, and lateral aspects of the legs bilaterally below the knees. Laboratory investigations revealed urea of 17 mg/dL, creatinine of 0.89 mg/dL, ESR of 117 mm/h, CRP of 88.14 mg/L, WBC count of 10.6 k/μL without peripheral eosinophilia, hemoglobin of 8.1 g/dL, and platelet count of 312 k/μL. Urinalysis was significant for 2+ proteinuria and microhematuria. Investigations for hepatitis B, hepatitis C, ANA, ANCA, and cryoglobulins were negative. Biopsy of the rash showed perivascular papillary dermal infiltration of polymorphonuclear leukocytes and eosinophils with prominent foci of leukocytoclasis and extravasated RBCs along with a spongiotic intraepidermal and subepidermal bullous change. Based on the clinical presentation, characteristic rash and biopsy results, he was diagnosed with leukocytoclastic vasculitis (LCV) secondary to cefazolin with cutaneous and renal involvement.
Corticosteroids were deferred in light of an ongoing infection and normal renal functions in this patient. Cefazolin was switched to vancomycin and colchicine was commenced with resultant improvement in the rash. When encountered with drug-induced leukocytoclastic vasculitis, a thorough review of medications should be made to identify the implicated drug and should be discontinued promptly. In the absence of overt organ damage, discontinuation of the offending drug is usually sufficient and immunosuppressive therapy may not be necessary as demonstrated by this case.
Most cases of leukocytoclastic vasculitis are idiopathic; however, drugs, bacterial, or viral infections, sepsis, connective tissue diseases, and underlying malignancies are associated in the rest . Histopathologically, the hallmark of diagnosis is perivascular polymorphonuclear infiltration, leukocytoclasia (degranulation and nuclear fragmentation, also known as karyorrhexis) of the neutrophils, and fibrinoid necrosis .

- Conditions ABSENT: chf, stroke_tia, vascular_disease
- Demographics: Age=67, Sex=male

args:
A 55-year-old man with a history of type II diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obstructive sleep apnea, and depression developed pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath, and had a syncopal episode during a car trip from Texas to Cleveland. In the emergency room (ER), his blood pressure was 120/83 mmHg, pulse 119/min, respiratory rate 22/min, temperature 36.6oC, and oxygen (O2) saturation was 93% on room air. Physical exam was notable for an obese male in no acute distress with tachycardia, regular rhythm, clear lungs, shallow breaths without accessory muscle usage, and mild epigastric tenderness. His electrocardiogram (ECG) demonstrated sinus tachycardia at 117 bpm with a deep S wave in lead I, a small Q wave and inverted T wave in lead III (Figure ).
Initial laboratory testing was significant for serum sodium 132 mmol/L, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL, ProBNP 3188pg/mL, and initial troponin 0.12, peaking at 0.2 on hospital day two. Computed tomography (CT) chest showed bilateral submassive pulmonary emboli in multiple branches of the right and left upper and lower lobe pulmonary arteries, with no indication of a saddle embolus or main pulmonary artery thrombus (Figures -).
The patient was started on an intravenous (IV) heparin drip and thrombolytics were deferred due to perceived hemodynamic stability and timing of presentation. While in the ER, he became more tachycardic with heart rate 130-140 bpm, and developed jugular venous distention and acute hypoxemia requiring 3 liters (L) nasal cannula to maintain normal O2 saturation. He was admitted to the medical intensive care unit (MICU) due to possible signs of acute decompensation.
In the MICU, ultrasound revealed a right popliteal deep vein thrombosis (DVT); an inferior vena cava filter was considered, but the patient was considered hemodynamically stable and placement of a filter was deferred. Over the second and third hospital days, he remained tachycardic at 105-130/min with blood pressures in the 110/80 mmHg range and stable O2 saturations on 3L per nasal cannula. Transthoracic echocardiogram revealed right ventricular strain and a mobile structure in the right atrium, possibly consistent with thrombus. On hospital day four, he remained hemodynamically stable but experienced mild pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath. On hospital day five, the patient was switched from IV heparin to enoxaparin 120 mg twice daily with a two-hour overlap; he was noted to have a transient O2 desaturation but remained normotensive. In the early hours of day six, he became diaphoretic and developed sustained tachycardia at 140/min with a blood pressure of 100/70 mmHg. He then developed chest pain and worsening dyspnea and lethargy, and was found to be in atrial flutter with 2:1 block. In the early afternoon, while speaking with the medical team and later while working with physical therapy, the patient complained of worsening shortness of breath, and was noted to be diaphoretic and persistently tachycardic at 120/min. Shortly thereafter, he lost consciousness and was found to be in cardiac arrest. He was treated per advanced cardiac life support (ACLS) protocol with temporary restoration of circulation, but became asystolic and was pronounced dead after 30 minutes.

output.reasoning: The PERC Rule criteria for Pulmonary Embolism Rule-out Criteria are: Age < 50 (55 present, so criteria failed), Pulse < 100 (119 present, so criteria failed), O2 Sat >= 95% (93% present, so criteria failed), Unilateral leg swelling (not explicitly mentioned, though DVT found later, not at time of initial assessment), Hemoptysis (not mentioned/absent), Recent surgery/trauma (not mentioned/absent), Prior PE/DVT (not mentioned/absent), Hormone use (not mentioned/absent). The patient fails the PERC rule.

A 55-year-old man with a history of type II diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obstructive sleep apnea, and depression developed pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath, and had a syncopal episode during a car trip from Texas to Cleveland. In the emergency room (ER), his blood pressure was 120/83 mmHg, pulse 119/min, respiratory rate 22/min, temperature 36.6oC, and oxygen (O2) saturation was 93% on room air. Physical exam was notable for an obese male in no acute distress with tachycardia, regular rhythm, clear lungs, shallow breaths without accessory muscle usage, and mild epigastric tenderness. His electrocardiogram (ECG) demonstrated sinus tachycardia at 117 bpm with a deep S wave in lead I, a small Q wave and inverted T wave in lead III (Figure ).
Initial laboratory testing was significant for serum sodium 132 mmol/L, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL, ProBNP 3188pg/mL, and initial troponin 0.12, peaking at 0.2 on hospital day two. Computed tomography (CT) chest showed bilateral submassive pulmonary emboli in multiple branches of the right and left upper and lower lobe pulmonary arteries, with no indication of a saddle embolus or main pulmonary artery thrombus (Figures -).
The patient was started on an intravenous (IV) heparin drip and thrombolytics were deferred due to perceived hemodynamic stability and timing of presentation. While in the ER, he became more tachycardic with heart rate 130-140 bpm, and developed jugular venous distention and acute hypoxemia requiring 3 liters (L) nasal cannula to maintain normal O2 saturation. He was admitted to the medical intensive care unit (MICU) due to possible signs of acute decompensation.

Over the second and third hospital days, he remained tachycardic at 105-130/min with blood pressures in the 110/80 mmHg range and stable O2 saturations on 3L per nasal cannula. Shortly thereafter, he lost consciousness and was found to be in cardiac arrest. - Conditions PRESENT: age_50_plus, pulse_ge_100, o2_sat_lt_95
- Conditions ABSENT: unilateral_leg_swelling, hemoptysis, recent_surgery_trauma, prior_pe_dvt, hormone_use
- Demographics: Age=55, Sex=male

args: A 59-year-old man was sent to the nephrology service by his primary internist after routine laboratory testing showed a rise in serum creatinine from 2.6 mg/dL six months ago to 3.1 mg/dL last week, with an accompanying estimated glomerular filtration rate of 24 mL/min/1.73 m². He reports gradually worsening early-morning fatigue and new nocturnal muscle cramps in the calves, but no dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea, or ankle swelling. Blood pressure at home averages 136/78 mm Hg on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 5 mg nightly. Ten years ago he was told that long-standing hypertension and microvascular ischemia of the kidneys were responsible for a slowly progressive decline in renal function; he has never required dialysis, but recent discussions have centered on possible pre-transplant evaluation. He strongly denies any episodes of prolonged chest pressure, emergent cardiac catheterization, hospital admissions for pump failure, or ever being told he sustained a heart attack, and he walks two flights of stairs without substernal discomfort or undue shortness of breath. Intermittent brief neurologic events occurred two summers ago: three episodes of transient right-hand numbness accompanied by word-finding difficulty, each resolving within 15 minutes without residual weakness. Work-up at that time, including non-contrast head CT and carotid duplex ultrasound, revealed no acute infarct or hemodynamically significant plaque, yet aspirin 81 mg daily and high-intensity statin therapy were initiated and no further spells have occurred. He denies sudden unilateral paralysis, visual field loss, or facial droop at any other time. Five years earlier he was hospitalized for hematemesis and melena; upper endoscopy demonstrated a 2-cm clean-based posterior duodenal ulcer with no visible vessel. He completed an eradication course for Helicobacter pylori, has remained on omeprazole 40 mg twice daily, and undergoes annual hemoglobin checks that have stayed above 13 g/dL. Since his twenties, liver chemistry panels have shown alanine aminotransferase values in the low 60s U/L and aspartate aminotransferase in the mid-40s U/L; abdominal ultrasound last winter again described mild diffuse hepatic steatosis without nodularity, ascites, or portal vein dilatation, and he has never manifested jaundice, easy bruising, encephalopathy, or variceal bleeding. Fasting glucose readings have persistently ranged between 88 and 104 mg/dL, and hemoglobin A1c obtained last month measured 5.7 %, so he continues to rely on dietary carbohydrate restriction alone. He is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks two bottles of beer on weekends, and has never used illicit substances. He has never been diagnosed with asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, emphysema, or chronic bronchitis and has never required maintenance inhaler therapy. He reports no history of rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, scleroderma, vasculitis, or other connective tissue disorders. He has not experienced rest pain, non-healing lower-extremity ulcers, or required vascular surgery for limb ischemia. He has never been diagnosed with any form of cancer, including solid organ or lymphoid malignancy, and his prior screening HIV testing has been negative. Family history is notable for a father who required coronary bypass at 72 and a sister who developed an ischemic stroke in her sixties; there is no familial kidney disease. Current medications include lisinopril, amlodipine, aspirin, rosuvastatin 20 mg nightly, omeprazole, cholecalciferol, and an over-the-counter magnesium supplement for cramps; he takes no nonsteroidal anti-inflammatories or nephrotoxic herbal preparations. Review of systems elicits no cough, wheeze, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, claudication, abdominal pain, hematochezia, dark urine, pruritus, photosensitive rash, or joint swelling. On arrival he appears well nourished, alert, and in no acute distress. Vital signs: temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 138/80 mm Hg in the right arm seated, heart rate 74 beats/min regular, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 98 % breathing ambient air, body mass index 28 kg/m². Jugular venous pulsation is 6 cm H₂O, carotid upstroke brisk without bruit, and the point of maximal impulse is nondisplaced. Cardiac examination reveals a crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs, gallops, or rubs. Lungs are clear to auscultation throughout. Abdomen is soft and non-tender with normal bowel sounds; the liver edge is palpable 1 cm below the right costal margin, smooth, and mildly tender to percussion, and no splenomegaly or shifting dullness is detected. Extremities show no edema; radial, femoral, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ bilaterally, and there is no femoral bruit. Neurologic evaluation demonstrates fluent speech, intact cranial nerves, 5/5 strength throughout, normal tone, symmetric reflexes, intact vibration and light-touch sensation, and steady tandem gait. Skin inspection reveals well-healed scars from prior intravenous line placements but no telangiectasias, palmar erythema, or spider angiomas. Laboratory studies drawn this morning reveal sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 5.0 mmol/L, chloride 101 mmol/L, bicarbonate 21 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 54 mg/dL, creatinine 3.2 mg/dL, calcium 8.4 mg/dL, phosphorus 4.9 mg/dL, albumin 3.9 g/dL, alkaline phosphatase 102 U/L, AST 48 U/L, ALT 61 U/L, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, international normalized ratio 1.1, hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, white blood cell count 7.2 × 10⁹/L, and platelets 248 × 10⁹/L. Urinalysis shows 1+ protein, no blood, and few granular casts; spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio is 0.6 g/g. Electrocardiogram obtained in clinic demonstrates normal sinus rhythm at 72 beats/min, axis within normal limits, no pathologic Q waves, and unchanged T-wave morphology. A recent transthoracic echocardiogram available in the electronic record lists left ventricular ejection fraction 58 %, no regional wall-motion abnormality, and normal valvular function. Duplex imaging of the renal arteries six months ago revealed mild intimal thickening without flow-limiting stenosis. The patient seeks guidance on slowing kidney decline, assessing candidacy for future renal replacement therapy, and ensuring control of associated comorbid conditions identified in prior hospitalizations.

args: A 63-year-old woman with a long-standing history of chronic hepatitis C infection genotype 1b that was treated eight years ago with sofosbuvir/ledipasvir yet left her with residual stage 3 fibrosis, presented to the emergency department shortly after dawn complaining of “coal-black diarrhea” that had begun the previous afternoon and recurred three more times overnight, each episode described as thick and tar-like with a pungent odor that stained the toilet bowl and was difficult to flush clean. She added that the last movement occurred an hour before arrival and was accompanied by mild crampy epigastric discomfort but no nausea or vomiting. She specifically denied ever feeling faint, losing consciousness, seeing stars, or needing to brace herself when she stood up from bed during the night; she did note feeling more fatigued than usual and a bit light-headed when walking from the parking lot into triage. Apart from her hepatic condition she carries diagnoses of well-controlled hypothyroidism and osteoarthritis of the knees; she has never undergone variceal banding, never had documented ascites, and her most recent hepatology visit three months earlier reported stable laboratory indices and no sonographic evidence of portal vein thrombosis. Surgical history is limited to an open appendectomy in her twenties. Her medicines include levothyroxine 75 µg each morning, acetaminophen up to 3 g daily for joint pain, and a daily vitamin D supplement; she explicitly denies aspirin, non-steroidal anti-inflammatories, anticoagulants, or antiplatelet agents. She consumes one glass of wine on special occasions, quit cigarette smoking fifteen years ago after a 10-pack-year exposure, and has never used intravenous drugs since completing antiviral therapy. There is no family history of bleeding disorders or gastrointestinal malignancy. On arrival she was seated upright, conversant, and appeared pale but not diaphoretic. Initial vital signs obtained before any intravenous access or medications showed a blood pressure of 104/67 mm Hg in the left arm with an automated cuff, a regular heart rate of 91 beats per minute by pulse oximeter, respirations 18 per minute, oral temperature 36.8 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % breathing ambient air. Capillary refill was brisk, jugular venous pulsation was not elevated, and peripheral pulses were symmetric. Sclerae were anicteric, though conjunctival pallor was noted; oral mucosa was moist without telangiectasias. The chest was clear to auscultation bilaterally, and cardiac examination revealed normal S1 and S2 without gallops, rubs, or murmurs. Abdominal inspection showed no distention; on palpation the abdomen was soft with mild tenderness localized to the epigastrium and right upper quadrant, without guarding, rebound, or palpable masses. No shifting dullness or fluid wave was appreciated, and the liver edge was not palpable below the costal margin. Several spider angiomas were visible over the upper chest, consistent with her known hepatic background. Bowel sounds were hyperactive. A digital rectal examination produced a generous smear of pitch-black, glossy stool that turned the bedside guaiac card deep blue within seconds. Neurologic screening demonstrated an alert, oriented individual with intact cranial nerves and full motor strength in all extremities; gait was not assessed in view of active bleeding concerns. Two 16-gauge peripheral intravenous catheters were placed after phlebotomy, but no crystalloids, blood products, vasoactive agents, proton pump inhibitors, or antibiotics had yet been administered. Laboratory studies drawn at triage revealed hemoglobin 11.7 g/dL, hematocrit 34 %, mean corpuscular volume 89 fL, platelet count 192 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 7.3 × 10⁹/L, blood urea nitrogen 33 mg/dL, serum creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL), sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, chloride 104 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, and glucose 108 mg/dL. Liver panel returned alanine aminotransferase 52 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 49 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 112 U/L, total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.4 g/dL, and an international normalized ratio of 1.3, similar to her outpatient baseline. Electrocardiography performed moments after arrival demonstrated normal sinus rhythm at 90–92 bpm without acute ischemic changes. Upright chest radiography showed clear lung fields and an unremarkable mediastinum; an abdominal radiograph revealed a nonspecific gas pattern without evidence of perforation. A type and crossmatch for two units of packed red blood cells was sent, the patient was kept nil per os, and gastroenterology was contacted for urgent esophagogastroduodenoscopy once hemodynamic stability was confirmed.

CRITICAL HINT: For GBS, use BUN in mg/dL: 18.2-22.3=2 pts, 22.4-27.9=3 pts, 28-69.9=4 pts, >=70=6 pts. Hemoglobin in g/dL: Men 12.0-12.9=1 pt, 10.0-11.9=3 pts, <10.0=6 pts. Women 10.0-11.9=1 pt, <10.0=6 pts. Systolic BP: 100-109=1 pt, 90-99=2 pts, <90=3 pts. Heart rate >=100=1 pt. Melena=1 pt, Syncope=2 pts, Hepatic disease=2 pts, Cardiac failure=2 pts.

output.reasoning: The patient is a 63-year-old woman. Clinical signs for GBS calculation identified: BUN is 33 mg/dL (4 pts, as 28-69.9 range). Hemoglobin is 11.7 g/dL (1 pt for women 10.0-11.9). Systolic BP is 104 mmHg (1 pt for 100-109 range). Heart rate is 91 bpm (0 pts, <100). Melena is present ("coal-black diarrhea", "pitch-black, glossy stool", guaiac positive) (1 pt). Syncope is denied (0 pts). Hepatic disease is present (chronic hepatitis C with stage 3 fibrosis) (2 pts). Cardiac failure is not mentioned (0 pts).

Sclerae were anicteric, though conjunctival pallor was noted; oral mucosa was moist without telangiectasias. The chest was clear to auscultation bilaterally, and cardiac examination revealed normal S1 and S2 without gallops, rubs, or murmurs. Abdominal inspection showed no distention; on palpation the abdomen was soft with mild tenderness localized to the epigastrium and right upper quadrant, without guarding, rebound, or palpable masses. No shifting dullness or fluid wave was appreciated, and the liver edge was not palpable below the costal margin. Several spider angiomas were visible over the upper chest, consistent with her known hepatic background. Bowel sounds were hyperactive. A digital rectal examination produced a generous smear of pitch-black, glossy stool that turned the bedside guaiac card deep blue within seconds. Neurologic screening demonstrated an alert, oriented individual with intact cranial nerves and full motor strength in all extremities; gait was not assessed in view of active bleeding concerns. Two 16-gauge peripheral intravenous catheters were placed after phlebotomy, but no crystalloids, blood products, vasoactive agents, proton pump inhibitors, or antibiotics had yet been administered. Laboratory studies drawn at triage revealed hemoglobin 11.7 g/dL, hematocrit 34 %, mean corpuscular volume 89 fL, platelet count 192 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 7.3 × 10⁹/L, blood urea nitrogen 33 mg/dL, serum creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL), sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, chloride 104 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, and glucose 108 mg/dL. Liver panel returned alanine aminotransferase 52 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 49 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 112 U/L, total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.4 g/dL, and an international normalized ratio of 1.3, similar to her outpatient baseline. Electrocardiography performed moments after arrival demonstrated normal sinus rhythm at 90–92 bpm without acute ischemic changes. Upright chest radiography showed clear lung fields and an unremarkable mediastinum; an abdominal radiograph revealed a nonspecific gas pattern without evidence of perforation. A type and crossmatch for two units of packed red blood cells was sent, the patient was kept nil per os, and gastroenterology was contacted for urgent esophagogastroduodenoscopy once hemodynamic stability was confirmed.

args: A 58-year-old woman presented to the hospital-based general internal-medicine clinic for a midday “turn-of-the-decade” physical at the insistence of her employer’s occupational-health division before she begins a new role supervising nightshift quality control at a regional food-processing plant; she walked briskly from the parking garage, placed a leather folder of prior laboratory printouts on the exam table, and remarked that her last physician retired two years ago, leaving her without formal follow-up since the onset of the pandemic. She reported climbing three flights of factory stairs several times per shift and cycling twelve miles along a riverside trail every Saturday without retrosternal pressure, arm or mandibular aching, unexplained perspiration, breathlessness beyond normal exertion, presyncope, or calf discomfort. She denied any prior hospitalization for myocardial infarction, angina evaluation, stent placement, coronary or peripheral bypass surgery, congestive-heart-failure treatment, cerebrovascular accident, or transient ischemic attack, and no clinician has informed her that a fasting glucose or hemoglobin A1c reached diabetic thresholds; the most recent basic metabolic panel from a community screening nine months ago showed a plasma glucose of 94 mg/dL, results she recalled because the nurse told her they were “nicely in range.” Childhood illnesses were limited to tonsillitis, and surgical history consists of a laparoscopic cholecystectomy at age 46 complicated only by transient shoulder tip pain. She takes a daily over-the-counter calcium–vitamin-D chew for osteopenia noted on a prior DEXA scan, a low-dose paracetamol tablet after particularly long production runs, and a lavender oil capsule she believes helps nighttime leg cramps; she has never been prescribed tablets or patches intended to lower arterial pressure, modify serum lipid fractions, regulate glucose, or inhibit platelet aggregation. Her father lived to 82 before succumbing to renal-cell carcinoma, and her mother, now 85, remains cognitively sharp with mild knee osteoarthritis; two older sisters are alive without early cardiac events, one treated for hypothyroidism and the other for migraine. The patient grew up in a household where tobacco was prohibited, finds the smell unpleasant, and has avoided cigarette, cigar, and vaping products entirely; she enjoys a five-ounce glass of pinot noir with Saturday dinner, limits caffeine to one morning mug of drip coffee, and denies recreational drug use, energy drinks, or herbal stimulants. Dietary recall revealed oatmeal topped with walnuts and berries at dawn, a mixed-bean salad with quinoa at midday, pan-seared salmon or grilled chicken with steamed vegetables in the evening, and an occasional square of dark chocolate during late-night audit report edits. Review of systems was negative for orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, pedal edema, palpitations, visual blurring, polyuria, polydipsia, cold or heat intolerance, unintended weight change, night sweats, or memory lapses. On examination she appeared well nourished, oriented, and in no acute distress, standing 165 cm tall and weighing 68 kg for a body-mass index of 25.0 kg/m². After five minutes of quiet sitting with her left arm supported at heart level, an automated oscillometric device registered 128/78 mm Hg, and manual confirmation on the contralateral arm reproduced the 128 mm Hg systolic reading with an 80 mm Hg diastolic value; radial pulse was regular at 68 beats per minute, respirations 14 per minute, oral temperature 36.7 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Carotid upstrokes were brisk without bruit, jugular venous pressure was not elevated, and no xanthelasma or corneal arcus were visible. The point of maximal impulse was palpable in the fifth intercostal space at the mid-clavicular line and nondisplaced; S1 and S2 were crisp with physiologic splitting, no murmurs, S3, S4, or pericardial rub appreciated. Lungs were clear throughout both fields, the abdomen was soft and nontender with a barely noticeable umbilical laparoscopic scar and no palpable aortic pulse beyond normal, and distal pulses were 2+ and symmetric without cyanosis, clubbing, edema, tendon xanthomata, or stasis changes. Cranial nerves II through XII were intact, strength 5/5 diffusely, sensation preserved, and gait steady with tandem walking. Fasting laboratory studies obtained earlier the same morning revealed a total cholesterol concentration of 211 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol 49 mg/dL, calculated low-density lipoprotein 138 mg/dL, triglycerides 122 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL with estimated glomerular filtration rate 84 mL/min/1.73 m², sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, alanine aminotransferase 18 U/L, and thyroid-stimulating hormone 2.3 µIU/mL. A resting twelve-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats per minute, PR interval 154 ms, QRS 88 ms, QTc 408 ms, and no Q waves, ST-segment deviations, or T-wave inversions. After discussion of findings, she expressed interest in continuing lifestyle measures to maintain favorable pressure readings without pharmacotherapy, scheduling interval lipid reassessment, and enrolling in a plant-based cooking class offered through her workplace wellness program before rotating onto the night schedule.

from typing import Any
def simulate_medical_value(patient_note: str, question: str) -> Any:
    """Calculate a medical value from a patient note.

    Calculate a medical value from a patient note.
    
    Given a patient note and a calculation question, reason step by step:
    1. Carefully read the patient note to extract all relevant clinical values
    2. Identify what medical calculation/score is needed
    3. Apply the appropriate formula from the reference below
    4. Show your calculation step by step, checking your arithmetic
    
    FORMULAS (use these exact formulas):
    - APACHE II Score: Acute Physiology Score + Age Points + Chronic Health Points
    - Adjusted Body Weight: ABW = IBW + 0.4 * (actual_weight - IBW)
    - Albumin Corrected Anion Gap: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    - Anion Gap: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    - Body Mass Index (BMI): BMI = weight_kg / (height_m)^2
    - Body Surface Area (Mosteller): BSA = sqrt((height_cm * weight_kg) / 3600)
    - CHA2DS2-VASc Score: Sum of: CHF(+1), HTN(+1), Age>=75(+2)/65-74(+1), DM(+1), Stroke/TIA(+2), Vascular(+1), Female(+1)
    - CKD-EPI GFR (2021): 142 * min(Cr/kappa,1)^alpha * max(Cr/kappa,1)^-1.200 * 0.9938^age * sex_coef
    - CURB-65 Score: C + U + R + B + 65 (0-5 points)
    - Calcium Correction for Hypoalbuminemia: Corrected Ca = Ca + 0.8 * (4 - albumin)
    - Caprini VTE Score: Sum of weighted risk factors
    - Centor Score (McIsaac): Sum with age modifier
    - Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI): Sum of weighted comorbidity points + age points
    - Child-Pugh Score: Sum of points for bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
    - Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault): CrCl = ((140 - age) * adjusted_weight * gender_coef) / (Cr * 72)
    - Date of Conception: gestational_weeks - 2 (conception ~2 weeks after LMP)
    - Delta Gap: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (where 12 is normal AG)
    - Delta Ratio: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    - Estimated Due Date: 40 - current_gestational_age
    - FIB-4 Index: FIB-4 = (Age * AST) / (Platelets * sqrt(ALT))
    - FeverPAIN Score: Fever + Purulence + Attend rapidly + Inflamed tonsils + No cough/coryza (0-5)
    - Fractional Excretion of Sodium (FENa): FENa = (UNa * SCr) / (SNa * UCr) * 100
    - Framingham Risk Score: Complex multivariate equation based on age, sex, lipids, BP, smoking
    - Free Water Deficit: FWD = TBW% * weight * (Na/140 - 1)
    - Gestational Age: weeks + days/7
    - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): Eye + Verbal + Motor (3-15)
    - Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS): Sum of clinical and lab criteria (0-23)
    - HAS-BLED Score: H-A-S-B-L-E-D criteria (0-9 points)
    - HEART Score: Sum of History + ECG + Age + Risk factors + Troponin (each 0-2 points)
    - HOMA-IR: HOMA-IR = (Glucose * Insulin) / 405
    - Ideal Body Weight (Devine): Male: IBW = 50 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60), Female: IBW = 45.5 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60)
    - LDL Calculated (Friedewald): LDL = Total Cholesterol - HDL - (Triglycerides / 5)
    - MDRD GFR: 175 * (Cr)^-1.154 * (age)^-0.203 * sex_coef
    - MELD-Na Score: MELD = 10 * (0.957*ln(Cr) + 0.378*ln(Bili) + 1.120*ln(INR) + 0.643)
    - Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1 Rule): First 10kg: 4ml/kg/hr, Next 10kg: 2ml/kg/hr, Remaining: 1ml/kg/hr
    - Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): MAP = (2 * DBP + SBP) / 3
    - Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME): Sum of (opioid dose * conversion factor)
    - PERC Rule: 8 criteria; PERC negative if all 0
    - PSI/PORT Score: Demographics + Comorbidities + Physical Exam + Labs
    - QTc (Bazett): QTc = QT / sqrt(RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Framingham): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Fridericia): QTc = QT / RR^(1/3) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Hodges): QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)
    - QTc (Rautaharju): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI): Sum of 6 risk factors (0-6 points)
    - SIRS Criteria: Count of criteria met (>= 2 = SIRS positive)
    - SOFA Score: Sum of 6 organ system scores (0-24)
    - Serum Osmolality: Osm = 2*Na + BUN/2.8 + Glucose/18
    - Sodium Correction for Hyperglycemia: Corrected Na = Na + 0.024 * (glucose - 100)
    - Steroid Conversion: Prednisone equivalent = dose * conversion_factor
    - Target Weight: Target Weight = target_bmi * (height_m)^2
    - Wells' Criteria for DVT: Sum of clinical criteria points
    - Wells' Criteria for PE: Sum of weighted criteria
    
    Important:
    - Be precise with extracted values. Double-check your arithmetic.
    - For sex/gender: "man"/"male"/"he" → male, "woman"/"female"/"she" → female.
    - Convert units as needed (lbs→kg: ×0.453592, feet/inches→cm: (ft×12+in)×2.54).
    - For scoring systems: carefully check each criterion against the patient note.
      Look for conditions implied by medications or clinical findings, not just
      explicitly named conditions.
    
    CRITICAL FORMATTING INSTRUCTIONS FOR THE FINAL ANSWER:
    - If the answer is a numeric value, return ONLY the raw number (e.g., 6.25). Do NOT include units, commas, or symbols like "%" or "mmHg".
    - If the answer is a date, return ONLY the exact date string in MM/DD/YYYY format (e.g., 06/09/2002). Do NOT wrap it in extra quotes.
    - If the answer is a tuple of weeks and days, return the exact Python tuple of strings (e.g., ('33 weeks', '5 days')).
    
    Examples:
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 70-year-old male weighing 80 kg, height 175 cm.", "What is the patient's BMI?")
    26.122
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 65-year-old female, BP 130/85 mmHg.", "What is the patient's MAP?")
    100.0
    """
    ...

A 43-year-old man presented to the gastroenterology department with colicky abdominal pain, microcytic hypochromic anemia, and fatigue. At colonoscopy, a tumor was seen in the ascending colon, as shown in .
His FBC was as follows: WBC: 17.7 × 109/L (normal: 4.0–11.0), NE: 15.9 × 109/L (normal: 2.0–7.5), LY: 0.7 × 109/L (normal: 1.5–4.0) MO: 0.8 × 109/L (normal: 0.2–0.8), HB: 98 g/L (normal: 130–180), MCV: 66.9 FL (normal: 76–100), MCH: 19.5 pg (normal: 27–32), MCHC: 291 g/L (normal: 310–360), RDW: 20 (normal: 10–15.7) crea: 198 μmol/L (normal: 58–110), urea: 11.0 mmol/L (normal: 2.5–7.8), bili: 8 μmol/L (normal: 0–21), ALT: 23 (normal: 3–53), ALP: 75 IU/L (normal: 30–130), albumin: 23 g/L (normal: 35–50), Ca: 2.45 mmol/L (normal: 2.20–2.60), and globulin: 20 g/L (normal: 18–36).
A CT scan of the thorax, abdomen, and pelvis showed a large soft tissue mass extending from the right iliac fossa into the pelvis with involvement of the adjacent small bowel loops. There was extensive bowel wall thickening of the caecum and ascending colon as shown in , extending over a length of approximately 20 cm, with the wall measuring up to 4.5 cm in thickness. Regional and mesenteric nodes were demonstrated and there was a suspicion of right juxtadiaphragmatic pulmonary and umbilical deposits. A subsequent dedicated skeletal survey was negative for lytic lesions.
CT scan pictures to follow as shown in .
The fragments of colonic mucosa showed extensive infiltration by large cells with eccentric nuclei and prominent nucleoli as shown in . Frequent mitotic figures were noted and the Ki67 proliferation index was 100%. The cells expressed plasma cell associated markers including CD138 and IRF4 as shown in though they lacked CD19 and showed strong expression of CD56 as shown in . There was no evidence of EBV. FISH identified a MYC rearrangement. The differential included plasmablastic lymphoma or soft tissue plasmacytoma though the presence of strong CD56 and lack of EBV favoured the latter. A subsequent bone marrow biopsy confirmed the diagnosis of myeloma with neoplastic plasma cells identified by flow cytometry (CD19−CD56++CD27−CD45−) and a multifocal infiltrate of plasma cells identified with similar blastic morphology as shown in . A c-MYC rearrangement was detected by FISH on the primary tissue biopsy and there was no evidence of other recurrent cytogenetic abnormalities.
The patient was then referred to the haematology department where serum protein electrophoresis was performed, but no monoclonal band was detected. IgG: 5.7 g/L (normal: 6–16), IgA: 2.5 g/L (normal: 0.80–4), IgM: <0.25 g/L (normal: 0.40–2.30), free kappa chains which were normal 15.6 mg/L (normal: 6.7–22.4), free lambda chains: 20.2 (normal: 8.3–27), SFLCR: 0.77 (normal: 0.31–1.56), within normal limits, and UBJ: negative, and urinary protein immunofixation did not detect a light chain band.
Consider the following: ESR: 47 mm/hr (normal: 3–15), ferritin: 55 μg/L (normal: 30–365), CRP: 162 mg/L (normal: 0–10), and LDH: 1415 IU/L (normal: 313–618).
The patient tested negative for HIV and EBV.
The bone marrow biopsy showed a normocellular marrow with active trilineage hematopoiesis.
There was a focal infiltrate of large blastic cells and a neoplastic plasma cell population was identified by flow cytometry. The features were consistent with plasma cell myeloma.
From the flow cytometry the plasma cells were 1.8% of leucocytes, of which 90% had a neoplastic phenotype CD19−, CD56++, CD27−, and CD45−.
Unfortunately the patient died of a pulmonary embolism before treatment could be commenced. Postmortem examination showed extensive tumour deposits in the abdominal cavity. The appearance in the abdominal cavity was consistent with disseminated myelomatous involvement. The plasma cell myeloma had put an increased risk for the development of pulmonary embolism and therefore would have also contributed indirectly to the cause of death. Patients with newly diagnosed multiple myeloma, prior to having received therapy, seem to be at increased risk for venous thromboembolic disease and pulmonary embolism as discussed by Auwerda et al. [].
