args: A 47-year-old Vietnamese-American woman presented to the university metabolic risk consultation service at 07:25 for a formally supervised fasting evaluation after an at-home glucometer borrowed from an elder relative displayed several “low one-hundred teens” readings on waking this past month; she had deliberately avoided changing her diet, restarting the jogging routine she maintained before pregnancy, or resuming any of the herbal teas her mother recommends so that today’s numbers would represent an untouched baseline. She works full time as a nail-salon technician, standing for prolonged stretches beneath acetone fumes, and often skips a proper lunch because customer volume is unpredictable; when appointments run long she quenches hunger with prepackaged bánh phồng tôm crackers and sweetened iced coffee kept behind the register. Six months ago she delivered her third child at term after a pregnancy complicated by borderline glucose values that never required pharmacotherapy but did trigger a hospital nutrition class; once the baby arrived she rapidly shed most gestational weight and now weighs 56 kg at a height of 160 cm (body-mass index 21.9 kg/m²), essentially matching her pre-pregnancy measurements, though she notices easier fatigability climbing the apartment stairs. She denies polyuria beyond one nocturnal void that coincides with breastfeeding, has not experienced polydipsia, visual blurring, cutaneous infections, or distal paresthesias, yet admits occasional light-headedness late in the afternoon if her first meal was delayed until after 14:00, a sensation that resolves within minutes of a sugary beverage. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable: no hypertension, dyslipidemia, thyroid disease, autoimmune disorders, or prior steroid exposure; she has never taken metformin, sulfonylureas, injectable incretin analogs, weight-loss medications, or over-the-counter bitter-melon capsules. Surgical history is limited to three uncomplicated cesarean deliveries. Both parents developed type 2 diabetes in their early 60s—her father now uses a basal-bolus insulin regimen—and two older brothers were started on oral agents before age 50. She has never smoked combustible tobacco or e-cigarettes, drinks half a glass of red wine at Lunar-New-Year banquets only, and denies illicit drug use. Structured exercise is minimal since the newborn’s arrival; her smartwatch shows an average of 4 000 steps per day, mostly from work. Review of systems is negative for chest pain, exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, claudication, depressive mood, heat or cold intolerance, or changes in shoe or hat size. On arrival she appeared well, nursing the infant in the waiting area, and was in no distress. After eight minutes seated quietly, vital signs were blood pressure 112/72 mm Hg in the right arm, pulse 64 beats/minute regular, respiratory rate 14/minute, oral temperature 36.6 °C, and oxygen saturation 99 % breathing ambient air. Neck circumference measured 32 cm; there was no thyromegaly or cervical acanthosis. Cardiac examination revealed crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; radial and posterior tibial pulses were 2+ and symmetric. Lungs were clear to auscultation. The abdomen was soft and nontender with a well-healed Pfannenstiel scar; no hepatosplenomegaly was palpable. Neither pedal edema nor xanthomata were present. Neurologic screen demonstrated intact cranial nerves, symmetric 2+ deep-tendon reflexes, preserved 10-g monofilament sensation on plantar surfaces, and normal vibration perception with a 128-Hz tuning fork at great toes. Funduscopic inspection revealed sharp disc margins and no retinal hemorrhages. She confirmed ingesting nothing but plain water after 20:00 the prior evening; venipuncture took place at 07:32, samples were placed on wet ice, and the laboratory accessioned them within fifteen minutes. The chemistry analyzer (hexokinase methodology) reported a fasting plasma glucose concentration of 115 mg/dL, while a concurrent fasting serum insulin level measured by chemiluminescent immunoassay was 6 µIU/mL. Additional studies from the same draw showed hemoglobin A1c 5.7 %, serum creatinine 0.71 mg/dL with estimated glomerular filtration rate >90 mL/min/1.73 m², sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 11 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 22 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 19 U/L, total cholesterol 182 mg/dL, low-density lipoprotein 109 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein 54 mg/dL, and triglycerides 98 mg/dL. Thyroid-stimulating hormone was 1.8 µIU/mL, morning cortisol 15 µg/dL, and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein 1.2 mg/L. Urinalysis revealed specific gravity 1.010, negative protein, absent ketones, and no glucosuria; spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio measured 8 mg/g. A 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained for baseline cardiovascular risk stratification showed normal sinus rhythm at 62 beats/minute without conduction delays or ischemic changes. She emphasized she has not yet reintroduced regular running, tightened carbohydrate intake, or taken any prescription or traditional remedies since the abnormal home readings and seeks guidance on evidence-based nutritional planning compatible with breastfeeding, realistic postpartum exercise scheduling, and clarification regarding whether pharmacologic therapy might be required should lifestyle measures prove insufficient.

fasting_glucose: Fasting glucose in mg/dL (e.g., 100)
fasting_insulin: Fasting insulin in uU/mL (e.g., 10)

args:
A 49-year-old male patient presented with acute-onset progressive abdominal cramping pain that had started 1 day previously. He had a medical history of poorly controlled diabetes mellitus and hypertension, as well as renal stone formation after percutaneous nephrolithotomy with double J replacement. There was no history of trauma. Dysuria and mild urgency were noted. He denied having any fever, chills, cough, chest pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. His temperature was 36.8 °C, blood pressure was 162/89 mmHg, and heart rate was 131/min. On physical examination, a hyperactive bowel sound was noted, accompanied by whole abdominal tenderness, especially at the left quadrant. The Murphy sign was negative, and no tenderness was noted at McBurney’s point. There was no bilateral knocking pain. The laboratory results were as follows: white blood cell count 40,250/µL (band-form neutrophils 2.0%, segment-form neutrophils 86.0%, lymphocytes 5.0%, eosinophils 0.0%, and monocytes 6.0%), hemoglobin 6.7 g/dL, platelet count 645,000/mL, blood urine nitrogen 51 mg/dL, creatinine 1.9 mg/dL, sodium 124 mmol/L, potassium 5.5 mmol/L, glucose 790 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 19 U/L, lipase 768 IU/L, total bilirubin 1.00 mg/dL, troponin I <0.01 μg/L, ketone bodies 4.5 mmol/L, and serum osmolarity 336 mOsm/kg. The urinalysis results were as follows: Red blood cell count 10–19/high-power field (HPF), white blood cell count 10–19/HPF, glucose 4+, ketone bodies 1+, bacteria 1+/HPF, and yeast 3+/HPF. The venous blood gas analysis revealed the following results: pH 7.390, pCO2 29.2 mmHg, pO2 44.5 mmHg, HCO3 17.3 mmol/L, actual base excess −6.3 mmol/L, base excess in extracellular fluid −7.6 mmol/L, and O2 saturation 78.9%. The detailed results of laboratory evaluations are listed in . The plain film showed diffuse bilateral opacities over the lung field (A). The kidney, ureter, and bladder (KUB) study revealed gallbladder stone, double J catheter placement from the pelvic cavity to the right renal region, and a heterogeneous mass with air density at the left side (B).
Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT was performed, which revealed several cavitary nodules in both lower lung fields (A,B). A perirenal heterogeneous mass with gas density was found inside the left renal capsule (C). According to the above-mentioned clinical symptoms and images, EPN and multiple septic pulmonary emboli were suspected. The broad-spectrum antibiotics meropenem and teicoplanin were administrated for sepsis. Meropenem was administered for 13 days and teicoplanin was used for 9 days; then, the patient was shifted to ceftriaxone for 3 days. In addition, the patient’s hyperkalemia was treated with insulin in 5% glucose solution and calcium polystyrene sulfonate powder (Kalimate). Transfusion of 2 U packed red blood cells was done for anemia. Famotidine was administered to prevent stress ulcer. Emergency percutaneous nephrostomy was done (D), and pus and urine were collected for culture. The cultures showed E. coli growth in urine and pus. No growth of significant aerobic or anaerobic pathogens in blood was noted. After timely treatment, the sepsis was controlled. The follow-up plain film and abdominal CT revealed a few cavitary nodules and less accumulation of perirenal abscess. The patient was discharged and followed up at the urologic and chest outpatient departments. The follow-up serum creatinine level is summarized in . This study was approved by the Institutional Review Board (IRB) of Taipei Tzu Chi Hospital, Buddhist Tzu Chi Medical Foundation (IRB no. 07-CR-059).

sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
bun: Blood urea nitrogen in mg/dL (e.g., 20)
glucose: Serum glucose in mg/dL (e.g., 100)

args: An 82-year-old woman was seen in the electrophysiology clinic for routine reassessment of long-term anticoagulation in the setting of permanent atrial fibrillation first documented during hospitalization for decompensated heart failure nine years earlier. She ambulated into the exam room with a four-pronged cane and reported that over the last two months her rhythm-related symptoms have consisted only of mild exertional fatigue without palpitations, chest discomfort, dyspnea at rest, syncope, or presyncope. Her primary concern today is balancing protection against recurrent cerebrovascular events with the possibility of another hemorrhage after a recent conversation with her daughter, who questioned the safety of continued anticoagulation at her age. Past medical history is notable for a left hemispheric ischemic stroke six years ago that presented with right facial droop and arm weakness; after intravenous thrombolysis and inpatient rehabilitation she regained full motor strength, though she continues to notice occasional word-finding difficulty when fatigued. Three years after that event she was hospitalized for a brisk upper gastrointestinal bleed provoked by a large duodenal ulcer; at that time her hemoglobin nadired at 6.8 g/dL, she received four units of packed red blood cells, and endoscopic therapy with epinephrine injection and hemoclip placement successfully achieved hemostasis. She has not experienced melena, hematemesis, or symptomatic anemia since, and a surveillance esophagogastroduodenoscopy performed one year ago showed a well-healed scar with no stigmata of recent bleeding. She denies epistaxis, gingival oozing, hematuria, heavy menstrual bleeding in earlier life, easy bruising, or a family history of hereditary coagulopathy. Her chronic medications include apixaban 5 mg twice daily, metoprolol succinate 50 mg every morning for rate control, torsemide 20 mg each day for fluid balance, and rosuvastatin 10 mg nightly; she specifically avoids aspirin, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, or herbal supplements such as ginkgo or ginseng. She has never been diagnosed with viral hepatitis, alcoholic cirrhosis, hemochromatosis, or cholestatic disease; a comprehensive metabolic panel drawn last month revealed alanine aminotransferase 22 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 25 U/L, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, albumin 4.0 g/dL, and an international normalized ratio of 1.0 obtained twelve hours after the missed evening apixaban dose for laboratory accuracy. Likewise, there is no history of nephropathy, nephrolithiasis, or proteinuria; her baseline serum creatinine has been stable between 0.8 and 0.9 mg/dL for several years with an estimated glomerular filtration rate consistently above 75 mL/min/1.73 m². She underwent cholecystectomy in her fifties and cataract extraction in her seventies without bleeding complications. Socially, she retired from teaching high-school literature a decade ago, lives independently in a single-story home, and continues to participate in a weekly book club held at the community library. She stopped cigarette smoking at age 48 and denies ever using recreational drugs. Alcohol intake consists of one 5-ounce glass of red wine with dinner every night and occasionally an extra glass on Saturdays, totaling about 7 drinks per week; she never binge-drinks and spaces consumption evenly. Dietary habits include a Mediterranean pattern rich in fruits, vegetables, legumes, and olive oil, with limited processed foods. She performs chair yoga routines on weekday mornings and walks half a mile around her cul-de-sac most afternoons, aided by her cane. Review of systems is negative for fevers, night sweats, unintended weight loss, jaundice, abdominal swelling, flank pain, dark urine, or lower-extremity edema. On examination she appears alert, oriented, and conversant, body mass index 26 kg/m². Pulse is irregularly irregular at 76 beats per minute and volume is normal; jugular venous pressure is not elevated; carotid upstroke is brisk without bruit. Cardiac auscultation reveals a variable first heart sound but no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally with good air movement. The abdomen is soft and non-tender; liver span per percussion measures 8 cm in the mid-clavicular line with a smooth, non-nodular edge on palpation; there is no splenomegaly or ascites. Extremities display warm, well-perfused skin without petechiae, purpura, or dependent edema. Gait is slow but steady with the cane, and neurologic testing shows intact cranial nerves, symmetric strength, preserved proprioception, and normal cerebellar function aside from mild word-finding pauses consistent with her known residual aphasia. Laboratory studies obtained this morning demonstrate hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL, hematocrit 37 %, platelets 230 × 10⁹/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, and blood urea nitrogen 15 mg/dL; repeat hepatic and renal panels are unchanged from prior measurements. A 12-lead electrocardiogram confirms atrial fibrillation with an average ventricular rate of 74 beats per minute, QRS duration 98 ms, and QTc 438 ms, without acute ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities. A transthoracic echocardiogram performed last quarter showed left atrial enlargement of 5.2 cm, preserved left-ventricular ejection fraction of 60 %, no significant valvular disease, and normal right-sided pressures. She expresses willingness to continue pharmacologic therapy if the quantitative benefit outweighs the danger of another gastrointestinal bleed but wishes to understand her personalized hemorrhagic risk profile before making a decision.

output.reasoning: The patient is an 82-year-old female (Age >=65=1 point). Hypertension is absent (BP is not stated, but generally hypertensive patients are noted; no history of HTN provided). Abnormal renal function: Absent (Cr stable 0.8-0.9, eGFR >75). Abnormal liver function: Absent (Normal enzymes, bilirubin, albumin, no cirrhosis). Stroke: Present (history of ischemic stroke 6 years ago). Bleeding: Present (history of major GI bleed requiring transfusion 3 years ago). Labile INR: Not applicable (patient is on apixaban, not warfarin; INR 1.0 is stable). Elderly: Present (Age >=65=1 point). Drugs/Alcohol: Drugs (Aspirin/NSAIDs/etc explicitly avoided, so absent). Alcohol (7 drinks/week is moderate/low; usually >8/week counts for H, so absent). Total Score calculation: Age(1) + Stroke(1) + Bleeding(1) = 3 points.

def extract_calculator_values(

    field_descriptions: str,
    reasoning_context: str,
) -> dict:
    """Extract specific clinical values from a patient note for a medical calculator.

    You are given:
    - A patient note with clinical information
    - The name of the calculator to extract values for
    - A description of each field to extract (with units and expected ranges)
    - Optional reasoning context from a prior analysis stage

    Instructions:
    - Use the EXACT parameter names from the field descriptions as JSON keys
    - CRITICAL: Always extract the patient's baseline or admission values (prior to any treatment or surgery) unless the question specifically asks for post-treatment values. Pay close attention to the timeline in the note.
    - CRITICAL: If a field name ends in '_score' or '_points' (e.g., 'bun_score', 'age_score'), you MUST calculate and provide the integer point value based on the scoring system, NOT the raw clinical measurement (e.g., if bun_score is requested, give the points for BUN, not the raw BUN in mg/dL).
    - For all other fields, extract the RAW numeric value (e.g., if 'platelets' is requested, provide 115, not the point value).
    - Convert units ONLY for weight (lbs→kg: ×0.453592) and height (feet/inches→cm).
    - CRITICAL: DO NOT convert units for lab values. Extract the raw numeric value exactly as it appears in the text (e.g., if the text says 26 g/L, extract 26. If it says 120 mmol/L, extract 120).
    - Extract dates exactly as they appear in the text (e.g., "09/05/2001").
    - For boolean conditions: True if present, False if absent
    - If a condition is not mentioned, assume it is absent (False or 0)
    - For scoring systems with mutually exclusive severity levels (e.g., mild vs moderate/severe liver disease), mark ONLY the single most appropriate severity level as True.
    - Include ALL fields, even if the value is False/0/absent
    - If a required numeric value or field is entirely unmentioned and cannot be inferred, OMIT it from the 'extracted' dictionary completely instead of writing 'missing' or null.
    - CRITICAL: Do NOT guess or assume standard physiological values. If it is missing, omit it.

    Return a dict with:
    - "extracted": {"param_name": value, ...} for each field
    - "missing": ["list of values truly not determinable from the note"]

    Examples:
    >>> extract_calculator_values("A 70yo male, 80kg, 175cm, Cr 1.2", "Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault)", "age: years, sex: male/female, weight_kg: kg, creatinine_mg_dl: mg/dL", "")

    """

args:
A 66-year-old Taiwanese man had a history of hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis on quinine, methotrexate, and methylprednisolone, and cement injection for compression fracture of osteoporotic vertebrae. He underwent an endovascular stent graft of abdominal aortic aneurysm 18 months ago at an urban hospital. According the clinical course, he had been suffering from recurrent episodes of fever, chills, and abdominal fullness 6 months ago, but no definite diagnosis could be confirmed. One month ago, his C-reactive protein (CRP) was 20 mg/dL and the contrast-enhanced abdominal computed tomography (CT) revealed an endoleak (Fig. ), yet no fluid accumulation within the psoas muscle was detected. After a 4-day course of intravenous (IV) amoxicillin/clavulanic acid, fever subsided and he was discharged.
This time, he presented to our emergency department with fever, chills, and watery diarrhea for 1 day. On examinations, he was febrile with a body temperature of 37.6°C, a blood pressure of 117/68 mmHg, and a heart rate of 95 beats/min. Laboratory tests revealed white blood cell count of 5700 cells/mm3 with segmented neutrophils of 85.5%, hemoglobin of 11.4 g/dL, platelet counts of 271 × 103 cells/mm3, blood urea nitrogen of 17 mg/dL, keratinize of 1.1 mg/dL, sodium of 132 mEq/L, potassium of 3.7 mEq/L, albumin of 3.4 g/dL, glutamic-pyruvic transaminase of 63 U/L, alkaline phosphates of 77 U/L, lactate dehydrogenase of 381 U/L, lactate 18.5 mg/dL, CRP of 9.73 mg/dL, blood glucose of 105 mg/dL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 113 mm/h. Stool analysis showed no pus cell. Abdominal CT uncovered the presence of an irregular enhancement within left psoas muscle, connecting with contiguous thromboses aneurysmal sac, which was suggestive of left psoas abscess associated with an infected endograft (Fig. ).
After admission, cardiovascular surgeon suggested initiating cautious trial of antimicrobial therapy alone instead of concomitant radical debridement with graft removal, considering patient's frailty with immunosuppressant's and the radiological absence of endoleak and connecting fistula. CT-guided drainage was not feasible in radiologist's opinion owing to a high risk of close proximity to great vessels. The blood culture yielded growth of Listeria monocytogenes, susceptible to ampicillin, penicillin, and sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim (SMX/TMP). IV ampicillin (2 g, 6 times daily) with 8 days of synergistic gentamicin (80 mg, twice daily) was chosen as the initial regiment and it created satisfactory response. Ampicillin was continued for 30 days and then shifted to IV co-trimoxazole (80/400 mg, 4 times daily) for 12 days. He remained asymptomatic with a decline of CRP to 0.36 mg/dL and ESR to 39 mm/h. He was discharged on the 44th hospital day.
Three months later, abdominal CT illustrated nearly complete resolution of bilateral psoas abscess. Orally SMX/TMP 400 mg/80 mg was prescribed for 13.5 months at outpatient follow-up. Until now, he kept regular outpatient surveillance and made an uneventful recovery.

sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
bun: Blood urea nitrogen in mg/dL (e.g., 20)
glucose: Serum glucose in mg/dL (e.g., 100)

args: A 67-years-old Caucasian man presented for refashioning of a left below-knee amputation stump. His co-morbidities included diabetes, cardiovascular disease, peripheral neuropathy, obesity and asthma. Medications included insulin, amlodipine, lisinopril, gabapentin and salbutamol. Previous regional block and general anaesthesia were uneventful. The patient made an informed choice of spinal anaesthesia. Standard perioperative monitoring was used. Spinal block was performed aseptically at the L4/5 spinal space with a 25 G Whitacre needle and 2.5 ml of heavy 0.5% bupivacaine plus diamorphine 300 mcg. The spinal block procedure was uneventful. Adequate motor and sensory block was achieved at five minutes. Adequate sensory block up to the T8 dermatome was confirmed by loss of cold sensation. The intraoperative course was uneventful. However, at the end of surgery, about 60 minutes after the spinal blockade, the patient complained of repetitive muscle spasm in the absent amputated limb. The phantom spasm was distressful to the patient and was promptly treated with intravenous midazolam 5 mg. There was no recurrence of myoclonus and no sequelae.

output.reasoning: Patient is a 67-year-old male. Age 67 does not meet the >=75 threshold, so age points are 0. Condition "atrial fibrillation" is not mentioned. Condition "hypertension" is implied by the use of amlodipine and lisinopril. Condition "diabetes" is explicitly stated and supported by insulin use. "Congestive heart failure" is not mentioned. "Vascular disease" is explicitly stated as "cardiovascular disease" and also supported by the history of amputation and peripheral neuropathy. "Stroke/TIA" is not mentioned.

args:
Abdominal pain and fullness.
The patient was a 52-year-old Asian woman, who was diagnosed with liver dysfunction during a medical examination in her twenties. A diagnosis of autoimmune hepatitis was made at 40 years of age. When the patient was 46 years old, the patient developed ascites, which improved with oral steroids. However, with disease progression, she developed decompensated cirrhosis at 51 years old that was resistant to medical management. She was then referred to our department.
There was no other significant medical history.
The patient was a nonsmoker and had stopped drinking socially 5 years prior. Her occupation was a housewife. There was no relevant family history.
According to the Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group Performance Status, her performance status was 2. At the physical examination, the patient’s height was 155 cm, her weight was 47 kg, and her vitals were stable; yellowish bulbar conjunctivae, ascites, and bilateral pedal edema were observed.
The Child-Pugh score was 11 points in class C, and the Model for end stage liver disease score was 11 points. The serologic tests for CMV showed that the patient was IgG positive (+), IgM negative (-), and AG negative, which is indicative of past CMV infection. A PCR test for CMV was not performed routinely before transplantation at our facility and was not performed in this case.
Abdominal computed tomography (CT) revealed liver cirrhosis with ascites before LDLT.
A blood-type compatible LDLT was performed using a left lobe graft, with the patient’s second son as the donor (20 years old, CMV IgG+/IgM-, which is indicative of past CMV infection). The graft-to-recipient weight ratio was 0.73, the operation duration was 849 min, and the bleeding volume was 822 mL. At our facility, in accordance with the protocol of CMV monitoring and treatment after a liver transplantation, CMV-AG is tested twice a week, but a CMV-PCR test is not performed routinely. In addition, prophylactic ganciclovir (GCV) is not administered, but GCV is initiated when the patient becomes CMV-AG positive or in the case of a seropositive donor.
Initially, cyclophosphamide (CyA), prednisolone (PSL) and mizoribine (MIZ) were used as the postoperative immunosuppressants, in accordance with the protocol of our facility[,], however, for this patient, MIZ was replaced by mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) due to pancytopenia, and CyA was replaced by tacrolimus (FK) due to renal failure. As the patient had jaundice and persistently elevated aspartate transaminase and alanine transaminase levels, a liver biopsy was performed on the 10th day after transplantation. The histopathological examination was negative for both acute cellular rejection (ACR) and CMV hepatitis, so her condition was suspected to be drug-induced or caused by cholestasis.
Starting on the 12th day after transplantation, the patient’s anemia worsened, and she required frequent packed red blood cell transfusions. Further investigations confirmed thrombocytopenia, jaundice, and renal failure. We suspected thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA), even though the peripheral smear was negative for fragmented red blood cells, and a fresh frozen plasma transfusion and FK dose reduction were carried out. The CMV-AG remained negative, and there were no clinical findings that were characteristic of a CMV infection, but prophylactic GCV administration was initiated. Thereafter, the thrombocytopenia gradually improved. On the 20th day after transplantation, the patient reported abdominal pain and black-colored stools, so upper gastrointestinal endoscopy was performed, which showed multiple gastric ulcers (Figure ). A biopsy tissue sample, taken from an ulcer, showed large cells with intranuclear inclusions with hematoxylin and eosin (HE) staining (Figure ), and CMV-positive cells were observed through immunostaining (Figure ). Once the diagnosis of CMV gastroenteritis was confirmed, GCV, which had already been initiated, was continued, and the dosage of all 3 immunosuppressants, FK, PSL, and MMF, was reduced.
On the 26th day after transplantation, the patient had frequent, watery diarrhea, for which a lower gastrointestinal endoscopy was performed, and a deep ulcer was observed in the transverse colon (Figure ). The biopsy tissue diagnosis of the ulcerative lesion revealed large cells with intranuclear inclusions with HE-staining (Figure ) and CMV-positive cells with immunostaining (Figure ). On the 28th day after transplantation, we noted the findings of abdominal pain, fever and an increased inflammatory response. A plain abdominal CT scan revealed intraperitoneal free air adjacent to the transverse colon (Figure ) and hemorrhagic ascites in the pelvis (Figure ).
The patient was diagnosed with gastrointestinal perforation, and an emergency surgery was performed. When the abdomen was incised, contaminated ascites were not observed. In the transverse colon, an impending perforation with a thinned serous membrane was confirmed.
The rejection activity index score of the liver tissues collected during the surgery was found to be P2, B1, and V1 during the histopathologic examination, indicating ACR.

sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
height_cm: Patient height in centimeters (e.g., 170.0)

args: A 57-year-old woman with a seven-year history of biopsy-confirmed nonalcoholic steatohepatitis–related cirrhosis was brought to the academic medical center’s urgent evaluation area by her sister at 0730 after being found slumped over the breakfast table mumbling incomprehensibly and failing to recognize her surroundings; according to the sister, the patient had slept more than usual over the prior week, spoken in sentences that trailed off midway, and twice attempted to put her slippers into the refrigerator, yet there had been no witnessed seizures, head trauma, recent falls, or ingestion of sedatives, ethanol, or illicit substances. She had never experienced overt gastrointestinal bleeding, had not previously undergone therapeutic paracentesis, and had never been prescribed lactulose, rifaximin, diuretics, vitamin K, or non-selective beta-blockers. Her medical background is otherwise notable for class II obesity (body mass index 35 kg/m²), type 2 diabetes controlled with metformin 1000 mg twice daily, hypertension treated with losartan 50 mg each morning, and hypertriglyceridemia for which she takes icosapent ethyl; she has no history of renal impairment, pulmonary disease, or thromboembolic events, and she underwent laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy four years earlier without complication. She denies tobacco use, ceased alcohol consumption after her diagnosis, and has no known drug allergies. There is a maternal aunt with primary biliary cholangitis but no family members with metabolic liver disease. Review of systems obtained from the sister prior to any hospital intervention was negative for fever, chills, chest pain, dyspnea, cough, rash, recent travel, or sick contacts; the patient herself was unable to provide reliable answers. On arrival—before placement of intravenous lines, administration of intravenous fluids, or phlebotomy—vital signs were temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 110/64 mm Hg, heart rate 96 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 97 % breathing ambient air. She appeared deeply lethargic with eyes half-open, responded only to her name spoken loudly, and moaned rather than forming words; she could not state the date, follow three-step commands, or maintain attention, though she withdrew her limbs briskly to painful stimulus. Pupils were equal and reactive, corneal reflexes intact, tone was normal, plantar responses were flexor, and when her wrists were passively extended a coarse irregular flap emerged immediately. There were no focal deficits. Breath carried a sweet musty odor. Sclerae were distinctly yellow, conjunctivae were mildly pale, oral mucosa was moist, and the tongue was coated but without thrush. The neck was supple with jugular venous pulsation 7 cm H₂O at 30°, carotid upstrokes were brisk, and heart examination revealed a regular rhythm without extra sounds or murmurs. Lungs were clear to auscultation. The abdomen protruded modestly but the umbilicus remained recessed; percussion produced tympany centrally transitioning to a thin rim of dullness along the right flank that shifted subtly when she was rolled onto her left side, and with the examiner’s hand placed firmly at the midline a faint fluid impulse was felt crossing the abdominal wall. Palpation disclosed a firm nodular liver edge 1 cm below the right costal margin on deep inspiration, nontender; the spleen tip was not appreciable. There was no pedal edema, though mild palmar erythema and several spider angiomas over the upper chest were present. Finger-stick glucose measured 102 mg/dL. Within ten minutes of arrival, venous blood was drawn—still prior to any pharmacologic therapy, vitamin supplementation, or volume resuscitation—and the initial panel revealed: total bilirubin 8.7 mg/dL, serum albumin 2.28 g/dL, alanine aminotransferase 62 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 88 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 198 U/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase 232 U/L, sodium 135 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, chloride 101 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 19 mg/dL, creatinine 0.96 mg/dL (estimated GFR 82 mL/min/1.73 m²), glucose 104 mg/dL, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, white blood cell count 7.2 ×10⁹/L, platelet count 92 ×10⁹/L, and a prothrombin time corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 3.6. Urinalysis from a straight catheter specimen showed moderate bilirubin, 1+ urobilinogen, and no leukocyte esterase or nitrite. A focused bedside ultrasound performed before any sedatives or diuretics were given demonstrated a shrunken heterogeneous liver with a rugose capsule, patent portal vein with hepatopetal flow at 15 cm/s, a spleen measuring 17 cm in length, and a barely perceptible anechoic stripe of fluid no more than 1.5 cm in depth tracking along the right paracolic gutter; kidneys appeared normal and there was no biliary dilation. Upright chest radiography obtained prior to blood draw displayed elevated hemidiaphragms, clear lung fields, and no pleural effusion, while a 12-lead electrocardiogram recorded sinus tachycardia at 98 beats/min with a QTc of 440 ms and no acute ischemic changes. Up to this juncture the patient has not received lactulose, rifaximin, mannitol, diuretics, intravenous albumin, antibiotics, vitamin K, or paracentesis, ensuring that all observations and laboratory measurements documented above reflect her untreated physiologic state at presentation.
CRITICAL HINT: For Child-Pugh, ascites_grade is 1=Absent, 2=Slight/Mild, 3=Moderate/Severe. encephalopathy_grade is 1=Absent, 2=Grade 1-2, 3=Grade 3-4.

output.reasoning: Patient is a 57-year-old female. Child-Pugh components evaluated: 1) Total Bilirubin = 8.7 mg/dL (>3 mg/dL = 3 points). 2) Serum Albumin = 2.28 g/dL (<2.8 g/dL = 3 points). 3) INR = 3.6 (>2.3 = 3 points). 4) Ascites: Exam noted "faint fluid impulse" and ultrasound showed "barely perceptible anechoic stripe of fluid no more than 1.5 cm in depth", implying mild/slight ascites (2 points). 5) Encephalopathy: Patient is "deeply lethargic", "responded only to her name spoken loudly", "moaned rather than forming words", "could not state the date", suggestive of Grade 3-4 (3 points).

args:
A 36-year-old female smoker presented to the emergency department with epigastric abdominal pain, radiating to her back, of 1 week duration. Her past medical history was significant for type 2 diabetes for which she was on oral hypoglycemic agents and insulin; presumed secondary HTG for which she was on atorvastatin and gemfibrozil; furthermore, she used medroxyprogesterone acetate for contraception. She had stopped all these medications except the contraceptive 12 months prior to her presentation. The patient has no history of alcohol abuse, thyroid dysfunction, or family history of HTG. On admission, all vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination was normal except for epigastric tenderness and a BMI of 31.4 kg/m2.
The initial relevant laboratory testing revealed sodium of 129 meq/l, potassium 3.7 meq/l, chloride 97 meq/l, BUN 10 mg/dl, creatinine 0.77 mg/dl (at baseline), bicarbonate 13.7 meq/l, anion gap 18 meq/l, glucose of 324 mg/dl, amylase 31 IU/l, lipase 206 IU/l, and TG 2,329 mg/dl with a previous baseline of around 2,000 mg/dl, serum calcium 9.0 mg/dl, TSH 1.182 µIU/ml, serum lactate 1.8 meq/l, negative urine ketones with serum beta-hydroxybutyrate 0.16 mmol/l, and negative urine pregnancy test. The abdominal computerized tomography scan showed no definite evidence of pancreatitis or any other pathology.
Despite unremarkable imaging, normal amylase, mild elevation of lipase, and a TG level that was mildly elevated from previous baseline, she was treated for possible HTGP with IV insulin based on the overall clinical picture. The differential diagnosis includes diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA); however, the patient lacked serum or urinary ketones. Regardless, IV insulin would treat both conditions of HTGP and DKA. The clinical impression of HTGP was further supported when her lipase increased to 370 IU/L and the TG levels on the initial sample was re-assayed resulting in a corrected reading of >10,000 mg/dl. The TG levels decreased to 6,069 mg/dl with IV insulin on day 5. However, given the worsening abdominal pain, plan for plasmapheresis to rapidly reduce the TG levels was taken. The TG levels decreased to 2,055 mg/dl after the first exchange and to 642 mg/dl after the second session, and her symptoms improved significantly.

args:
An 88-year-old male presented to our emergency department (ED) with an acute onset of a painless loss of vision to his right eye. His past medical history was significant for hypertension, coronary artery disease and atrial fibrillation (on apixaban). He stated he was lying in bed reading when he had a sudden painless loss of vision to his right eye, described as a curtain closing, for which he presented to our ED ~24 h later. He denied any other symptoms including headache, scalp pain, jaw claudication, focal weakness, numbness, angina or shortness of breath.
Vitals on arrival to the ED were as follows: 36.9°C, heart rate of 62 beats/min, respiratory rate of 16 breaths/min, blood pressure of 123/53 mmHg and pulse oximetry of 99% on room air. His cardiopulmonary exam was unremarkable. His neurologic exam was notable for a National Institute of Health Stroke Scale score of +2 for complete unilateral vision loss to the right eye. Intraocular pressures were 14 mmHg bilaterally. His remaining physical exam was notable for a right afferent pupillary defect (APD). To further assess the eye and its structures, a point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) was then performed. Utilizing the high-frequency linear probe to the right eye, a transverse view demonstrated a retrobulbar spot sign (RBSS) in the distal portion of the central retinal artery within the optic nerve sheath (, red arrow). He had normal blood flow in his left eye (), and no blood flow was seen to the central retinal artery of the right eye, with the yellow arrow pointing to the RBSS (). The patient was then transferred to our tertiary referral center (TRC) for specialist consultation.
Ophthalmology visualized a plaque on the dilated fundoscopic exam with associated retinal ischemia. Given the onset of ~24 h prior to arrival, the risks of tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) outweighed the benefits, as such, the patient was medically managed. His head computed tomography (CT) was unremarkable for acute changes. A magnetic resonance imaging MRI could not be done, secondary to his pacemaker not being compatible. His carotid duplex was without severe stenosis or thrombus. His echocardiography showed left ventricular diastolic dysfunction with an ejection fraction of 40%, which was similar to previous studies.
Overall, his workup was without evidence of carotid artery disease or vasculitis and it was suspected to be cardioembolic in origin related to his atrial fibrillation. On further questioning, it was found that the patient’s family member had passed away 2 weeks prior, leading to depression and multiple missed doses of his anticoagulation. Compliance of his anticoagulation was stressed on discharge. At his 1-month follow-up after his hospital admission, he has regained light perception and 15% of his vision.

output.reasoning: The patient is an 88-year-old male (Age ≥75 = 2 points). History of hypertension (1 point). History of coronary artery disease (not counted in CHA2DS2-VASc). History of atrial fibrillation (2 points, age is already ≥75 so this is for the AF, not the age component). No history of heart failure (ejection fraction of 40% is noted, but he does not have a formal diagnosis of heart failure listed in history). No history of stroke, TIA, or thromboembolism (the current event is the first incidence). No vascular disease (carotid duplex was negative for stenosis).

args:
An 88-year-old male presented to our emergency department (ED) with an acute onset of a painless loss of vision to his right eye. His past medical history was significant for hypertension, coronary artery disease and atrial fibrillation (on apixaban). He stated he was lying in bed reading when he had a sudden painless loss of vision to his right eye, described as a curtain closing, for which he presented to our ED ~24 h later. He denied any other symptoms including headache, scalp pain, jaw claudication, focal weakness, numbness, angina or shortness of breath.
Vitals on arrival to the ED were as follows: 36.9°C, heart rate of 62 beats/min, respiratory rate of 16 breaths/min, blood pressure of 123/53 mmHg and pulse oximetry of 99% on room air. His cardiopulmonary exam was unremarkable. His neurologic exam was notable for a National Institute of Health Stroke Scale score of +2 for complete unilateral vision loss to the right eye. Intraocular pressures were 14 mmHg bilaterally. His remaining physical exam was notable for a right afferent pupillary defect (APD). To further assess the eye and its structures, a point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) was then performed. Utilizing the high-frequency linear probe to the right eye, a transverse view demonstrated a retrobulbar spot sign (RBSS) in the distal portion of the central retinal artery within the optic nerve sheath (, red arrow). He had normal blood flow in his left eye (), and no blood flow was seen to the central retinal artery of the right eye, with the yellow arrow pointing to the RBSS (). The patient was then transferred to our tertiary referral center (TRC) for specialist consultation.
Ophthalmology visualized a plaque on the dilated fundoscopic exam with associated retinal ischemia. Given the onset of ~24 h prior to arrival, the risks of tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) outweighed the benefits, as such, the patient was medically managed. His head computed tomography (CT) was unremarkable for acute changes. A magnetic resonance imaging MRI could not be done, secondary to his pacemaker not being compatible. His carotid duplex was without severe stenosis or thrombus. His echocardiography showed left ventricular diastolic dysfunction with an ejection fraction of 40%, which was similar to previous studies.
Overall, his workup was without evidence of carotid artery disease or vasculitis and it was suspected to be cardioembolic in origin related to his atrial fibrillation. On further questioning, it was found that the patient’s family member had passed away 2 weeks prior, leading to depression and multiple missed doses of his anticoagulation. Compliance of his anticoagulation was stressed on discharge. At his 1-month follow-up after his hospital admission, he has regained light perception and 15% of his vision.

- Demographics: Age=88, Sex=male

args:
A 67-year-old male patient who had been on CAPD therapy for end-stage renal disease secondary to type 2 diabetes mellitus. He had been on CAPD therapy for 3 years. He experienced two episodes of CAPD peritonitis secondary to Enterococcus spp and metycilline-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus in December 2007 and June 2008, respectively. All episodes were successfully treated with standard antibiotic therapy.
The patient presented to our hospital due to abdominal pain, tenderness, cloudy effluent and pruritus. His body temperature was 37.8°C. The abdominal examination showed diffuse abdominal tenderness and signs of peritonitis. The remaining physical examination was normal. On admission, haemogram showed white blood cell count 10.700/mm3 with 76% neutrophils and 6% eosinophils and haemoglobin 11.6 g/dL. Blood urea nitrogen was 72 mg/dL, and serum creatinine was 6.6 mg/dL. C-reactive protein was high (23.3 mg/dL). Peritoneal effluent leukocyte count was 1550/mm3 with 80% neutrophils and 10% eosinophils. S. stercoralis larvae were seen on centrifuged dialysate sediment with microscopic examination (Figure ). Stool examination was also positive for S. stercoralis larvae. The patient was treated with albendazole 400 mg orally for 1 month. The dialysate and stool cultures were negative. The patients' complaints disappeared and dialysate white blood cell count decreased to 100/mm3 4 days later without a need for catheter removal. We did not detect larvae in three stool samples and treatment finished at the end of the first month. Strongyloides larva was also not detected in stool samples 2 months after the therapy.

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 65)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
creatinine_mg_dl: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.2)

from typing import Any
def simulate_medical_value(patient_note: str, question: str) -> Any:
    """Calculate a medical value from a patient note.

    Calculate a medical value from a patient note.
    
    Given a patient note and a calculation question, reason step by step:
    1. Carefully read the patient note to extract all relevant clinical values
    2. Identify what medical calculation/score is needed
    3. Apply the appropriate formula from the reference below
    4. Show your calculation step by step, checking your arithmetic
    
    FORMULAS (use these exact formulas):
    - APACHE II Score: Acute Physiology Score + Age Points + Chronic Health Points
    - Adjusted Body Weight: ABW = IBW + 0.4 * (actual_weight - IBW)
    - Albumin Corrected Anion Gap: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    - Anion Gap: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    - Body Mass Index (BMI): BMI = weight_kg / (height_m)^2
    - Body Surface Area (Mosteller): BSA = sqrt((height_cm * weight_kg) / 3600)
    - CHA2DS2-VASc Score: Sum of: CHF(+1), HTN(+1), Age>=75(+2)/65-74(+1), DM(+1), Stroke/TIA(+2), Vascular(+1), Female(+1)
    - CKD-EPI GFR (2021): 142 * min(Cr/kappa,1)^alpha * max(Cr/kappa,1)^-1.200 * 0.9938^age * sex_coef
    - CURB-65 Score: C + U + R + B + 65 (0-5 points)
    - Calcium Correction for Hypoalbuminemia: Corrected Ca = Ca + 0.8 * (4 - albumin)
    - Caprini VTE Score: Sum of weighted risk factors
    - Centor Score (McIsaac): Sum with age modifier
    - Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI): Sum of weighted comorbidity points + age points
    - Child-Pugh Score: Sum of points for bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
    - Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault): CrCl = ((140 - age) * adjusted_weight * gender_coef) / (Cr * 72)
    - Date of Conception: gestational_weeks - 2 (conception ~2 weeks after LMP)
    - Delta Gap: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (where 12 is normal AG)
    - Delta Ratio: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    - Estimated Due Date: 40 - current_gestational_age
    - FIB-4 Index: FIB-4 = (Age * AST) / (Platelets * sqrt(ALT))
    - FeverPAIN Score: Fever + Purulence + Attend rapidly + Inflamed tonsils + No cough/coryza (0-5)
    - Fractional Excretion of Sodium (FENa): FENa = (UNa * SCr) / (SNa * UCr) * 100
    - Framingham Risk Score: Complex multivariate equation based on age, sex, lipids, BP, smoking
    - Free Water Deficit: FWD = TBW% * weight * (Na/140 - 1)
    - Gestational Age: weeks + days/7
    - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): Eye + Verbal + Motor (3-15)
    - Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS): Sum of clinical and lab criteria (0-23)
    - HAS-BLED Score: H-A-S-B-L-E-D criteria (0-9 points)
    - HEART Score: Sum of History + ECG + Age + Risk factors + Troponin (each 0-2 points)
    - HOMA-IR: HOMA-IR = (Glucose * Insulin) / 405
    - Ideal Body Weight (Devine): Male: IBW = 50 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60), Female: IBW = 45.5 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60)
    - LDL Calculated (Friedewald): LDL = Total Cholesterol - HDL - (Triglycerides / 5)
    - MDRD GFR: 175 * (Cr)^-1.154 * (age)^-0.203 * sex_coef
    - MELD-Na Score: MELD = 10 * (0.957*ln(Cr) + 0.378*ln(Bili) + 1.120*ln(INR) + 0.643)
    - Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1 Rule): First 10kg: 4ml/kg/hr, Next 10kg: 2ml/kg/hr, Remaining: 1ml/kg/hr
    - Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): MAP = (2 * DBP + SBP) / 3
    - Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME): Sum of (opioid dose * conversion factor)
    - PERC Rule: 8 criteria; PERC negative if all 0
    - PSI/PORT Score: Demographics + Comorbidities + Physical Exam + Labs
    - QTc (Bazett): QTc = QT / sqrt(RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Framingham): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Fridericia): QTc = QT / RR^(1/3) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Hodges): QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)
    - QTc (Rautaharju): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI): Sum of 6 risk factors (0-6 points)
    - SIRS Criteria: Count of criteria met (>= 2 = SIRS positive)
    - SOFA Score: Sum of 6 organ system scores (0-24)
    - Serum Osmolality: Osm = 2*Na + BUN/2.8 + Glucose/18
    - Sodium Correction for Hyperglycemia: Corrected Na = Na + 0.024 * (glucose - 100)
    - Steroid Conversion: Prednisone equivalent = dose * conversion_factor
    - Target Weight: Target Weight = target_bmi * (height_m)^2
    - Wells' Criteria for DVT: Sum of clinical criteria points
    - Wells' Criteria for PE: Sum of weighted criteria
    
    Important:
    - Be precise with extracted values. Double-check your arithmetic.
    - For sex/gender: "man"/"male"/"he" → male, "woman"/"female"/"she" → female.
    - Convert units as needed (lbs→kg: ×0.453592, feet/inches→cm: (ft×12+in)×2.54).
    - For scoring systems: carefully check each criterion against the patient note.
      Look for conditions implied by medications or clinical findings, not just
      explicitly named conditions.
    
    CRITICAL FORMATTING INSTRUCTIONS FOR THE FINAL ANSWER:
    - If the answer is a numeric value, return ONLY the raw number (e.g., 6.25). Do NOT include units, commas, or symbols like "%" or "mmHg".
    - If the answer is a date, return ONLY the exact date string in MM/DD/YYYY format (e.g., 06/09/2002). Do NOT wrap it in extra quotes.
    - If the answer is a tuple of weeks and days, return the exact Python tuple of strings (e.g., ('33 weeks', '5 days')).
    
    Examples:
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 70-year-old male weighing 80 kg, height 175 cm.", "What is the patient's BMI?")
    26.122
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 65-year-old female, BP 130/85 mmHg.", "What is the patient's MAP?")
    100.0
    """
    ...

args:
A 56-year-old man is reviewed in the short-stay pre-operative area an hour before transfer to the minimally invasive suite, where the surgical team plans a four-port laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy expected to last about ninety minutes under general anesthesia with overnight observation on the bariatric ward. He ambulated into the unit unassisted after parking his own car, reports that he still completes a two-mile neighborhood walk every evening with his spouse, and denies any recent limitation in daily activities other than occasional exertional dyspnea attributed to long-standing chronic obstructive pulmonary disease for which he uses once-daily tiotropium and intermittent albuterol; he has not required oral corticosteroids or hospital admission for a pulmonary exacerbation in more than two years, though he continues to carry a 35-pack-year smoking history and quit only three months ago. He has not developed pneumonia or other lower respiratory tract infection within the past month, and there has been no episode of sepsis or bacteremia requiring antimicrobials. The patient has never been treated for congestive heart failure, coronary ischemia, cerebrovascular events, spinal cord injury, or systemic infection, and he has avoided inpatient stays or operative procedures during the past twelve months apart from routine colonoscopy. Musculoskeletal history is free of casts, fractures of the pelvis or long bones, or multi-system trauma, and he currently sleeps in his own bed rather than a recliner without orthopnea. He recalls that his father developed a blood clot in the calf following hip arthroplasty in middle age and that a paternal aunt required long-term warfarin after an unprovoked pulmonary embolus; because of that background he underwent a thrombophilia work-up last year that demonstrated normal factor V Leiden genotype, normal prothrombin gene analysis, negative lupus anticoagulant, and a mild elevation of fasting plasma homocysteine at 18 μmol L⁻¹, for which lifestyle and vitamin supplementation were recommended. He himself has never experienced unilateral leg swelling, chest pain with breathlessness, or radiologist-confirmed venous thromboembolism, and duplex examinations performed twice in the past decade for vague calf aches were unremarkable. Examination today reveals serpentine, thin-walled superficial veins that become more prominent when his feet are placed dependent, coursing along the medial aspects of both lower legs without tenderness, edema, or skin discoloration; calf circumferences measured 10 cm below the tibial tuberosities are 36 cm bilaterally and equal. No central lines, ports, or tunneled catheters are in situ; the only vascular access is a single 18-gauge peripheral cannula in the right cephalic vein through which lactated Ringer’s is running at keep-open rate. He has never developed thrombocytopenia or antibody-mediated complications during prior brief exposures to unfractionated or low-molecular-weight heparin in the peri-procedural setting. He also denies any history of inflammatory bowel disease such as Crohn’s disease or ulcerative colitis. 

Vital signs ten minutes before review: temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 132/78 mm Hg, heart rate 84 beats min⁻¹ in regular rhythm, respiratory rate 16 min⁻¹ with a mild occasional expiratory wheeze, and oxygen saturation 95 % on room air. Airway evaluation discloses Mallampati class II anatomy, full dentition, three-finger thyromental distance, and unrestricted cervical extension. Cardiovascular examination notes a nondisplaced apical impulse and crisp S₁-S₂ without murmurs, gallops, or rubs; jugular venous contour is not elevated. Breath sounds are vesicular with scattered end-expiratory wheeze over both upper lobes but no rales or rhonchi. The abdomen is protuberant yet soft and nontender, without palpable hepatosplenomegaly or hernia defects other than the planned operative site markings. Distal pulses are 2+ in dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial arteries, capillary refill is brisk, and there is no pitting edema. Neurologic screen shows the patient to be alert, oriented, with 5/5 strength throughout, intact sensation to light touch, and steady tandem gait when asked to walk in the corridor. Morning laboratory tests obtained from the peripheral line reveal hemoglobin 14.0 g dL⁻¹, hematocrit 42 %, platelets 248 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, leukocytes 7.2 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, sodium 139 mmol L⁻¹, potassium 4.2 mmol L⁻¹, bicarbonate 25 mmol L⁻¹, creatinine 0.96 mg dL⁻¹, AST 22 U L⁻¹, ALT 24 U L⁻¹, total bilirubin 0.6 mg dL⁻¹, prothrombin time 12.1 s with an international normalized ratio of 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 30 s; fasting lipid profile and HbA1c earlier this month were within target ranges after recent weight loss efforts. A 12-lead electrocardiogram printed on arrival shows normal sinus rhythm at 80 beats min⁻¹, QRS duration 90 ms, no pathologic Q waves, and flat ST segments without dynamic changes compared with a tracing from his pulmonary clinic six months ago. Posterior-anterior chest radiography obtained yesterday displays mild upper-lobe hyperlucency consistent with emphysematous change but otherwise clear lung fields and an unremarkable cardiac silhouette. Body mass index measured at registration is 34 kg m⁻². He has been nil per os since midnight, removed dentures and jewelry, and last used his bronchodilator four hours ago. Sequential calf compression sleeves are wrapped but remain inactive until induction, and pharmacologic antithrombotic prophylaxis has been deferred until after the surgical pause. The anesthesia team has discussed risks of venous stasis, pulmonary complications related to pneumoperitoneum, and the importance of early ambulation and incentive spirometry post-operatively; the patient verbalizes understanding and consent, requests no further clarification, and awaits transfer to the operating theater.

CRITICAL HINT: For Caprini, Age 41-60=1 pt, 61-74=2 pts, >=75=3 pts. Elective lower extremity arthroplasty = 5 pts. Hip, pelvis or leg fracture = 5 pts. Stroke/paralysis = 5 pts. Multiple trauma = 5 pts. Acute spinal cord injury = 5 pts. Major surgery (>45 min) = 2 pts. Central venous access = 2 pts. Bed rest > 72 hours = 2 pts. Minor surgery = 1 pt. History of major surgery (<1 month) = 1 pt. COPD = 1 pt. BMI > 25 = 1 pt.

output.reasoning: The patient is 56 years old (Age 41-60 = 1pt). He is undergoing a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy, which is a major surgery (>45 min = 2pts). He has a BMI of 34 kg/m² (>25 = 1pt). He has a diagnosis of COPD (1pt). He has no history of stroke, spinal cord injury, or major surgery within the last month. He has no central venous access. There is no bed rest > 72 hours noted.

    You are given:
    - A patient note with clinical information

    - A description of each field to extract (with units and expected ranges)
    - Optional reasoning context from a prior analysis stage

    Instructions:
    - Use the EXACT parameter names from the field descriptions as JSON keys
    - CRITICAL: Always extract the patient's baseline or admission values (prior to any treatment or surgery) unless the question specifically asks for post-treatment values. Pay close attention to the timeline in the note.
    - CRITICAL: If a field name ends in '_score' or '_points' (e.g., 'bun_score', 'age_score'), you MUST calculate and provide the integer point value based on the scoring system, NOT the raw clinical measurement (e.g., if bun_score is requested, give the points for BUN, not the raw BUN in mg/dL).
    - For all other fields, extract the RAW numeric value (e.g., if 'platelets' is requested, provide 115, not the point value).

    - For boolean conditions: True if present, False if absent

    - Include ALL fields, even if the value is False/0/absent
    - If a required numeric value or field is entirely unmentioned and cannot be inferred, OMIT it from the 'extracted' dictionary completely instead of writing 'missing' or null.

args: A 24-year-old man with no known chronic medical problems, no history of prior hospitalizations, and no regular medications was air-lifted to the emergency department after being pulled from an icy mountain lake during a winter wilderness‐training exercise earlier the same afternoon; witnesses estimated that he was submerged for less than a minute but aspirated a significant amount of frigid water and then endured a two-hour sled evacuation through sub-zero ambient temperatures. Throughout transport he remained awake, conversant, and oriented, and the flight crew supplied low-flow supplemental oxygen through a Venturi mask that the respiratory therapist later confirmed to be delivering 30 % inspired oxygen; no intravenous fluids, vasopressors, sedatives, antibiotics, steroids, or analgesics were given in the field or en route. Upon stretcher arrival to the critical care bay—and before any hospital interventions—the initial set of vitals documented a core temperature of 35.8 °C measured rectally, a heart rate of 78 beats per minute in regular sinus rhythm, a respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute with quiet chest excursions, and a non-invasive blood pressure of 118/72 mm Hg confirmed manually on the contralateral arm. The patient opened his eyes spontaneously, answered questions coherently, and obeyed commands without hesitation, yielding a full Glasgow Coma Score of 15. His skin was pale and cool to the touch but well perfused, with brisk capillary refill and no cyanosis or mottling noted; fingertips exhibited faint maceration consistent with prolonged damp exposure. Neck veins were flat at 30 degrees. Auscultation revealed a crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; distal pulses were strong and symmetric. Lung examination demonstrated fine inspiratory crackles scattered over both bases with slightly decreased breath sounds posteriorly, but no wheezes or stridor. The abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender; bowel sounds were present. There was no costovertebral angle tenderness, no peripheral edema, and no focal neurologic deficit. A radial arterial blood sample obtained seven minutes after bay entry while he remained on the same 30 % oxygen source showed a pH of 7.41, PaCO₂ 38 mm Hg, and PaO₂ 59 mm Hg; end-tidal CO₂ on capnography was 36 mm Hg, corroborating adequate ventilation. Simultaneously drawn serum chemistry returned sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, glucose 96 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 12 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.7 mg/dL, indicating preserved renal function and normal electrolyte balance. Complete blood count demonstrated a white cell count of 9 000 cells/mm³ with a normal differential, hematocrit 43 %, hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL, and platelets 245 000/µL. Venous lactate measured at the bedside was 1.6 mmol/L. Coagulation profile showed an international normalized ratio of 1.0 and activated partial thromboplastin time of 31 seconds. Portable anteroposterior chest radiograph performed before any diuretics or antibiotics displayed faint bilateral perihilar opacities and increased interstitial markings without overt consolidation or pleural effusion, findings interpreted as early noncardiogenic pulmonary edema versus aspiration pneumonitis. Focused cardiac ultrasound revealed a small, vigorously contracting left ventricle and an inferior vena cava that collapsed more than 50 % with inspiration, suggesting euvolemia; no pericardial effusion was visualized. Lung ultrasound demonstrated scattered B-lines in both posterior lower zones, supporting the radiographic impression of mild interstitial involvement. Bedside abdominal scan showed normal solid organ echogenicity and no free fluid. Electrocardiography printed during the arterial puncture displayed normal sinus rhythm at 76 beats per minute, normal intervals, and no ST-segment deviations or Osborn waves. A Foley catheter placed for temperature monitoring produced 60 mL of clear yellow urine in the first fifteen minutes. No bronchodilators, antimicrobials, analgesics, warming blankets, or intravenous fluids had been started at the moment the intensive care team accepted him for close observation, passive rewarming, and serial reassessment of gas exchange; therefore the recorded values—temperature 35.8 °C, systolic pressure 118 mm Hg, diastolic pressure 72 mm Hg, pulse 78 beats per minute, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, arterial pH 7.41, serum sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 0.7 mg/dL, hematocrit 43 %, white blood cell count 9 000 /mm³, fraction of inspired oxygen 30 %, and PaO₂ 59 mm Hg—accurately depict his untreated physiologic state immediately upon presentation after a brief near-drowning event complicated by environmental hypothermia and mild aspiration-related hypoxemia.

CRITICAL HINT: For APACHE II oxygenation, use PaO2 to score if FiO2 < 0.50. Only use A-a gradient if FiO2 >= 0.50. Do not hallucinate A-a gradient if PaCO2 is missing. GCS score points = 15 minus actual GCS.

args: A 24-year-old man with no known chronic medical problems, no history of prior hospitalizations, and no regular medications was air-lifted to the emergency department after being pulled from an icy mountain lake during a winter wilderness‐training exercise earlier the same afternoon; witnesses estimated that he was submerged for less than a minute but aspirated a significant amount of frigid water and then endured a two-hour sled evacuation through sub-zero ambient temperatures. Throughout transport he remained awake, conversant, and oriented, and the flight crew supplied low-flow supplemental oxygen through a Venturi mask that the respiratory therapist later confirmed to be delivering 30 % inspired oxygen; no intravenous fluids, vasopressors, sedatives, antibiotics, steroids, or analgesics were given in the field or en route. Upon stretcher arrival to the critical care bay—and before any hospital interventions—the initial set of vitals documented a core temperature of 35.8 °C measured rectally, a heart rate of 78 beats per minute in regular sinus rhythm, a respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute with quiet chest excursions, and a non-invasive blood pressure of 118/72 mm Hg confirmed manually on the contralateral arm. The patient opened his eyes spontaneously, answered questions coherently, and obeyed commands without hesitation, yielding a full Glasgow Coma Score of 15. His skin was pale and cool to the touch but well perfused, with brisk capillary refill and no cyanosis or mottling noted; fingertips exhibited faint maceration consistent with prolonged damp exposure. Neck veins were flat at 30 degrees. Auscultation revealed a crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; distal pulses were strong and symmetric. Lung examination demonstrated fine inspiratory crackles scattered over both bases with slightly decreased breath sounds posteriorly, but no wheezes or stridor. The abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender; bowel sounds were present. There was no costovertebral angle tenderness, no peripheral edema, and no focal neurologic deficit. A radial arterial blood sample obtained seven minutes after bay entry while he remained on the same 30 % oxygen source showed a pH of 7.41, PaCO₂ 38 mm Hg, and PaO₂ 59 mm Hg; end-tidal CO₂ on capnography was 36 mm Hg, corroborating adequate ventilation. Simultaneously drawn serum chemistry returned sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, glucose 96 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 12 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.7 mg/dL, indicating preserved renal function and normal electrolyte balance. Complete blood count demonstrated a white cell count of 9 000 cells/mm³ with a normal differential, hematocrit 43 %, hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL, and platelets 245 000/µL. Venous lactate measured at the bedside was 1.6 mmol/L. Coagulation profile showed an international normalized ratio of 1.0 and activated partial thromboplastin time of 31 seconds. Portable anteroposterior chest radiograph performed before any diuretics or antibiotics displayed faint bilateral perihilar opacities and increased interstitial markings without overt consolidation or pleural effusion, findings interpreted as early noncardiogenic pulmonary edema versus aspiration pneumonitis. Focused cardiac ultrasound revealed a small, vigorously contracting left ventricle and an inferior vena cava that collapsed more than 50 % with inspiration, suggesting euvolemia; no pericardial effusion was visualized. Lung ultrasound demonstrated scattered B-lines in both posterior lower zones, supporting the radiographic impression of mild interstitial involvement. Bedside abdominal scan showed normal solid organ echogenicity and no free fluid. Electrocardiography printed during the arterial puncture displayed normal sinus rhythm at 76 beats per minute, normal intervals, and no ST-segment deviations or Osborn waves. A Foley catheter placed for temperature monitoring produced 60 mL of clear yellow urine in the first fifteen minutes. No bronchodilators, antimicrobials, analgesics, warming blankets, or intravenous fluids had been started at the moment the intensive care team accepted him for close observation, passive rewarming, and serial reassessment of gas exchange; therefore the recorded values—temperature 35.8 °C, systolic pressure 118 mm Hg, diastolic pressure 72 mm Hg, pulse 78 beats per minute, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, arterial pH 7.41, serum sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 0.7 mg/dL, hematocrit 43 %, white blood cell count 9 000 /mm³, fraction of inspired oxygen 30 %, and PaO₂ 59 mm Hg—accurately depict his untreated physiologic state immediately upon presentation after a brief near-drowning event complicated by environmental hypothermia and mild aspiration-related hypoxemia.

temperature_score: Temperature points (0-4)
map_score: Mean arterial pressure points (0-4)
heart_rate_score: Heart rate points (0-4)
respiratory_rate_score: Respiratory rate points (0-4)
oxygenation_score: A-aDO2 or PaO2 points (0-4)
ph_score: Arterial pH points (0-4)
sodium_score: Serum sodium points (0-4)
potassium_score: Serum potassium points (0-4)
creatinine_score: Serum creatinine points (0-4, doubled for ARF)
hematocrit_score: Hematocrit points (0-4)
wbc_score: WBC points (0-4)
gcs_score: GCS points (15 - GCS, so 0-12)
age_score: Age points
chronic_health_score: Chronic health points

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: age_24, temperature_35.8_c, map_87_mmhg, heart_rate_78, respiratory_rate_18, pao2_59, fio2_0.30, ph_7.41, sodium_138, potassium_4.1, creatinine_0.7, hematocrit_43, wbc_9000, gcs_15
- Conditions ABSENT: chronic_illness
- Demographics: Age=24, Sex=male

CALCULATOR SPECIFIC HINTS:
CRITICAL HINT: For APACHE II oxygenation, use PaO2 to score if FiO2 < 0.50. Only use A-a gradient if FiO2 >= 0.50. Do not hallucinate A-a gradient if PaCO2 is missing. GCS score points = 15 minus actual GCS.

args: A 41-year-old woman who ordinarily works as a middle-school physical-education instructor and runs half-marathons on weekends was transferred directly from the emergency department to the medical intensive care unit less than an hour after collapsing in her classroom with pleuritic chest pain, a dry cough that had worsened over three days, and a single episode of hemoptysis described by colleagues as no more than a teaspoon of bright blood. She has no prior history of chronic pulmonary, cardiovascular, hepatic, or renal disease, takes only an oral combined contraceptive for dysmenorrhea, and has never required hospitalization, systemic corticosteroids, chemotherapy, or other immune-modifying agents. She denies tobacco use, drinks wine socially once or twice per month, and has never used recreational substances. Family medical history is notable only for a father with hypertension in his late fifties; there is no diabetes, cystic fibrosis, or clotting disorder in first-degree relatives. According to emergency medical services, on arrival to the school she was seated on the gym floor, speaking in full sentences but reporting “sharp stitches” beneath the right scapula with every deep inspiration. Initial portable pulse oximetry on room air registered 88 % and improved to the low 90s after placement of a Venturi mask that respiratory therapy later confirmed was set at 38 % inspired oxygen; no intravenous fluids, analgesics, antibiotics, or bronchodilators were administered en route, and transport time was under ten minutes. Upon arrival to the resuscitation bay—and before any further interventions—the first documented vital signs showed an oral temperature of 38.8 °C, regular pulse of 99 beats per minute, respiratory rate of 22 breaths per minute with mild intercostal retractions, and non-invasive blood pressure of 116/71 mm Hg corroborated manually on a second reading. She was seated upright, speaking clearly, oriented to person, place, and date, able to recall three objects after five minutes, and followed multistep commands without hesitation, yielding a Glasgow Coma Score of 15 with no sedatives or intoxicants on board. Skin was warm and slightly diaphoretic with brisk capillary refill; there was no peripheral cyanosis, edema, or rash. Conjunctivae were injected, sclerae anicteric, and pupils equal and reactive. Jugular venous pressure was not elevated. Cardiac auscultation revealed a normal S1 and single physiologic S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and distal pulses were 2+ and symmetric. Breath sounds were diminished at the right posterior base with scattered fine crackles but no wheezes or bronchial breath sounds; percussion over that area was dull compared with the contralateral field. The remainder of the lung exam was clear, and tactile fremitus was increased over the involved segment. Abdomen was soft, nondistended, and mildly tender in the right upper quadrant on deep palpation, likely secondary to splinting; bowel sounds were present and normoactive. There was no costovertebral angle tenderness and no flank ecchymosis. Neurologic examination showed symmetrical strength and intact sensation in all extremities; coordination and gait could not be fully assessed given her monitored status. An arterial blood gas drawn within seven minutes of ICU bed placement while she remained on 38 % oxygen revealed pH 7.34, PaCO₂ 38 mm Hg, and PaO₂ 65 mm Hg, indicating moderate hypoxemia responsive to supplemental oxygen. Simultaneous serum chemistry panel demonstrated sodium 142 mmol/L, potassium 3.9 mmol/L, chloride 104 mmol/L, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, glucose 102 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.46 mg/dL, the latter marginally above her last wellness-screening value of 0.9 mg/dL sixteen months earlier but still within laboratory reference limits and without accompanying oliguria, as Foley catheterization in the bay yielded 120 mL of clear yellow urine collected since her collapse. Complete blood count returned a white cell count of 12 600 /mm³ with 82 % neutrophils, hematocrit 38 %, hemoglobin 12.7 g/dL, and platelets 245 000 /µL. Point-of-care lactate measured 1.6 mmol/L. Electrocardiography conducted concurrently showed normal sinus rhythm at 98 beats per minute with no ST-segment deviation or T-wave inversion. A portable anteroposterior chest radiograph obtained before initiation of antimicrobials or diuretics revealed a dense, wedge-shaped opacity in the lateral segment of the right lower lobe without air bronchograms, a normal cardiac silhouette, and no pleural effusion. Bedside bilateral lower-limb compression ultrasonography performed by emergency staff immediately afterward demonstrated a non-compressible popliteal vein on the right, raising concern for acute venous thromboembolism with pulmonary sequelae versus superimposed pneumonia. Focused cardiac ultrasound in the ICU showed normal biventricular size and function with no regional wall-motion abnormality and a collapsible inferior vena cava measuring 1.5 cm in diameter. No empiric anticoagulation, antibiotics, vasopressors, steroids, or intravenous fluids had yet been administered at the point these physiologic and laboratory data were finalized, ensuring that the recorded values—temperature 38.8 °C, heart rate 99 beats per minute, respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute, blood pressure 116/71 mm Hg, arterial pH 7.34, serum sodium 142 mmol/L, potassium 3.9 mmol/L, creatinine 1.46 mg/dL, hematocrit 38 %, white blood cell count 12 600 /mm³, fraction of inspired oxygen 38 %, and PaO₂ 65 mm Hg—accurately reflect her untreated presentation at the moment of ICU admission for evaluation and management of suspected pulmonary infarction with secondary inflammatory response.

CRITICAL HINT: For APACHE II oxygenation, use PaO2 to score if FiO2 < 0.50. Only use A-a gradient if FiO2 >= 0.50. Do not hallucinate A-a gradient if PaCO2 is missing. GCS score points = 15 minus actual GCS.

    - For historical scores (e.g., Charlson Comorbidity Index), a past history of a condition (e.g., past ulcer, past TIA, past malignancy) often counts as PRESENT unless the calculator strictly requires active disease. Erroneously marking past conditions as absent is a common mistake.
    CRITICAL INSTRUCTION: Score the conditions EXACTLY as they appear in the text, REGARDLESS of the primary diagnosis or whether the scoring system seems clinically appropriate for the patient's presentation. Do not overthink clinical applicability.

    """
